persona: He is a 31-year-old man in Downey, California, a former athlete who now works as a football analyst and is doing well financially overall, though he sometimes hesitates on pricier prescriptions and tries to stretch what he can. He speaks in a direct, factual way, answers questions plainly, and tends to make stern, quick decisions, but there is a quiet sense of longing when he talks about how his body used to feel before the crash. He leans into preventive care, values community service, and regularly encourages friends and coworkers to get HIV testing and learn prevention, even while he works on getting his weekend drinking under better control.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of deep right groin/hip pain and difficulty bearing weight starting 5 days ago after returning to high-intensity workouts, with worsening over the last 48 hours.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports deep, aching pain centered in the right groin and lateral hip, intermittently sharp with pivoting and climbing stairs, now occurring at rest and waking him at night. Pain worsens with weight-bearing and prolonged standing and has led to a noticeable limp and reduced activity. Associated stiffness on first standing, decreased range of motion (difficulty flexing the hip and rotating the leg), and occasional clicking sensation. Notes mild perceived swelling and tightness around the hip and early weakness from avoiding use. Denies fever, chills, recent respiratory or gastrointestinal infection, redness or warmth over the joint, numbness or tingling down the leg, calf pain/swelling, low back pain radiating below the knee, urinary symptoms, or recent open wound.

current_general_status.diet: Keto-inclined diet; appetite slightly reduced due to pain.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor for several nights due to nighttime hip pain and difficulty finding a comfortable position.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements; no constipation or diarrhea.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant recent weight change.

previous_diseases: Right hip fracture/dislocation from a motor vehicle collision 2 years ago, treated non-operatively after reduction and immobilization
Alcohol use disorder (binge-pattern), not in sustained remission
medical_history.long_term_medication: None. Uses over-the-counter ibuprofen intermittently for pain.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Major trauma: right hip fracture/dislocation 2 years ago; no surgeries reported.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on childhood immunizations; last tetanus booster approximately 2 years ago after the motor vehicle collision; influenza vaccine not received this season.

personal_history.smoking: Occasional cigarette smoking socially, about 1 to 2 cigarettes on weekends for several years; no vaping.
personal_history.alcohol: Excessive intake: binge drinking on weekends (often 8 to 12 drinks in a night) with intermittent weekday drinking; acknowledges difficulty cutting down.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father with hypertension; otherwise none known.

persona: He is a 26-year-old medical administrator who lives in a shared household and has been under extra stress lately from difficult customer interactions at work. He speaks in a direct, factual way and often organizes his thoughts in quick bullet-point style, expecting an empathetic bedside manner even if he comes off guarded or short-tempered when he feels dismissed. Between episodes he functions normally and tries to stick to a simple exercise program, but poor sleep has made him more forgetful and on edge. Despite that, he values harmony and keeping things steady at home.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Abrupt onset of severe, strictly left-sided pain around the eye that began 6 days ago, often waking him from sleep about 60–90 minutes after falling asleep.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: For the past 6 days he has had sudden, excruciating left retro-orbital/orbital pain described as drilling/burning, peaking within about 10 minutes and lasting 45–90 minutes per episode. Attacks initially occurred once nightly but have progressed to 2–3 attacks per day, commonly late night and early morning; he feels completely normal between attacks. Episodes are accompanied on the same side by tearing, red eye, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, and mild eyelid swelling; he becomes restless and paces, reporting that lying flat worsens the pain. He notes palpitations and a sense of panic during severe attacks and has been avoiding sleep due to fear of recurrence. He reports increased emotional stress and depressed mood over the last month with high-functioning anxiety, and describes fragmented REM sleep with vivid dreams and frequent awakenings. He denies fever, neck stiffness, persistent headache between attacks, focal weakness/numbness, speech or vision loss (other than tearing during attacks), head trauma, rash, jaw claudication, sinus pressure with purulent discharge, cough, or recent sick contacts. No nausea/vomiting or photophobia as a dominant feature, and no aura symptoms.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite during the week due to sleep disruption and anxiety; otherwise regular diet when pain-free.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep with frequent awakenings and avoidance of bedtime; reports REM-related awakenings and daytime fatigue.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change.

previous_diseases: Generalized anxiety symptoms with intermittent depressed mood (not formally hospitalized)
Seasonal allergic rhinitis
medical_history.long_term_medication: None. Uses occasional over-the-counter ibuprofen with little benefit for current attacks.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Childhood immunizations up to date; received tetanus booster during military service.

personal_history.smoking: Former smoker; smoked about 1 pack per day from age 18 to 24, quit 2 years ago.
personal_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol on weekends; reports that alcohol can trigger or worsen the current episodes so he has stopped drinking this week.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother with depression; otherwise none known.

persona: He is a full-term newborn whose days are shaped by feeding, sleep, and close observation from his parents. His family lives on a tight budget and leans on tradition, elders, and stories of ancestors when making decisions, which makes them steady and practical even when worried. They speak in a stoic, minimizing way, often saying he is "probably fine" and downplaying how sleepy or floppy he seems until symptoms become hard to ignore. They also struggle to describe details clearly due to a language barrier and feel uneasy about telehealth, preferring in-person help they can trust.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent poor feeding with vomiting and increasing sleepiness starting on day 9 of life, worsening over the past 24 hours.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Full-term male newborn initially fed well for the first week of life, then developed recurrent non-bilious vomiting after feeds with poor appetite and reduced wakefulness. Over several days he became more irritable between feeds, with decreased urine output and fewer wet diapers; parents report episodes of cool extremities and appearing "floppy" during crying. Mild generalized darkening of the skin has been noticed compared with siblings. No documented fever, no cough or nasal congestion, no projectile vomiting, no green/bilious emesis, no blood in stool, no seizures, and no known sick contacts. Symptoms have been recurrent and gradually progressive since onset, with concern for dehydration.

current_general_status.diet: Breastfeeding with supplemental formula; currently taking smaller volumes and often vomits shortly after feeding.
current_general_status.sleep: More sleepy than usual, difficult to arouse for feeds over the last day.
current_general_status.excretion: Decreased urine output; fewer wet diapers. Stools less frequent but not bloody.
current_general_status.weight_change: Poor weight gain since birth with slight weight loss over the past few days per caregiver report.

previous_diseases: Current neonatal presentation with recurrent vomiting and dehydration since day 9 of life
medical_history.long_term_medication: None currently; newborn has not yet started chronic medications.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Received routine newborn vaccinations per local schedule to date (including birth-dose vaccines as applicable); others not yet due.

personal_history.smoking: None (infant). Household reports no indoor smoking.
personal_history.alcohol: None (infant).
personal_history.obstetric_history: Born at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery to a G2P2 mother; normal prenatal ultrasounds. No NICU stay. Discharged home with mother. Breastfed from birth.

family_history.similar_illness: Positive: an older maternal uncle had congenital adrenal hypoplasia with severe salt-wasting episodes in infancy; two male cousins on the maternal side affected. Mother is a known carrier of a pathogenic DAX-1 (NR0B1) variant. Parents report marriage between individuals from families with a known history of the condition, and they pursued family-based counseling before conception.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Known X-linked DAX-1 (NR0B1) pathogenic variant in the maternal lineage; otherwise none reported.

demographics.name: Rose Olson
demographics.occupation: Recreational skier; recently started a new job (non-strenuous)

persona: He is a 64-year-old middle-class man who lives simply and keeps to himself, preferring minimal contact outside a small circle of people who care about him. He is generally optimistic and often looks for practical encouragement, guided by strong beliefs in forgiveness and justice, and he tends to make decisions with others in mind. He’s adjusting to a new job and likes the convenience of eating out, while also choosing services based on what his insurance plan covers. When talking with clinicians, he is direct and factual, describing what happened in plain everyday terms without overexplaining.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of severe headache with repeated vomiting and a first-time generalized seizure starting 1 day ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient was in his usual state of health until yesterday morning when a new, progressively worsening holocephalic headache began abruptly, described as pressure-like and worse with bending over. Over the next several hours he developed multiple episodes of projectile, non-bloody vomiting without abdominal pain or diarrhea, with brief partial relief of headache after emesis. Family noted intermittent confusion and slowed speech later in the day. In the evening he had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about 1 minute with postictal drowsiness; no prior seizure history. He also reports new constipation over the past several days and intermittent ringing in the left ear. Denies fever, chills, neck stiffness, recent head trauma, chest pain, syncope, focal limb numbness prior to the seizure, or recent alcohol binge. No persistent cough, shortness of breath, or recent gastrointestinal illness.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite since symptom onset; tolerating small sips of water only due to nausea.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep overnight due to headache.
current_general_status.excretion: Constipated for 3 days; urination unchanged.
current_general_status.weight_change: No acute weight change reported; long-standing gradual weight gain.

previous_diseases: Obesity
Hypertension
Remote treated tuberculous meningitis in his 30s with full functional recovery
Known intracranial mass previously followed intermittently on imaging (reported as likely meningioma), no prior surgery
medical_history.long_term_medication: Amlodipine daily for blood pressure; inconsistent adherence to medications per family. No chronic antiseizure therapy.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Appendectomy in his 20s; no significant head trauma history.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccines per patient; received influenza vaccine this season; unsure about pneumococcal booster dates.

personal_history.smoking: Former smoker: quit 10 years ago after about 25 years of smoking; no vaping.
personal_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol (1-2 drinks/week); no heavy use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.similar_illness: Mother had a brain tumor in later life (details unknown); otherwise None.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes; no known hereditary neurologic disorders.

persona: He is a 35-year-old man who works long, irregular nights breeding and caring for sled dogs, and he is focused on moving into steadier airline work for better career growth. He tends to be reserved and a bit anxious about criticism, so he prefers straightforward, educational explanations and will answer questions in a direct, factual way using simple words. Money is tight and he struggles with online patient portals, but he values getting back on track and will follow a clear plan if it feels realistic. When stress piles up, he unwinds by watching a comedy show.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Since about 3 weeks ago, new onset of ejaculating sooner than desired during intercourse with difficulty delaying climax, starting after a period of increased stress and relationship tension.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports a noticeable recent shortening of time to ejaculation during partnered vaginal intercourse, often occurring within a couple of minutes of penetration and sometimes before he feels ready, with a sense that once the urge starts he cannot "hold back." This is a new change from his prior baseline and has become more frequent over the past several weeks, leading to worry before sex and preoccupation with "performing well." He describes anticipatory anxiety, irritability afterward, and occasional avoidance of initiating sex due to fear of disappointing his partner; partner conflict has increased, and he feels significant social pressure. He generally maintains erection and libido but notes reduced enjoyment because he is focused on trying not to climax. No penile pain, no dysuria, no urethral discharge, no hematuria, no fever, no perineal pain, and no new genital rash or ulcers. No clear episodes of complete inability to obtain an erection, no anorgasmia, and no numbness or weakness.

current_general_status.diet: Tries to follow dietary restrictions and generally eats health-conscious meals, but night-shift work leads to irregular meal timing and more late-night snacking.
current_general_status.sleep: Typically sleeps less than 6 hours per night due to night shifts and caregiving responsibilities; feels chronically fatigued.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel movements; urination without burning, frequency change, or urgency.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional mild weight loss over the past 1 to 2 months.

previous_diseases: Hyperthyroidism diagnosed 6 months ago (ongoing symptoms include heat intolerance, palpitations at times)
medical_history.long_term_medication: Methimazole started 5 months ago with variable adherence; occasional propranolol as needed for palpitations. No current psychiatric medications.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
infectious_diseases: No known history of sexually transmitted infections
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult immunizations per patient report; last tetanus booster within the past 10 years.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker.
personal_history.alcohol: Drinks socially about 1 to 2 times per month; avoids heavy use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Not applicable

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother with hypothyroidism; otherwise none known.

persona: He is a 7-year-old elementary student who likes solving Rubik's cubes and is surprisingly sharp, though he can be a bit disorganized about routines and homework. He tends to be stoic and minimizing about how he feels, insisting he is fine and downplaying discomfort until his caregiver pushes for help, which can leave him irritable and stressed during flare-ups. His family is financially stable and values charity, and his caregiver leans toward a free-range approach, encouraging independence while staying attentive after a past serious car-accident hospitalization that made health worries feel more urgent.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent sore throat with bad breath and intermittent ear blockage/hearing difficulty for about 8 months, worse again over the past 2 weeks after a cold.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past several months, the child has had repeated episodes of sore throat with a scratchy or foreign-body sensation, loud snoring during colds, and persistent halitosis. During or shortly after these upper respiratory infections, he often develops a feeling of fullness or blockage in one or both ears with muffled hearing and intermittent tinnitus; symptoms usually improve partially between colds but recur with seasonal changes (notably during late fall and early spring). This pattern is most consistent with recurrent/persistent middle-ear effusion with Eustachian tube dysfunction; no episodes of severe acute ear pain and no persistent purulent otorrhea. In the past 2 weeks, after a runny nose and cough, the ear blockage and hearing difficulty became more noticeable at school, and he has complained of mild ear discomfort at night; no severe acute ear pain. Occasional low-grade fever and fatigue during flare-ups. No dizziness/vertigo, no severe headache, no facial weakness, no neck stiffness, no persistent high fever, no shortness of breath, and no known foreign body insertion into the ear. No continuous foul-smelling ear drainage reported, though caregiver notes rare brief episodes of dampness at the ear canal after colds.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite slightly decreased during sore-throat flare-ups; otherwise normal, prefers soft foods when throat hurts.
current_general_status.sleep: Restless sleep during colds with snoring and mouth breathing; otherwise adequate.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change reported.

Allergic rhinitis symptoms during pollen seasons (intermittent sneezing and nasal congestion)
medical_history.long_term_medication: None. Uses intermittent acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever/pain and occasional over-the-counter saline nasal spray during colds; caregiver reports the child sometimes confuses similarly named medicines and needs close supervision for dosing. Current weight: 26 kg.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Reported up to date for age per caregiver.

personal_history.alcohol: None.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Full-term birth by uncomplicated vaginal delivery per caregiver; no neonatal intensive care stay.

persona: She is a 51-year-old textile curator who values aesthetics and has a shoestring budget, often juggling practical needs with a creative, “starving artist” lifestyle. With strangers she can be a bit shy at first, but once comfortable she’s direct and factual, answering questions in simple terms and clearly describing what she feels without using medical jargon. She tends to be persistent and appreciative, wanting to do the right thing and thanking staff for their time, though she sometimes needs extra time to make choices, especially when costs or injections are involved. She doesn’t drink alcohol and tries to manage her health steadily while fostering a child.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Abrupt onset of high fever with severe body aches and profound fatigue starting 2 days ago after a coworker had similar symptoms.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Two days ago she developed sudden chills followed by a rapid fever peaking around 39.4C with severe generalized myalgia, headache, and marked weakness described as being "completely drained." Over the last 24 hours she has developed mild sore throat, nasal congestion, and a dry cough with intermittent chest tightness but no pleuritic chest pain. She reports decreased appetite and mild nausea without vomiting; no diarrhea. She feels more short of breath than usual with exertion but denies resting dyspnea, cyanosis, confusion, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, neck stiffness, or seizures. No dysuria or flank pain. No recent travel; reports multiple close indoor contacts at a new workplace. She has not taken antiviral therapy; used acetaminophen with partial temporary relief. Negative differential symptoms: no purulent sputum, no wheezing crisis, no hemoptysis, no new leg swelling, and no rash.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite since onset; taking mostly fluids and light vegetarian foods when tolerated.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented due to fever, chills, and body aches.
current_general_status.excretion: Urine output slightly reduced; no diarrhea; one normal bowel movement yesterday.
current_general_status.weight_change: No known recent weight change over the past week.

previous_diseases: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Coronary artery disease with prior stent placement
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Obesity
Chronic kidney disease (stage 3 by history)
Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis with chronically elevated liver enzymes
History of breast cancer treated with surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy, currently in remission
Rheumatoid arthritis on immunosuppressive therapy
Chronic anemia related to prior chemotherapy (intermittent)
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy
medical_history.long_term_medication: Metformin and basal insulin; carvedilol and lisinopril; atorvastatin; low-dose aspirin; furosemide as needed; immunosuppressive therapy for rheumatoid arthritis (methotrexate with periodic biologic injections); vitamin D. Adherence is sometimes limited by cost and difficulty with injections due to needle-phobia.

medical_history.blood_transfusion: Received transfusion during chemotherapy in her 20s per patient report; none since.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother with type 2 diabetes and hypertension; father with ischemic heart disease; no known hereditary cancer syndromes reported.

persona: He is a 53-year-old jewelry artist living on a tight budget, proud of doing careful, hands-on work and holding onto dignity even when life feels unstable. He speaks in clipped, minimizing phrases like "it's nothing" and "I'm fine," and only showed up because his spouse insisted, despite being more rattled by symptoms and discomfort than he admits. He processes information slowly and prefers long, in-depth reading he can review later, so he asks for things to be explained plainly and written down. Off-hours, he keeps his mood peaceful, is an indifferent moviegoer, and relies on rideshare to get around, especially when pain flares.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset inability to achieve a firm erection during attempted intercourse starting 10 days ago after a flare of pelvic pain while packing for relocation.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports abrupt difficulty obtaining and maintaining an erection despite usual stimulation, with markedly reduced rigidity and erections that subside within minutes, resulting in inability to complete satisfactory intercourse. Over the past 10 days the problem has persisted with each attempt and has caused rising performance worry, low mood, and avoidance of intimacy. Libido is somewhat reduced but still present; he notes occasional early-morning erections are less frequent than before. Accompanying symptoms include chronic pelvic/hip pain that limits activity and intermittent urinary frequency and weak stream, without burning. Denies fever, urethral discharge, genital ulcers, new penile curvature, testicular pain or swelling, hematuria, flank pain, acute back injury, numbness/weakness in legs, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals while relocating; tends toward rich, high-calorie foods and frequent takeout; variable hydration.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep with frequent awakenings due to anxiety and pelvic discomfort.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements every 1-2 days; occasional constipation when using pain medication. Urination: intermittent frequency and weak stream, no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear recent weight change.

previous_diseases: Chronic anxiety and recurrent depressive episodes
Chronic pelvic and hip pain after prior pelvic fractures
Benign prostatic hyperplasia symptoms (urinary hesitancy/weak stream, not formally evaluated recently)
medical_history.long_term_medication: Intermittent NSAIDs for pelvic pain; occasional short courses of opioid analgesics for flares. Previously prescribed an SSRI for anxiety/depression but adherence has been inconsistent. No nitrates reported.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: History of pelvic fractures from a fall from height approximately 6 years ago; treated with orthopedic fixation and prolonged rehabilitation. No known urologic surgery.
medical_history.blood_transfusion: Received blood transfusion during hospitalization for pelvic fracture management (single admission, no complications reported).
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on tetanus; receives influenza vaccine intermittently; has not had recent adult booster review.

personal_history.smoking: Former smoker; quit about 8 years ago after a 20+ year history. No vaping.
personal_history.alcohol: Social drinking 1-2 times per week; heavier intake during periods of stress but denies daily use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension; mother with major depressive disorder. No known inherited bleeding or clotting disorders.

persona: He is a 26-year-old man who works freelance as an advocacy coordinator and is careful about money, aiming for financial independence even when things feel underfunded. He talks in a direct, matter-of-fact way, describing what he notices day to day without using medical jargon, and he answers questions clearly without drifting off topic. Recently separated and still figuring out life, he stays personable and confident when making decisions, double-checking details before committing. He’s been trying to improve how he communicates with caregivers and tracks sleep and activity with a Fitbit, usually reading only at bedtime.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent purulent discharge from the left ear with reduced hearing, recurring over the past 3 months; current flare started 5 days ago after a cold.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports recurrent episodes of ear drainage that improve temporarily with over-the-counter ear drops and then return, often after upper respiratory symptoms. Over the last 5 days, drainage has increased in volume and thickness (yellow-green, occasionally blood-streaked), with mild to moderate dull ear discomfort and a blocked-ear sensation. Hearing on the affected side has gradually worsened during flares and only partially recovers between episodes; occasional tinnitus during active drainage. Notes mild sore throat and nasal congestion preceding this episode. Denies high fever, chills, severe sharp otalgia, facial weakness, severe headache, vertigo, neck stiffness, swelling/redness behind the ear, or foul chemical exposure. No recent swimming/diving and no known foreign body insertion.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite mildly decreased during the current flare; otherwise regular meals.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep; uses sleep medication on some nights, worse when ear discomfort and congestion increase.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements; no diarrhea.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change noted.

previous_diseases: Recurrent upper respiratory tract infections (self-limited)
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, diagnosed in adulthood

persona: He is a 10-year-old student who is friendly and outgoing, but his feelings can be mixed and harder to explain, especially when he is hurting or tired. Because his family has been moving around a lot with limited money and spotty internet, they rely on faith and community support, and he often looks to trusted adults for reassurance. When asked questions, he answers in short, clear, everyday words, saying things like "my lower belly hurts" and "my belly button is leaking," and he will mention what makes it worse, like bumps during sports. He tries to be careful about falls and talks excitedly about wanting to sing country music someday.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent lower midline abdominal pain with intermittent umbilical drainage for about 6 months, worsening over the past 5 days after a minor fall during sports practice.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 6 months, the patient has had episodes of dull, pressure-like pain in the lower midline abdomen, sometimes tender to touch, occurring every few weeks and lasting hours to a day. Over the past 5 days, pain has become more frequent and constant, with the family noticing a small amount of cloudy, foul-smelling moisture at the umbilicus and mild redness around it. He reports occasional urinary urgency and burning with urination during flares, but is still able to void; no gross blood in urine. He has had intermittent low-grade temperatures at home and decreased appetite during painful episodes. No persistent vomiting, no inability to pass stool or gas, no severe diarrhea, no chest pain or shortness of breath, and no testicular pain or swelling.

current_general_status.diet: Vegetarian diet; appetite slightly decreased during recent flare but tolerating oral fluids and regular diet as tolerated.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, interrupted sleep due to abdominal discomfort for the past several nights.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements daily without constipation; urination more frequent during flares with occasional dysuria, no urinary retention reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change reported.

previous_diseases: Recurrent episodes of lower abdominal pain with occasional urinary symptoms since early childhood, not fully evaluated
Possible prior urinary tract infection treated at urgent care about 1 year ago (family recalls short course of oral antibiotics)
medical_history.long_term_medication: None.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No prior surgeries. Minor sports-related bruises; no major trauma.

persona: She is a 45-year-old single wellness director who tries to live cleanly on a vegetarian diet, but the reality of a low-wage job and an overbooked schedule keeps her stressed and short on rest. She speaks with the certainty of an "Internet doctor," insisting she has already pinpointed the problem from late-night searches and pushing for specific scans or treatments, getting prickly when questioned. A decorated veteran who is outspoken about gentrification, she likes having influence in decisions and uses a medication reminder app, though cost sometimes makes follow-through uneven. Sensitive skin and a penicillin allergy make her cautious about new products and prescriptions.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden inability to extend the right wrist and fingers with numbness over the back of the hand starting immediately after a fall from a motorcycle 12 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient reports an acute fall onto an outstretched right hand with immediate severe wrist pain and visible swelling, followed shortly by weakness lifting the wrist and straightening the fingers and thumb (described as the hand 'hanging down'). She also notes tingling and reduced sensation over the dorsal thumb-index web space and dorsum of the thumb. Symptoms have remained stable since the injury, with difficulty gripping objects and performing daily tasks. Accompanying symptoms include localized tenderness at the distal forearm/wrist, bruising, and pain worsened by movement. She denies neck pain, shoulder pain, facial droop, speech difficulty, unilateral leg weakness, loss of consciousness, chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, or new rash. No color change or severe coldness of the hand is reported.

current_general_status.diet: Vegetarian diet; reduced intake since injury due to pain and stress.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep overnight due to pain and worry about function.
current_general_status.excretion: No change in urination or bowel habits.
current_general_status.weight_change: No recent weight change.

previous_diseases: Smoke inhalation injury from a house fire in her 30s (recovered)
Intermittent asthma-like wheeze since smoke exposure (uses rescue inhaler rarely)

medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Prior left distal radius fracture treated with casting about 6 years ago. Remote laceration to the right forearm from a kitchen accident requiring sutures (no lasting deficits). History of traction-type arm injury while lifting/assisting an elderly parent about 1 year ago with transient forearm pain.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on tetanus booster within the last 5 years per patient; routine adult immunizations otherwise uncertain.

persona: He is a newborn boy in a financially stable Paris household, with parents who value keeping the family calm and united while they navigate ongoing developmental worries. His caregivers speak in a direct, factual way and answer questions clearly, though they sometimes struggle to find the right words in French and keep it simple. They can come across tough and a bit angry from stress, but they try to be fair and cooperative during visits. They mostly skim news headlines and remain very cautious about illness, especially since the pandemic, and they track feeding, sleep, and milestones closely. Note: the patient name may be a synthetic placeholder used for de-identification; clinicians and caregivers consistently refer to the patient as male.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Parents report persistent developmental concerns noted since the first weeks of life in an infant under 1 year old (currently around 2 months of age), with ongoing follow-up over the past 2 months after receiving abnormal genetic test results.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Since early infancy (from the first weeks of life) in this infant under 1 year old, the parents have noticed reduced visual tracking and social engagement compared with peers, with inconsistent calming and increased startle/irritability during handling; feeding has been variable with occasional poor latch and prolonged feeding times. Over the past several weeks (around 6-8 weeks of age), tone has seemed mildly low with delayed head control for age and limited babbling for developmental stage. No episodes of cyanosis, no fever, no cough, no vomiting or diarrhea beyond typical spit-up, no seizures or staring spells observed, and no acute change suggesting infection. The family is seeking follow-up after laboratory testing identified a pathogenic variant involving CGG repeat expansion in the 5' untranslated region of exon 1 of the FMR1 gene with abnormal methylation and reduced/absent synthesis of fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP), in the context of a high number of maternal CGG repeats.

current_general_status.diet: Primarily breastmilk with occasional formula supplementation; intake can be slow with intermittent fatigue during feeds.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with frequent awakenings; difficult to settle during warm nights.
current_general_status.excretion: Wet diapers appropriate; stools soft, regular; no blood or mucus reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: Slow but steady gain per parental report; no sudden weight loss.

previous_diseases: None
medical_history.long_term_medication: None.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Newborn vaccinations up to date per local schedule; parents report plans to continue routine immunizations.

personal_history.smoking: None (infant). No household smoking reported by parents.
personal_history.alcohol: None (infant).
personal_history.obstetric_history: Born in Paris, France after an uncomplicated pregnancy with routine prenatal care. Term delivery via spontaneous vaginal birth; no NICU stay reported. Newborn screening otherwise unremarkable. Family notes a recent heatwave period that made feeding/sleep more difficult but no heat-related illness.

family_history.similar_illness: Maternal family history notable for a maternal uncle described as having lifelong learning difficulties and social/attention problems; no confirmed diagnosis reported. Mother reported to carry a high number of CGG repeats on prior testing.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: None known beyond the reported maternal CGG repeat finding; no known congenital syndromes in two generations.

persona: She is a 72-year-old married woman who gets by on a tight budget and spends her spare time tinkering with small game projects, finding that creative problem-solving helps her stay focused on rebuilding her strength and routines. She speaks quickly and at length, mixing wry humor with a cynical edge, and often interrupts to ask for reassurance because she worries her symptoms mean something dire, especially after reading alarming news. She prefers simple, convenient refill plans and scheduling that fits around obligations at her residence, and she is outspoken about women's rights while staying cautious in unfamiliar settings after a past assault.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of nasal congestion and sore throat followed by worsening cough and wheeze over the past 6 months, with the current flare beginning 7 days ago after a sudden temperature drop and wildfire smoke exposure.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months she reports multiple bouts that start with runny nose, nasal congestion, scratchy sore throat, and low-grade fever, then progress over one to three days to chest tightness, shortness of breath with exertion, and paroxysmal cough that is worse at night and early morning. During the current episode (7 days), upper respiratory symptoms began after several days of fatigue and high stress related to a system overhaul at her residence; within two days she developed audible expiratory wheeze, increased use of rescue inhaler with only partial relief, and sleep disruption from cough. She notes clear to pale-yellow sputum in small amounts without a clear increase in volume or frank purulence; hoarseness; and post-nasal drip. Given recurrent episodes with smoking exposure plus obstructive spirometry/hyperinflation, COPD or asthma-COPD overlap is considered; asthma is favored due to symptom variability, allergic features, eosinophilia, and bronchodilator reversibility. She denies coughing up blood, pleuritic chest pain, leg swelling, fainting, severe chills/rigors, facial swelling, or new rash. No known recent aspiration event; no persistent vomiting or severe diarrhea. She denies sharp one-sided throat pain with drooling, and denies sudden severe shortness of breath at rest suggestive of an acute emergency event.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite during this week due to sore throat; otherwise health-conscious and checks food labels; tries to limit salty processed foods.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep with frequent nighttime awakenings from cough and chest tightness; reports acute anxiety when symptoms worsen.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel and urination habits unchanged; no dysuria or diarrhea.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant recent weight change reported.

previous_diseases: Recurrent episodic wheeze and cough for many years, worse with smoke exposure and seasonal temperature changes
Allergic rhinitis (intermittent sneezing and nasal itching, seasonal)
Hypertension
medical_history.long_term_medication: Uses an inhaled controller intermittently and a short-acting rescue inhaler as needed; takes metoprolol succinate (cardioselective beta blocker) daily for hypertension with caution given asthma (avoid nonselective beta blockers). Adherence is inconsistent due to difficulty following complex instructions and difficulty reading long forms; often relies on family or staff to clarify dosing.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Cataract surgery in both eyes several years ago; no major trauma reported.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccines per patient report; receives annual influenza vaccine most years; uncertain about timing of last booster.

personal_history.smoking: Long-term smoking history with ongoing smoke irritation; previously smoked about a pack per day for decades and is currently trying to cut down but still smokes some days. Also exposed to air pollution and recent wildfire smoke.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare social alcohol use; none during the current illness.
personal_history.obstetric_history: G3P3; all term vaginal deliveries; no major obstetric complications reported.

family_history.similar_illness: Daughter has a history of recurrent wheeze with seasonal allergies; otherwise none reported.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother had hypertension; father had type 2 diabetes; no other known hereditary conditions.

persona: She is an 89-year-old woman in Berwyn Heights who has lived with years of unstable housing and now relies on shelters and familiar community spots, leaning on routines and small traditions to feel grounded while still talking passionately about how society should improve. A retired correctional facility warden, she remains fiercely loyal to the teams and causes she follows, and she insists she is managing fine even as stress pulls her toward salty fast food and other comfort choices. In visits she speaks rapidly and at length, jumping from news stories to minor aches, interrupting with fears that every spell of dizziness means something fatal, and she repeatedly asks for reassurance while trying to “troubleshoot” her iPhone to track readings.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of worsening high blood pressure with headaches and dizziness over the past 6 months, with more frequent spikes in the last 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months she has noted progressively higher home blood pressure readings despite adherence to medications, with intermittent severe headaches, lightheadedness, and blurred vision during spikes. In the last 3 weeks, episodes have become more frequent and harder to bring down, sometimes accompanied by chest tightness, palpitations, nausea, and reduced exercise tolerance. She also reports intermittent dull flank discomfort and increasing ankle swelling. She denies fever, chills, cough, pleuritic chest pain, focal weakness or speech difficulty, sudden tearing back pain, syncope, gross hematuria, dysuria, or acute severe colicky flank pain. No recent use of decongestants or stimulant medications is reported.

current_general_status.diet: High-calorie diet with preference for salty foods; attempting partial carbohydrate restriction for diabetes but inconsistent.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with frequent awakenings; occasionally naps during the day.
current_general_status.excretion: No dysuria; reports nocturia with increased urine volume (polyuria), especially on some days; occasional constipation.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain over several years; weight stable over the last few months.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Hyperlipidemia
Obesity
Osteoarthritis with chronic low back and knee pain limiting activity
Gastroesophageal reflux disease

infectious_diseases: Remote uncomplicated urinary tract infection episodes
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date for age including influenza most years and prior pneumococcal vaccination per patient report.

persona: She is a 47-year-old mid-income cybersecurity analyst with a low-key, anti-ostentatious style who values accuracy and truthfulness, even when she feels self-conscious about her appearance. In conversation she is a poor historian, describing things as "weird" or "off" and mixing up timelines, and she often struggles with long intake forms or confusing similar medication names, so she appreciates calm, step-by-step questions. Widowed and currently pregnant with twins, she leans on routine and finds steady comfort in animals, but tends to put off preventive screenings unless someone she trusts explains why they matter.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually enlarging yellow patches on the inner aspects of both upper eyelids noticed about 10 months ago, with intermittent concern about recurrence/enlargement over the past several months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She first noticed faint, light-yellow discoloration near the medial corners of the upper eyelids that slowly became more visible and slightly raised, with a smooth surface and soft feel. Over time, the plaques have very gradually enlarged and appear more symmetric, and she is increasingly bothered by their cosmetic appearance and social confidence. She denies pain, tenderness, significant itching, bleeding, crusting, ulceration, or drainage. No vision changes, eye redness, photophobia, diplopia, or foreign-body sensation. No fever, facial rash elsewhere, generalized pruritus, eyelid swelling consistent with acute allergy, or recent new topical product exposure.

current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep with occasional nightmares; averages about 5 to 6 hours on work nights.

persona: He is a 4-year-old preschooler (name is de-identified and not indicative of gender) who lives in a large household and his caregivers are very protective, keeping to themselves and relying on public transport to get to appointments. Because money is tight and housing is unstable, they arrive tired and distracted, but are firmly family-first and want quick relief so he can sleep and eat again. One caregiver comes in acting like an “internet doctor,” confidently insisting it must be a bacterial ear-and-sinus infection and pushing for specific antibiotics and imaging, yet they calm down when given plain-language explanations and clear next steps.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Brought to the emergency department for nasal congestion with thick nasal discharge and new right ear pain that started after a common cold about 3 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Child developed symptoms of an upper respiratory tract infection one week ago with runny nose and mild cough that partially improved, then over the past 3 days nasal obstruction worsened with yellow-green nasal discharge and foul breath. Over the past 24 hours he developed ear pain and a sense of ear fullness with reduced response to sounds and intermittent ringing-like complaints, worse when lying down. Associated symptoms include frontal/facial pressure, irritability, decreased appetite, low-grade fever, and disturbed sleep. No ear drainage noted. Given the borderline duration for bacterial acute rhinosinusitis, antibiotic therapy is primarily driven by confirmed suppurative acute otitis media with concurrent acute rhinosinusitis symptoms. No severe vomiting, no neck stiffness, no photophobia, no new rash, no wheezing or shortness of breath, and no altered level of consciousness; no reported foreign body insertion or recent swimming/diving.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite but tolerating fluids; picky eater; no special diet currently.

persona: She is a 37-year-old single campaign manager working long, low-wage hours on a catastrophic health plan and strongly values privacy, so she avoids discussing personal or medical details unless pressed. In conversation she is stoic and minimizing, insisting it is "nothing" and claiming she is fine, only showing up because a family member pushed her to, even as sleepless nights leave her irritable and constantly hungry. With low health literacy and a history of family separation and past exposure to warfare-related stress, she tends to push through discomfort, bruises easily, and struggles to explain what she needs or ask for help.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of severe itching with thickened, rough patches of skin on the back of the neck and wrists worsening over the past 7 months, with a flare over the last 2 weeks after increased work stress and poor sleep.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Itching began intermittently on the posterior neck and bilateral wrists, described as an intense, mosquito-bite-like sensation that comes in bouts and becomes difficult to resist scratching. Over months, repeated scratching and rubbing against shirt collars/watch bands led to progressively thickened, rough, darker patches with more visible skin lines and occasional superficial excoriations and mild scaling. Symptoms are worse at night and during stress, and flares are triggered by spicy meals, alcohol, sweating, and use of fragranced cosmetics; she also reports occasional stinging when applying certain lotions. Associated symptoms include sleep disruption, daytime fatigue, irritability, and intermittent upper abdominal discomfort with bloating and early satiety. She denies fever, chills, purulent drainage, rapidly spreading redness, blistering, oral/genital ulcers, diffuse hives, facial/lip swelling, wheezing, new joint pain, or recent sick contacts. No recent travel or known scabies exposure; no similar rash in household contacts.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals; frequent spicy foods; uses takeout due to schedule; drinks coffee daily; tries to hydrate but often forgets during flares.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep quality with chronic insomnia related to stress; wakes frequently due to itching, especially at night.
current_general_status.excretion: Intermittent dyspepsia with bloating and occasional constipation during stressful weeks; no blood in stool reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant unintentional weight change.

medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine childhood vaccines; influenza vaccine inconsistent in recent years.

persona: An 88-year-old widower and retired instructor, he’s a dependable, reliability-first decision-maker who built his life around practical skills and steady routines, shaped in part by his years as an Air Force veteran. He speaks in a stoic, minimizing way, brushing off symptoms with “it’s nothing” and only showing up because a family member insisted, though his low energy and fatigue are hard to hide. Financially comfortable and strongly invested in rehabilitation and safety, he prefers clear plans he can follow at home, uses paratransit for appointments due to limited mobility, and takes quiet pride in having raised independent kids.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent sudden episodes of rapid pounding heartbeat with lightheadedness starting about 6 months ago, with an episode lasting about 30 minutes earlier today while resting at home.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months he has had intermittent spells of abrupt-onset racing heartbeat that often begin at rest or with minimal activity and can stop suddenly. Episodes initially occurred about once every few weeks but have become more frequent in the last month, sometimes several times per week, lasting from a few minutes up to an hour. During episodes he reports chest tightness, mild shortness of breath, and anxiety with a sense of impending doom; he sometimes feels sweaty and mildly nauseated. He notes occasional near-fainting but denies complete loss of consciousness. Between episodes he has chronic exertional fatigue and baseline shortness of breath consistent with his known reduced exercise tolerance. He denies fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling, new focal neurologic deficits, or persistent crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw/arm lasting more than 20 minutes.

present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past months, the patient notes progressive difficulty with contrast and peripheral “missing spots,” with intermittent brow/temple pressure and occasional glare/washed-out colors. While glare and dim-light difficulty may reflect coexisting age-related lens changes and/or ocular surface dryness, objective testing (abnormal Ishihara, delayed VEP P100, RNFL thinning on OCT, and patchy peripheral scotomas on perimetry) raises concern for optic nerve/visual pathway dysfunction, with suspected glaucomatous optic neuropathy as a leading contributor. Symptoms are described as mixed, with functional impact on reading and detailed work tasks.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradual worsening of blurry vision and difficulty seeing in dim light over the past 10 months, noticed while reading on a phone and driving at night.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over ~10 months he has had progressively reduced clarity of both distance and near vision, needing brighter light and larger font size; he reports increasing glare and halos around lights at night and more frequent eye strain after prolonged screen use at work. Over the last 6 weeks he also notes intermittent patchy “missing spots” in peripheral vision, more noticeable in the evenings, and occasional mild brow/temple pressure without severe eye pain. He describes colors as looking “more washed out” compared with last year. Accompanying symptoms include dryness/foreign-body sensation after long near work, intermittent mild headaches, and increased fatigue with reading. He denies sudden curtain-like vision loss, new shower of floaters, flashing lights, diplopia, focal limb weakness, facial droop, fever, jaw claudication, severe nausea/vomiting, or acute severe eye pain/redness.

current_general_status.diet: Intermittent fasting most days; tries to limit salt and processed foods; drinks adequate water; avoids grapefruit and other foods he worries may interact with medications.

persona: She is a 52-year-old Head Start director who takes pride in being a steady, supportive presence in her community, shaped in part by earlier years of structured leadership before moving into education work. Money is often tight, so she usually relies on rides from family or friends to get to appointments, and she tries to stay engaged in her care through the patient portal even though the steps sometimes confuse her. In conversation she can be warm and charismatic yet oddly elusive, describing things as “weird” or “off” and mixing up timelines, so clinicians often have to ask focused follow-up questions to pin down details.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradual onset of burning pain and numbness in both feet with nighttime worsening for about 8 months, prompting primary care consultation.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 8 months she has noticed intermittent tingling and a crawling sensation in the toes that progressed to persistent burning dysesthesia and electric shock-like stabbing pain in both feet, now extending to the ankles in a symmetric pattern. Symptoms are worse at night and with heat exposure during summer, and light contact from socks or bedsheets can trigger disproportionate pain. She also reports reduced sensation when stepping on small objects and one minor blister that she did not notice until later. Over the past 3 months she has had episodes of lightheadedness on standing and increased fatigue with exertion, along with dry skin on the feet. She denies focal weakness, acute back pain with radiating leg pain, new bowel or bladder incontinence, fever, rash, joint swelling, recent toxin exposure, or unilateral facial droop. No sudden onset neurologic deficit or speech disturbance.

current_general_status.diet: Attempts a calorie- and carbohydrate-restricted diet with intermittent fasting and supplements, but adherence is inconsistent; increased cravings during stress.

Lately she has been more subdued and tired, tracking her heart rate on a wearable and insisting she has figured out what is happening from online searches, then pushing for specific tests or medications while brushing off details that do not fit her theory. She normally likes weekend bike-share rides with family, but has been avoiding play because of chest tightness, breathlessness, and joint aches that feel worse in cold, rainy weather.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: New onset of palpitations with chest tightness and shortness of breath that began 3 days ago after a week of sore throat and fever about 3 weeks ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: About 3 weeks prior, she had an upper-respiratory illness with sore throat, fever, and enlarged/tender neck glands that improved without medical evaluation. Over the last 3 days, she developed intermittent palpitations at rest with chest tightness and exertional shortness of breath, becoming more noticeable and limiting play; caregiver notes she seems unusually tired and prefers to lie down. She also developed migratory pain and swelling in large joints (knees and ankles), worse in cold, rainy weather, with warmth and decreased willingness to bear weight. Occasional low-grade fever and sweating are reported. No syncope, no cyanosis, no severe pleuritic chest pain, no productive cough, no wheezing, no hemoptysis, no persistent vomiting/diarrhea, and no known recent toxin or medication exposure.

persona: She is a 41-year-old woman with a stable home life who works in targeted sales and outreach, and she tends to approach decisions with curious, slightly whimsical ideas even while feeling easily slighted and sensitive under stress. In visits she speaks quickly and at length, jumping from detailed symptom timelines to side notes about what she read online, her family routines, and why she chose generic medications at the pharmacy, repeatedly asking for reassurance that nothing life-threatening is being missed. She values being inclusive and agreeable, prefers salty convenience foods despite trying to cut back, and is a devoted sports supporter whose fragmented sleep and ongoing legal stress have made her more on-edge lately.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of difficult-to-control high blood pressure with headaches and intermittent muscle weakness for about 3 years, with worsening over the past 2 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 3 years, she reports persistently elevated home and clinic blood pressure despite lifestyle efforts and multiple medication adjustments, with frequent morning headaches, episodic dizziness, and reduced exercise tolerance. Over the last 6 months she has developed intermittent muscle cramps and weakness (notably after heat exposure or gastrointestinal upset), with occasional tingling in the hands and feet; in the past 2 months these episodes have become more frequent and longer lasting. She also notes increased thirst and urinary frequency, including nocturia, which is attributed to hypokalemia/metabolic alkalosis causing a renal concentrating defect (polyuria) rather than a primary glycemic cause; she initially thought it was due to working in hot conditions. She denies chest pain, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, fever, cough, wheezing, hematuria, flank pain, or acute severe "tearing" back pain. No recent pregnancy, no significant recent weight loss, and no stimulant or decongestant use. Prior outpatient evaluation reportedly showed suppressed renal renin activity and evidence of excessive sodium retention (tendency toward fluid retention and salt sensitivity).

current_general_status.diet: Prefers salty foods and frequently consumes preserved/packaged meals; attempting to reduce sodium recently but finds it difficult.

current_general_status.excretion: Increased urine volume with nocturia, attributed to hypokalemia/metabolic alkalosis-related renal concentrating defect; bowel movements generally regular, occasional loose stools during heat exposure.

She speaks in a direct, factual way and asks for clear written steps because medical portals and technology feel overwhelming, and today’s ultrasound news left her startled and shaky, though she uses deep breathing to steady herself. Social and sharing by nature, she leans on a small literature club community, tries to stay culturally respectful in conversations, and is drawn to pediatric safety advocacy.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: During a routine prenatal ultrasound earlier today, was told the fetus has an abdominal wall abnormality at the umbilical area and that diagnostic testing shows 47,XX,+18 (trisomy 18); patient presents the same day for urgent counseling regarding prognosis, further fetal evaluation for associated anomalies, and pregnancy/delivery planning.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient is currently pregnant and reports no acute physical symptoms herself. She describes sudden onset of significant anxiety and distress after being informed that the fetus has protruding abdominal contents at the umbilical region seen on screening imaging, with concern for a membrane-covered sac and possible involvement beyond bowel, and that karyotype results show 47,XX,+18 (trisomy 18). Since the appointment, she has had persistent worry, difficulty concentrating, and intermittent tearfulness. She denies abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, leakage of fluid, fever, dysuria, foul-smelling vaginal discharge, syncope, or decreased fetal movement (if she is already perceiving movement). No recent trauma. No known exposures to alcohol binges, illicit drugs, or new prescription medications since learning of the findings. A detailed fetal anatomic survey and targeted evaluation for anomalies commonly associated with trisomy 18 (including fetal echocardiography and assessment of growth and other organ systems) is planned/underway in coordination with maternal-fetal medicine.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite decreased since receiving prenatal findings; trying to maintain regular meals. History of an eating disorder in remission; currently contemplating weight loss but recognizes pregnancy-related nutritional needs.

persona: He is a 14-year-old student from Tecumseh, Nebraska, raised in a working-class household where he learned to value charity and quietly looks for small ways to give back. He tends to be stoic and minimizing in conversations with clinicians, insisting he is "fine" and downplaying discomfort, and he only showed up because a family member pushed him to come. Despite a generally upbeat, sometimes elated mood when he feels safe, he is skeptical of unconventional approaches and relaxes most when plans are explained step by step, shaped in part by difficult experiences he witnessed growing up.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of painful red eyes with light sensitivity and blurry vision along with a new skin rash for 3 days, following a recent episode of herpes labialis about 10 days prior; symptoms worsened over the last 24 hours. Recent significant sun/UV exposure may have exacerbated symptoms.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three days ago, the patient developed burning eye pain and gritty foreign-body sensation in both eyes with marked photophobia, excessive tearing, and progressive redness and eyelid swelling. Over the next 1 to 2 days, he noticed worsening blurred vision (right worse than left) and increased discomfort with eye opening. Around the same time, a symmetric rash appeared on the backs of the hands and forearms and then spread to elbows and knees, described as round spots with darker centers and surrounding redness; some lesions became small blisters. He reports mild oral soreness and chapped lips but is able to eat. He had a recent episode of clustered lip sores with tingling about 10 days ago that resolved without prescription treatment. He also reports intense ultraviolet exposure from prolonged outdoor sun while in Cairo, Egypt, without consistent eye protection. Accompanying symptoms include mild low-grade fever, fatigue, headache, and mild itching at rash sites. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, wheezing, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, painful urination, joint swelling, neck stiffness, confusion, purulent eye discharge, contact lens use, chemical splash, eye trauma, or recent new medications.

persona: She is a 54-year-old retired senior software engineer who keeps her personal life private and tends to be budget-conscious, often putting off appointments unless something feels really "off." In conversation she is laid-back and a bit spontaneous, but she is also a poor historian, describing symptoms as "weird heat waves" or "funny heart flutters" and mixing up timelines, especially when she is tired and irritable from broken sleep. Recently she has felt vaguely dissatisfied and yearning to feel like herself again, and she admits medical terminology goes over her head, so she double-checks medication interactions and is trying to learn to monitor her blood pressure. She bruises easily and prefers lower-key activities now, though she used to enjoy skiing. She reports increasing difficulty concentrating during day-to-day activities (e.g., errands and household tasks), with forgetfulness (misplacing items, trouble following multi-step instructions).

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of intense hot flashes with night sweats and palpitations starting 10 days ago during a local heatwave, along with new menstrual irregularity over the past 2 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 10 days she has had abrupt episodes of heat rising from the chest to the face with flushing and drenching sweats, occurring multiple times daily and waking her at night; episodes last a few minutes and are followed by chills and fatigue. She reports intermittent palpitations, lightheadedness, and headaches during attacks, with worsening insomnia and daytime irritability. Over the prior 2 months her periods became irregular with variable flow and occasional spotting, which she attributes to stress and recent medical treatment. She also notes vaginal dryness and decreased libido, and increasing difficulty concentrating at work with forgetfulness (misplacing items, trouble following multi-step instructions). Triggers include warm rooms, layered clothing, and emotional stress. Denies fever, persistent cough, chest pain/pressure, syncope, unilateral leg swelling, new focal neurologic deficits, severe unintentional weight loss, or heat exposure causing confusion.

persona: He is a 2-year-old boy in Binghamton who usually bounces back quickly from the colds he seems to pick up often. His caregiver speaks in a direct, factual way, sticking to simple details like when the fever started, how often he threw up, and how many wet diapers he has had, and they are the kind of family that tries to keep up with checkups and safety rules. Lately, the caregiver has been more tense and a bit resentful, leaning on the internet for reassurance, while he gets fussy in waves but will still play for short bursts between belly pain.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Fever followed by intermittent abdominal pain and vomiting starting 1 day ago after several days of cold-like symptoms in late winter.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Had runny nose, mild cough, and decreased appetite for about 3 days, then developed fever yesterday (peaking around 38.8 C) followed several hours later by intermittent crampy abdominal pain that seems worse on the right lower side; between episodes he plays briefly but then becomes fussy again. Vomited 3 times (non-bilious, non-bloody) since onset and has been drinking less. One loose stool today but no persistent diarrhea; urine output is slightly decreased. Overall picture (preceding URI symptoms with lymphocyte-predominant leukocytosis) is compatible with a reactive/viral trigger for mesenteric lymphadenitis. No rash, no neck stiffness, no difficulty breathing, no persistent severe localized abdominal guarding, no bilious vomiting, and no blood in stool.

persona: He is a 67-year-old man who tries to keep life calm and private, yet he arrives visibly keyed up, speaking quickly and at length, often jumping from his worries to side stories about things he read online and advice he picks up in forums. He’s empathetic and apologetic when he feels he’s taking too much time, but he repeatedly asks for reassurance and catastrophizes small lapses, interrupting with fears about what this could mean for his future. Financial stress and being separated add to his anxiety, and he leans on simple routines like heart-healthy grocery planning and gentle training drills to feel in control.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradual worsening forgetfulness and getting lost in familiar places over the past 18 months, noticeably worse in the last 3 months after a missed paratransit pickup.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 1.5 years he has had increasing difficulty learning and retaining new information, repeatedly asking the same questions and misplacing items, with increasing reliance on written reminders that are often forgotten or duplicated. Family reports progressive problems with orientation (confusing dates, occasionally not recognizing a familiar route) and executive function (difficulty managing bills, following multi-step instructions, and organizing appointments). Language changes include intermittent word-finding difficulty and circumstantial, repetitive storytelling. Behavioral changes include irritability and suspiciousness when corrected, with anxiety during complex tasks; no reported hallucinations. Functional impact has progressed from needing help with finances and scheduling to requiring supervision for transportation and medication organization. Accompanying symptoms include reduced attention span and occasional nighttime restlessness. Negative symptoms: no acute focal weakness or numbness, no sudden severe headache, no recent head trauma, no fever, no persistent cough, no chest pain, and no episodes of loss of consciousness; no prominent tremor or sustained rigidity reported.

persona: She is a 61-year-old data science professor living on a limited budget, usually resilient and health-conscious, tracking her weight with a smart scale and trying to protect her digital privacy. Lately she has felt overworked, and she often misplaces everyday items like her keys or phone, which adds to her stress. She speaks in a direct, factual way, describing what she can and cannot do without extra details, but she can become anxious and tearful when her breathing feels worse and she worries about needing anesthesia. Art and creative projects help her unwind, and she hopes to train as a yoga instructor once she is stronger.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Abrupt onset of fever and dry cough with shortness of breath starting 5 days ago after helping clean a storage/barn area with heavy dust and bird droppings at her rehabilitation facility.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Five days ago she developed chills and fever followed within 24 hours by a persistent dry cough, chest tightness, and exertional dyspnea that has gradually worsened, now becoming short of breath with minimal activity and during transfers from her wheelchair. She also reports marked fatigue, reduced appetite, mild pleuritic chest discomfort when coughing, and intermittent headache. She denies hemoptysis, leg swelling or calf pain, syncope, palpitations, focal neurologic deficits, rash, abdominal pain, vomiting, or diarrhea. No known sick contacts. persona: She is a 24-year-old self-employed educational therapist with a stable routine, quietly faith-guided and more comfortable keeping to herself than leaning on others. In conversation she is a poor historian, describing things as "weird" or "off" and mixing up when symptoms started, often gesturing broadly rather than naming one exact spot. She has a guarded, low-trust stance toward healthcare and needs time and plain explanations before she opens up.

About 8 days ago she noticed new scleral yellowing, generalized itching, and dark urine, followed by dull right upper quadrant discomfort and marked fatigue that does not improve with rest. She reports mild low-grade fever early in the course and an unintentional small weight loss due to poor intake. No confusion, no easy bruising or bleeding, no black or bloody stools, no severe abdominal pain, no persistent high fever or chills, no cough, no dysuria, and no recent acetaminophen overdose. Acute HCV is presumed based on abrupt symptom onset with markedly elevated transaminases and a recent high-risk exposure/relapse approximately 2 months prior; no prior documented HCV Ab/RNA results are available to confirm seroconversion, and chronic HCV cannot be fully excluded.

She denies acute severe eye pain, significant photophobia, vision loss, contact lens use, fever, facial swelling, or recent trauma; no vesicular rash, no severe headache, and no diplopia.

He notes mild exertional shortness of breath when walking uphill and a weaker cough during colds, but no resting dyspnea. Intermittent muscle cramps and morning stiffness are present, and he has noticed reduced muscle bulk in the upper arms and thighs. He endorses mild forgetfulness and difficulty remembering multi-step instructions at work, causing anxiety and self-doubt, with periods of reduced appetite and dietary restriction that may contribute to his mild weight loss. He denies acute focal neurologic deficits, numbness or tingling, severe muscle pain, dark urine, fever, rash associated with muscle symptoms, vision loss, ptosis, double vision, choking episodes, or significant dysphagia. He reports occasional palpitations without syncope or chest pain. Course is chronic and recurrent, with gradual progression rather than episodic attacks.

persona: She is a bright, family-centered kindergartener whose mom keeps careful notes and wants to understand what is going on so she can make steady, supportive choices for her child. At home, the family is settled and community-minded, and her caregiver leans on dependable health coverage and practical routines while adjusting to a new job. When asked questions, her mom answers in direct, factual, everyday language: when the rash shows up, where it is, whether it fades when pressed, and when belly pain or limping happens. She stays calm but noticeably more guarded since these flares keep returning.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Mother reports a recurrent, non-blanching purple rash on both legs with intermittent belly pain and ankle/knee aches, flaring again over the past 3 days.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 6–8 months, the child has had several episodes of a purplish-red, slightly raised rash that appears symmetrically on the lower legs and buttocks, does not fade with pressure, darkens over a few days, then slowly fades to brown before clearing. Current flare began after a sore throat and low-grade fever about 10 days ago; she was seen at a local clinic and given an oral antibiotic (mother recalls it was a penicillin or cephalosporin) for a presumed bacterial throat infection. Since the rash returned 3 days ago, she has had intermittent crampy abdominal pain around the belly button with reduced appetite and one episode of non-bloody vomiting, plus aching/swelling in the ankles and one knee with brief limping. No high fever, no persistent cough or shortness of breath, no mouth sores, no neck stiffness, no recent falls/trauma to explain bruising, no generalized easy bleeding (no nosebleeds or gum bleeding), and no known tick bites. Mother has not noticed tea-colored urine, painful urination, facial puffiness, or marked leg swelling.

current_general_status.diet: Eating less than usual since the abdominal pain started; drinking small amounts of water and oral rehydration when offered.
current_general_status.sleep: Restless sleep during pain episodes; otherwise sleeps through the night.
current_general_status.excretion: Stools slightly reduced in frequency; no black stools reported. Urination normal in frequency per caregiver.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear weight loss noted by caregiver.

Recurrent pharyngitis/upper respiratory infections, including a recent presumed streptococcal throat infection

drug: None known (caregiver unsure; has taken antibiotics previously without a clearly documented reaction)
