demographics.name: Daniel Wells
demographics.occupation: High school student (part-time rare coin dealer)

persona: A 17-year-old high school student who does a small, part-time rare-coin trading hustle, he keeps a stable routine on the surface but quietly runs himself down with late nights and inconsistent self-care. In conversation he’s stoic and minimizing, insisting he’s “fine” and downplaying how much the congestion, throat clearing, and poor sleep are affecting his focus, only showing flashes of irritation when pressed. He’s skeptical about how his issues affect others, yet he values community safety and tends to favor traditional treatments while also asking for a more holistic plan; when stressed, he’ll call a close friend or family member to cool off.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent nasal congestion with persistent runny nose and postnasal drip for about 9 months, worsening over the past 6 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Symptoms began as repeated "colds" with nasal stuffiness and watery discharge that were treated inconsistently; over months, congestion became more frequent and is now daily with alternating blockage that is worse at night and when lying flat. Nasal discharge has become thicker at times with occasional yellow-green mucus and a sensation of mucus draining into the throat, leading to frequent throat clearing, sore/dry throat on waking, and intermittent hoarseness. He reports reduced sleep quality due to nighttime blockage, daytime fatigue, mild headaches/pressure around the forehead, and occasional decreased sense of smell. He also notes intermittent aural fullness after prolonged congestion but no persistent hearing loss. He reports intermittent sneezing/itchy nose and watery rhinorrhea with dust exposure and sometimes during spring/fall seasons; symptoms can worsen in musty rooms and with strong odors/fragrances or smoke. Tried oral antihistamines intermittently with partial relief of sneezing/watery discharge but minimal improvement in nasal obstruction; tried oral decongestants with only temporary relief and congestion recurred. No fever, no significant facial/tooth pain, no severe unilateral pain, no epistaxis, no weight loss, no neck mass, no wheezing or shortness of breath, and no acute severe eye pain or vision changes.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals; tends to skip breakfast and often relies on convenience foods. Limited intake of fresh fruits/vegetables recently.
current_general_status.sleep: Typically sleeps less than 6 hours per night; frequent awakenings due to nasal blockage.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements; no chronic diarrhea or constipation.
current_general_status.weight_change: Mild unintentional weight loss of about 2 kg over the past 3 months.

previous_diseases: Recurrent episodes of acute rhinitis/upper respiratory infections over the past 1-2 years
Hypothyroidism diagnosed in early adolescence, intermittently adherent with treatment
medical_history.long_term_medication: Levothyroxine prescribed but taken inconsistently; uses over-the-counter intranasal decongestant sprays episodically during flares; occasional oral antihistamine without sustained benefit.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date for age per caregiver report; no recent travel vaccines documented.

personal_history.smoking: Denies cigarette smoking or vaping.
personal_history.alcohol: Heavy alcohol use for age: binge drinking on weekends for the past year; last use within the past week.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Not applicable (male).
personal_history.environmental_exposures_and_triggers: Symptoms worse at night and on waking; reports intermittent seasonal worsening (spring/fall). Nasal symptoms flare with dust exposure (cleaning/old books), musty environments suggestive of mold, and strong odors/fragrances/smoke; no regular use of nasal irritant sprays. Lives with no pets at home; occasional exposure to friends' cats/dogs can trigger sneezing/watery rhinorrhea. School environment includes chalk/dust exposure; symptoms can worsen in classrooms with poor ventilation.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother with hypothyroidism; otherwise none known.

persona: She is a 13-year-old middle school student from a lower-income family who tries to stay organized and realistic, but lately feels desperate when health problems disrupt school and sleep. She speaks in a direct, factual way and prefers step-by-step explanations, answering questions clearly in simple terms without guessing. She keeps a private journal to calm down, tries to make her own unconventional food choices when she can, and doesn’t drink or vape. She cares a lot about being inclusive and is especially vocal about safety rules, often pushing for clearer standards and accountability.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden painful swelling and redness of the outer upper left eyelid with tearing and yellow discharge starting about 2 days ago, with fever since yesterday.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Symptoms began abruptly with localized tenderness over the superolateral left orbit and a sense of pressure when blinking, followed within hours by increasing eyelid edema and erythema with an S-shaped contour of the upper lid. She reports tearing, gritty irritation, and thick yellow discharge that is worse in the morning; the tearing is likely reflex tearing from surface irritation/inflammation despite reduced baseline tear function measures during acute inflammation. The eye feels mildly sore with upgaze and she notices mild double vision when looking up and outward, felt to be a mild mechanical effect from upper lid/preseptal edema rather than definite orbital involvement. Accompanied by low-grade fever, headache, fatigue, and a tender node in front of the left ear. No significant vision loss, no photophobia, no trauma, no foreign body exposure, no contact lens use, no severe pain with eye movement, no progressive proptosis noticed by family, no vesicular rash, and no cough or sore throat at present; however, if worsening pain with eye movement, decreased vision, increasing proptosis, or worsening diplopia occurs, urgent reassessment and consideration of repeat imaging are advised despite prior CT showing no postseptal disease.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite reduced over the last 2 days; dietary preference kept private by family.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep due to eye pain and feverish feeling.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination; no diarrhea or constipation.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear recent weight change.

persona: She is a 55-year-old mission analyst who lives with her spouse and keeps a careful eye on spending, preferring straightforward essentials over extras. During medical visits she gets visibly anxious and, with limited English, tends to describe symptoms as feeling "weird" or "off" and mixes up timelines, so she often needs gentle, repeated questions to clarify details. She leans toward natural, low-additive choices and is inclusive in how she talks about family and coworkers, but lately she is preoccupied with staying steady on her feet and preventing falls at home. When she is stressed, she calms herself by knitting.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of severe spinning vertigo with nausea and vomiting starting about 18 hours ago after recovering from a flu-like illness a week ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She describes abrupt, room-spinning vertigo that is worse with head movement and makes it difficult to stand or walk without support. Over the first several hours she developed marked nausea with multiple episodes of non-bloody vomiting and a sense of imbalance; she reports new muffled hearing and intermittent ringing in the left ear along with a deep, dull ear discomfort. She notes brief spontaneous eye "jumping" sensations when trying to focus. She feels fatigued but denies high fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, fainting, new limb weakness/numbness, slurred speech, facial droop, double vision, chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. No recent head trauma, no sudden worst headache, and no persistent focal neurologic deficits. Ear findings consistent with acute otitis media were present on the left: otoscopy showed an erythematous, bulging tympanic membrane with decreased mobility and visible middle-ear effusion; mild tenderness/fullness was noted around the left ear without clear post-auricular swelling. A middle-ear specimen was obtained under sterile technique via tympanocentesis (not an external canal swab); if otorrhea was present, it was collected directly from the middle ear through the perforation/tympanostomy site rather than from the canal.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased intake due to nausea; usually ingredient-conscious and reads food labels; has tolerated small sips of water and bland foods today.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep overnight due to vertigo and nausea.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination slightly decreased today; no diarrhea; no blood in stool.

persona: He is a 60-year-old retired man who used to work as a hotline operator, quietly proud of having rebuilt stability after a period of homelessness and now protective of his privacy. He speaks in a stoic, minimizing way, insisting he is “fine” and downplaying discomfort, and he only came in because a family member pressed the issue. Though he tries to approach problems with a design-thinking mindset, he feels torn between wanting clear answers and wanting to keep details to himself, and he can struggle with complex instructions, relying on his Android phone and rideshare apps to manage daily logistics.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of high fever with chills and painful, frequent urination starting 2 days ago after heavy physical exertion at home.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Two days ago, after unusually strenuous physical activity at home with poor hydration, he developed abrupt chills followed by fever with marked fatigue and body aches. Within hours he noticed urinary frequency and urgency with burning dysuria and suprapubic discomfort, then progressively worsening perineal and low back pain and a sensation of rectal fullness/tenesmus. Over the last 24 hours, he reports weaker urinary stream with dribbling and a feeling of incomplete emptying; he has not had complete urinary retention. He notes mild nausea but no persistent vomiting. He reports a recent bladder infection treated during a hospitalization and was told he had an active systemic infection at discharge; urinary symptoms improved briefly but returned and rapidly worsened. He denies flank colicky pain, visible blood in the urine, new cough or shortness of breath, chest pain, rash, or recent diarrhea. No known recent urethral instrumentation or catheter placement since discharge. No confusion or fainting, though he feels lightheaded during fevers.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite since symptom onset; trying to follow carbohydrate-aware meals for diabetes but intake has been inconsistent due to illness.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep due to fever, chills, and nocturia.
current_general_status.excretion: Frequent small-volume urination with urgency and dysuria; occasional dribbling and incomplete emptying sensation. Bowel movements slightly reduced; rectal fullness sensation noted.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear recent weight change; reports possible mild short-term decrease in intake over the last 2 days.

previous_diseases: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Recurrent cystitis
Recent systemic bacterial infection requiring hospitalization (discharged within the past 2 weeks)
medical_history.long_term_medication: Metformin and a basal insulin regimen (patient reports using a data-driven approach with glucose checks but is inconsistent with medication timing); daily multivitamin. Discharge medications included an oral antibiotic course for urinary infection, but adherence is uncertain due to difficulty remembering instructions.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None significant. No known recent urologic procedures.
infectious_diseases: Recent urinary tract infection with bladder involvement
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccinations per patient report; last influenza vaccine this season; uncertain about pneumococcal booster timing.

personal_history.smoking: Former smoker; quit about 10 years ago after approximately 20 years of smoking. No current tobacco use.
personal_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol use; reports a few drinks on weekends, none since symptoms began.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Not applicable (male).

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father had type 2 diabetes; otherwise none known.

persona: She is a 48-year-old woman who balances a shared household and is currently writing a thesis while doing law research and studying part-time. She is ambitious and tries to stay focused on healthy living, leaning keto and making climate-conscious choices like planning groceries and reading labels. She sleeps under six hours most nights and feels increasingly worn down when her cramps flare, which makes her feel a bit threatened and on edge. In visits she is direct and factual, describing the pain in simple terms and often referencing hospital website articles she has read.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent pelvic pain and worsening menstrual cramps over the past 8 months, prompting a telehealth visit after another painful cycle last week.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports long-standing menstrual cramps since adolescence but notes a clear increase in severity and a more localized, sometimes one-sided lower abdominal/pelvic ache over the last 8 months. Pain is most prominent in the days leading up to and during menses, occasionally lingering as a dull pelvic pressure afterward; improved somewhat with NSAIDs and heat but increasingly interferes with sleep and daily activities. Pattern raises concern for pain related to the rudimentary horn (e.g., cyclic irritation/possible occult obstruction or associated endometriosis) despite no imaging-confirmed hematometra. Associated symptoms include intermittent bloating and mild nausea during severe episodes. Denies fever, chills, abnormal vaginal discharge, dysuria, flank pain, gross hematuria, sudden sharp generalized abdominal pain, syncope, or unintentional weight loss. No postmenopausal bleeding; cycles remain present though slightly more irregular over the past year consistent with perimenopause.

current_general_status.diet: Generally balanced diet; recently focusing on heart-healthy grocery shopping and label reading.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep, worse during painful menstrual days; sensitive to environmental noise.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel movements overall; occasional constipation around menses. Urination without burning or urgency.
current_general_status.weight_change: Stable weight over the past year.

previous_diseases: Congenital uterine (Mullerian duct) malformation identified on prior pelvic imaging during infertility evaluation
Intermittent dysmenorrhea since menarche
Mild essential hypertension
medical_history.long_term_medication: Amlodipine 5 mg daily; uses ibuprofen or naproxen as needed for menstrual pain.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccines per patient; annual influenza vaccination most years.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker.
personal_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol, about 1 to 2 drinks per week.
personal_history.obstetric_history: History of difficulty conceiving; one early miscarriage in her 30s; no living children. No history of ectopic pregnancy reported.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Menarche at 13. Historically regular cycles with moderate flow; cramps since adolescence. Over the past year, cycles have become somewhat irregular with occasional skipped months; no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father with coronary artery disease; mother with hypertension. No known hereditary kidney disease.

persona: She is a 61-year-old woman living on a tight budget who gets around by bus or walking and tends to be careful about safety. She speaks in a direct, matter-of-fact way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, but she can seem a bit emotionally checked out, especially when topics feel personal. She values tradition and often mentions what her parents or grandparents did, and she drinks tea throughout the day. When discussing treatment, she asks about alternatives and reads labels closely, though she sometimes mixes up similar medication names and prefers clear, written instructions.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent vulvar itching with increased watery vaginal discharge and burning with urination for about 6 months, worse again over the past 2 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months she has had intermittent episodes of intense vulvar itching described as a crawling/irritated sensation, along with vaginal dryness and a burning/stinging discomfort during daily activities. Discharge has become more noticeable on underwear, often thin and watery and occasionally yellow-tinged with mild odor; in the past 2 weeks it has been more frequent and bothersome. She reports dysuria with urethral stinging and a sense of urinary urgency soon after voiding, with increased daytime frequency but no flank pain. Intercourse has become uncomfortable with reduced lubrication, and she has noted slight spotting after friction on a few occasions. Symptoms tend to recur after she stops using over-the-counter soothing creams and when hygiene is difficult during long days away from home. No fever or chills, no lower abdominal/pelvic mass sensation, no nausea/vomiting, no gross hematuria, no new back pain, and no vesicular rash or painful genital ulcers.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite fair; tends to snack irregularly during the day and eats simple home meals.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep due to itching and nighttime urinary frequency.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination more frequent with occasional post-void urgency; bowel movements regular without blood.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change reported in the past 6 months.

previous_diseases: Cervical cancer treated with pelvic radiotherapy in her early 50s (in remission per patient report)
Postpartum depression after her second pregnancy, resolved with counseling
Occasional uncomplicated urinary tract infections in the past
medical_history.long_term_medication: None daily; uses occasional over-the-counter vaginal moisturizer and topical anti-itch cream without lasting relief.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No major surgeries; no pelvic surgery reported.
infectious_diseases: No history of sexually transmitted infections reported
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccines per patient; influenza vaccine most years.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare alcohol use (a few times per year).
personal_history.obstetric_history: G3P3; all full-term vaginal deliveries. Breastfed each child, including one period of prolonged lactation lasting about 10 months. Natural menopause at around age 50.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Natural menopause around age 50; no menses since then. No hormone therapy currently.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother had type 2 diabetes; otherwise none known.

persona: He is a 64-year-old man who used to work as a cartographer and now lives on a tight budget while looking for steady work, leaning on close friendships for support. He speaks directly and in plain language, answering questions clearly without drifting into medical jargon, but he can be sensitive and a bit reactive when he feels worried or rushed. Curious and somewhat idealistic, he wants to understand what is going on and follow the plan, including his preferred gluten-free choices and other diet limits, though money and transportation are hard with his sore knee. He co-parents in a cooperative, practical way and mentions having sensitive skin.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent bright red blood on toilet paper and a persistent urge to defecate with incomplete evacuation for about 4 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 4 months, he has noticed episodic small-volume bright red blood streaking the stool or on wiping, occurring a few times per week, often after straining. He also reports intermittent tenesmus with a sense of incomplete emptying and increased passage of mucus mixed with stool, gradually becoming more frequent over the last month. He denies black tarry stools, large-volume bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fevers, night sweats, or recent travel. No chest pain, syncope, or shortness of breath. He has not noticed a persistent painful anal lump, and he reports no continuous perianal drainage. He pursued evaluation because he is motivated by preventive screening and is concerned due to a family history of similar bowel growths and colorectal cancer in relatives.

current_general_status.diet: High-salt, high-fat pattern with frequent processed and fried foods; attempting diabetes-conscious carbohydrate counting but struggles with consistency due to finances.
current_general_status.sleep: Light and fragmented; often awakened by worry about finances and job searching, improved somewhat with nightly meditation.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements 1 to 3 times daily; occasional straining; intermittent mucus and small amounts of bright red blood; no persistent watery diarrhea; no urinary complaints.
current_general_status.weight_change: No major change; reports gradual weight gain over years.

previous_diseases: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Hypertension
Hyperlipidemia
Recurrent infectious gastroenteritis episodes over many years, sometimes treated with short antibiotic courses
medical_history.long_term_medication: Metformin; basal insulin used intermittently when sugars run high; lisinopril; atorvastatin; occasional over-the-counter antidiarrheals during gastroenteritis episodes.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Remote right knee injury with chronic pain and reduced mobility; no major abdominal surgeries.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult immunizations per patient report; receives annual influenza vaccine inconsistently.

personal_history.smoking: Former smoker; quit about 12 years ago after roughly 20 pack-years.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare alcohol use, about 1 to 2 drinks per month.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.similar_illness: Father had colorectal cancer diagnosed in his early 70s; an older brother had multiple colon polyps removed on screening colonoscopy.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Type 2 diabetes and hypertension in multiple first-degree relatives.

demographics.name: Daniel Garza
demographics.occupation: Self-employed musician and part-time art-history tutor

persona: She is a 34-year-old pregnant woman who keeps her life small-scale and family-centered, drawing comfort from tradition and stories about her ancestors. She speaks in a direct, factual way and prefers simple written instructions because anxiety makes it hard for her to hold onto details when she is stressed. She uses she/her pronouns and is a woman; "Daniel Garza" is her legal name. Since feeling sick, she clearly reports fever, thirst, a dry mouth, lightheadedness, and less peeing, and she is straightforward about what she has and has not been able to eat or drink. She tries to stay steady by following basic fitness guidance and making careful, socially conscious decisions.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of high fever with dehydration starting about 12 hours ago; also presenting today after a prenatal ultrasound raised concern for a major fetal brain and facial developmental abnormality identified within the last week (neurology consulted for counseling and coordination regarding fetal brain anomaly imaging/MRI results and specialty referrals).
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 12 hours she developed fever with chills, marked thirst, dry mouth, lightheadedness (worse with standing), and reduced urine output, prompting ED arrival by hospital shuttle. She reports a recorded Tmax at home of ~102.4 F (39.1 C) and poor oral intake with difficulty keeping up with fluids, though she has had only mild nausea without persistent vomiting. No known sick contacts reported. She also describes significant anxiety and difficulty coping since being told at a recent prenatal visit that the fetus has a serious structural abnormality; she requests written instructions because she forgets verbal details when stressed. She notes intermittent lower abdominal cramping without a regular pattern. No vaginal bleeding, no gush of fluid, and fetal movement is present but perceived as less consistent than usual. No chest pain, no shortness of breath, no rash, no severe headache, no neck stiffness, and no focal neurologic deficits. No dysuria or flank pain, and no foul-smelling vaginal discharge. Urine sample obtained for dip/urinalysis and culture due to fever with oliguria despite lack of urinary symptoms.

current_general_status.diet: Poor oral intake since yesterday; mainly sips of water, unable to tolerate a full meal.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep for several nights due to worry; slept less than usual last night.
current_general_status.excretion: Decreased urine output over the last day (reports oliguria despite no dysuria); no diarrhea reported. Urine testing (dip/urinalysis) and culture were obtained to evaluate for occult infection/dehydration.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear recent weight change reported.

previous_diseases: Gestational diabetes in a prior pregnancy (diet-controlled)
medical_history.long_term_medication: Prenatal vitamin; occasional acetaminophen as needed. No chronic daily prescriptions.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Cesarean delivery in a prior pregnancy; no major trauma.
medical_history.vaccinations: Reports routine childhood immunizations; influenza vaccine this season; Tdap updated during a prior pregnancy.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker; recent frequent exposure to wildfire smoke in her home region.
personal_history.alcohol: None during pregnancy; rare social use prior to pregnancy.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Currently pregnant; gravida 3 with two prior term deliveries. Approximately 22 weeks gestation by last menstrual period consistent with early ultrasound dating. Adult children and limited local childcare support. Current pregnancy had prenatal imaging that detected a major fetal structural brain/facial abnormality; subsequent counseling initiated. Reports that prenatal genetic testing identified a pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant associated with fetal malformations; she is awaiting confirmatory counseling and discussion of inheritance.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Previously regular menses prior to current pregnancy; last menstrual period consistent with current gestational dating per prenatal care.

family_history.similar_illness: One prior pregnancy in her extended family reportedly ended due to a severe congenital brain malformation (details unclear); otherwise none known.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Family history notable for an inherited genetic variant identified during current prenatal testing; paternal aunt with an unspecified congenital anomaly. No other known hereditary diseases.

persona: She is a 29-year-old low-income economist who also writes a part-time column, and lately she has felt a bit overshadowed and insecure, leaning on close friendships to stay grounded. With occasional overnight deadlines/rotating late-hours work in her economist role and a first pregnancy, she tries to be heart-health-focused, keeps a strict vegan diet, and uses a medication reminder app to stay on track, though symptoms still flare when she is run down. In visits she is direct and factual, describing things in everyday terms and answering questions clearly; she reads medical journals for context but is skeptical of synthetic biology and prefers practical, proven options.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Developed worsening nasal blockage with thick nasal discharge and recurrent ear fullness with reduced hearing over the past 5 months, flaring after repeated colds.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Main symptoms include persistent nasal congestion with mucopurulent discharge, postnasal drip, and intermittent facial pressure, accompanied by recurrent episodes of ear fullness and muffled hearing, occasional tinnitus, and autophony; ear discomfort is typically a deep, dull ache rather than sharp pain. Symptoms began as repeated upper respiratory infections and never fully resolved between episodes; over the last 5 months she reports more frequent flares, especially after night shifts and when reflux symptoms worsen, with temporary partial relief from saline rinses and over-the-counter decongestants. Accompanying symptoms include throat clearing, intermittent hoarseness in the mornings, and cough at night. She denies high fever, severe acute ear pain, otorrhea, vertigo, sudden complete hearing loss, severe headache, neck stiffness, shortness of breath, or dental pain.

current_general_status.diet: Strict vegan; appetite generally preserved though reduced during flares due to nasal obstruction and reflux.
current_general_status.sleep: Night shift sleep schedule with fragmented daytime sleep; congestion worsens when lying flat.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowels regular; occasional acid regurgitation and heartburn; urination normal.
current_general_status.weight_change: Mild unintentional weight loss of about 2 kg over several months due to reduced intake during symptomatic periods.

previous_diseases: Recurrent upper respiratory tract infections since late teens
Gastroesophageal reflux symptoms for several years
medical_history.long_term_medication: Intermittent antacids or acid-suppressing therapy for reflux; occasional intranasal saline irrigation; uses over-the-counter oral decongestants or antihistamines during flares (not daily).
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No prior nasal or ear surgery; no major trauma.
infectious_diseases: Frequent viral upper respiratory infections; no known chronic viral hepatitis or tuberculosis
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date, including seasonal influenza and routine adult immunizations.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker; avoids secondhand smoke when possible.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare, avoids due to reflux and pregnancy.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Currently pregnant with first child; receiving routine prenatal care; no prior pregnancies reported.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Previously regular cycles; currently amenorrheic due to pregnancy.

family_history.similar_illness: Mother with long-standing nasal allergies and recurrent sinus congestion; no other similar conditions reported.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: None.

persona: She is a curious first-grader who loves telling imaginative stories and collecting family “folklore,” and she usually stays upbeat by finding small joys even on rough days. When asked about symptoms, she tends to say her legs or arms feel “weird” or “off” and can’t always remember if it started yesterday or last week, so her caregiver often helps fill in details. Her family is careful about costs while looking for more stable housing, and they try to be practical and reform-minded about advocating at school. They rely on a simple wearable tracker to notice activity and sleep patterns and keep meals on the lighter, lower-salt side when possible.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent joint pain with episodes of joints “slipping” and easy bruising after minor bumps for about 18 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 18 months, the child has had intermittent pain in the knees, ankles, and shoulders, with frequent complaints after playground activity and occasional episodes where a joint briefly feels out of place and then settles back with rest. Caregivers report that she is “very flexible” compared with peers and can bend fingers/elbows unusually far. She develops bruises and small hematomas after mild knocks, and minor scrapes sometimes heal slowly with thin, widened scars. Symptoms worsen after strenuous play (running, jumping, climbing) and improve with rest, gentle activity, and occasional over-the-counter analgesics. Accompanying symptoms include fatigue after activity and fear of participating in sports due to prior painful episodes; no persistent swelling, warmth, or morning stiffness lasting more than a few minutes, though swelling/effusion may be mild and intermittent after activity. No fever, no weight loss, no prolonged rash, no chronic cough, no recurrent oral ulcers, no focal neurologic deficits, and no episodes of fainting or chest pain.

current_general_status.diet: Picky eater; generally adequate intake but tends to avoid protein-rich foods; drinks water and occasional juice.
current_general_status.sleep: Light sleeper; wakes some nights due to limb discomfort after active days.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination; stools every 1 to 2 days, sometimes mildly constipated.
current_general_status.weight_change: No noticeable recent weight change; growth tracking has been steady per caregiver report.

previous_diseases: Recurrent ankle sprains associated with playground falls
Frequent bruising noted since toddler years
Occasional constipation
medical_history.long_term_medication: None. Uses acetaminophen or ibuprofen intermittently for pain; takes a children’s multivitamin inconsistently.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No surgeries. History of minor trauma from falls during play and one emergency visit for a shoulder subluxation that reduced spontaneously.
infectious_diseases: None significant beyond typical childhood viral upper respiratory infections
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per caregiver report.

personal_history.smoking: None (child; no tobacco exposure reported at home).
personal_history.alcohol: None.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Born at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery; no NICU stay; normal newborn screen per caregiver report.

family_history.similar_illness: Mother reports lifelong joint hypermobility with recurrent sprains and easy bruising; no formal diagnosis documented. No known early sudden deaths in the family.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: None known.

demographics.name: Danielle Butler
demographics.occupation: Aspiring music lawyer (currently in entry-level legal support work)

persona: He is a 32-year-old man living in Tongzhou, Beijing, getting by on a lower income while working in entry-level legal support and studying toward a future in music law through friends in the local jazz scene. He speaks with the certainty of an "internet doctor," arriving with screenshots and a firm theory of what is happening and which treatments he thinks he needs, and he can get defensive if pushed off that track. Underneath the stubborn front he is sentimental and empathetic, values harmony, and worries about reliable specialist follow-up and care coordination; he commutes by ferry when he can and prides himself on helping teams recover when things get messy.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent ring-shaped skin lesions on the backs of the hands and wrists beginning about 8 months ago, with a new flare over the past 3 weeks after repeated minor skin trauma at work.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: The patient reports intermittently appearing, slowly enlarging circular to semicircular patches and small firm bumps on the dorsal hands and wrists, with raised borders and a relatively normal-appearing center. Lesions tend to persist for weeks to a few months, partially fade, then recur in nearby areas. He notes minimal to no itching and no pain, but increasing cosmetic concern and mild anxiety about recurrence. He associates flares with local minor trauma and friction (small cuts/abrasions and pressure points), and he has noticed post-inflammatory darker marks after some areas improve. No fever, malaise, weight loss, joint swelling, oral ulcers, genital lesions, eye redness, new medications preceding onset, or known contagious exposures. No purulent drainage, no significant scaling, and no burning dermatomal pain to suggest shingles. Recent CBC showed mild leukocytosis; patient denies systemic infectious symptoms, and no local infection signs were noted—will repeat CBC to assess for transient/reactive leukocytosis and reassess if any new symptoms arise.

current_general_status.diet: Plant-based diet; quality-conscious with food choices; appetite normal.
current_general_status.sleep: Generally adequate, occasionally disrupted by stress about appearance of lesions.
current_general_status.excretion: No changes in bowel or urinary habits reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: No unintentional weight change.

previous_diseases: Intermittent hand eczema/irritant dermatitis related to frequent handwashing and friction
Myopia with low vision (uses corrective lenses)
medical_history.long_term_medication: None; uses occasional over-the-counter emollients for dry skin.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No surgeries. Recurrent minor local skin trauma on hands/wrists from daily activities and work-related friction/pressure; no major injuries.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per patient; received routine adult boosters as recommended.

personal_history.smoking: Current smoker, approximately 10 cigarettes per day for about 12 years; interested in reducing but not yet quit.
personal_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol use on weekends, typically 1-2 drinks; no heavy use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Father with type 2 diabetes; otherwise none known in first- or second-degree relatives.

demographics.name: Jordan Ward
demographics.occupation: Retired social worker and community organizer

persona: He is a 59-year-old man in Vinnytsia, Ukraine, retired after years in community-focused work, and he still likes practical, concrete tasks he can finish and track. Widowed and steady by nature, he values peace, justice, and equality, and he’s become an active voice for food allergy awareness in local schools after dealing with his own shellfish reactions. When he talks with clinicians, he’s direct and factual, using plain words and wanting clear cost estimates and no surprises on billing. Lately he’s been keeping simple notes on his energy, bruising, and how full his stomach feels, hoping for straightforward answers.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive fatigue with reduced exercise tolerance and early satiety for about 5 months, worsening over the past 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last several months, he has had increasing tiredness, generalized weakness, and intermittent lightheadedness on exertion, with a gradual decline in stamina. Over the past 3 weeks he reports more frequent easy bruising and occasional gum bleeding when brushing, along with a sense of abdominal fullness and early satiety that has become more noticeable. He notes an unintentional weight loss and reduced appetite. He also describes more frequent minor respiratory infections than usual with slower recovery. He denies acute high fever, persistent productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, severe sore throat, dysuria, or focal neurologic deficits. He denies melena or hematemesis, and denies new bone pain. No recent travel-related acute illness; no known recent tick bites. Symptoms are chronic/recurrent with waxing and waning fatigue and infections.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite decreased; tends to choose comfort foods during stress; tries to avoid known food triggers and reads labels carefully due to being allergy-aware.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleep fragmented with occasional night waking; no consistent drenching night sweats reported.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements regular; no black stools; urination normal without burning.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss over several months.

previous_diseases: Epstein–Barr virus (EBV) infection in early adulthood (infectious mononucleosis, resolved)
Immune dysfunction with recurrent sinus/bronchial infections over the past few years (not previously fully evaluated)
Chronic gastritis/GERD
medical_history.long_term_medication: Omeprazole as needed for reflux; intermittent antihistamine for seasonal allergies; no chronic anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Remote appendectomy in his 20s; no major trauma reported.
medical_history.vaccinations: Childhood vaccinations completed; receives influenza vaccine inconsistently; last tetanus booster uncertain; no recent shingles or pneumococcal vaccination documented. If feasible, administer indicated inactivated vaccines (pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19) prior to initiating cladribine; otherwise defer until hematologic/immune recovery due to likely reduced immunogenicity during active purine-analog therapy and severe leukopenia, with consideration for seasonal influenza urgency.

personal_history.smoking: Current smoker, about 1 pack per day for roughly 35 years; has attempted to cut down but has not quit.
personal_history.alcohol: Social alcohol use, typically a few drinks per week; no history of heavy use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: None.

family_history.similar_illness: Father diagnosed with hairy cell leukemia in his late 60s; treated and later died of unrelated causes. No known similar illness in siblings.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Two-generation history notable for malignancy predisposition concerns: paternal aunt with non-Hodgkin lymphoma; mother with type 2 diabetes. No known inherited syndromes formally diagnosed.

persona: She is a 43-year-old woman living on a tight budget, separated and raising her child while trying to stay grounded in her principles and commitment to community service. She used to coach basketball and still thinks in terms of teamwork and protecting the people she’s responsible for, though she admits she can get emotionally overwhelmed when stress piles up. She doesn’t own a smartphone and prefers straightforward phone calls or paper reminders, and she speaks in a direct, factual way, describing what she feels in plain language. She wants a clear explanation of what’s going on and what the next steps are.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: New onset color changes and pain in fingers triggered by cold exposure with progressive hand swelling and stiffness for 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three weeks ago, after several days of waiting outdoors for public transport in cold weather, she began having episodic finger pallor followed by bluish discoloration and then redness on rewarming, associated with numbness and burning pain. Over the next 1 to 2 weeks she developed progressive puffiness of both hands and tightness of the skin over the fingers with reduced grip strength and morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She reports aching in the wrists and small joints of the hands, intermittent muscle soreness in the forearms, and two small painful sores at the fingertips that are slow to heal. She also notes new heartburn and acid regurgitation, early satiety, and intermittent constipation. Over the past week she has noticed exertional shortness of breath when climbing stairs and mild chest tightness without syncope. No fever, no productive cough, no pleuritic chest pain, no calf swelling, no focal weakness, no facial droop, no hemoptysis, and no recent travel or known sick contacts.

current_general_status.diet: Following a structured dietary plan for health reasons but admits to frequent high-salt convenience foods due to time constraints as a single parent.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep due to hand pain and reflux symptoms; averages 5 to 6 hours per night.
current_general_status.excretion: Intermittent constipation; urination normal without dysuria or hematuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss of about 2 kg over the past month due to reduced appetite and early satiety.

previous_diseases: Gastroesophageal reflux symptoms (intermittent, previously self-treated with antacids)
Iron deficiency anemia in her 30s (treated with oral iron)
medical_history.long_term_medication: None prescribed long-term; uses over-the-counter antacids as needed and occasional ibuprofen for joint pain.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Cesarean delivery 12 years ago; no major trauma.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult vaccines per patient report; last influenza vaccine this season.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare alcohol use, about 1 to 2 drinks per month.

persona: She is a 72-year-old divorced, working-class healthcare provider who is semi-retired and in the middle of relocating to a new city, proud of having built a respected career that once included genetic testing work. She speaks with confident, evidence-focused certainty, often arriving with printouts and telling you what she believes is happening based on her own research, pushing for specific imaging and proactive pain control while brushing aside details that do not match her conclusion. Beneath the stubbornness she is nostalgic and heartfelt, trying mindfulness for stress, and she is easily irritated by mosquitoes and other small discomforts.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden severe right heel pain and inability to bear weight after a low-energy fall at home 6 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: The patient reports she tripped on a loose rug in a dim hallway at home and landed awkwardly on her right foot, immediately developing sharp, intense heel pain with rapid swelling. Over the next few hours, swelling increased with bruising around the heel and ankle, and she became unable to stand or walk on the affected side; she notes the heel looks wider than usual and her shoe feels tight. Accompanying symptoms include localized warmth and tenderness and nausea from pain. She denies head strike, loss of consciousness, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, fever, open wound, numbness or tingling in the foot, calf pain, or pain in the knee or hip. She reports no skin breakdown or blistering at presentation. She is able to wiggle her toes and notes the foot does not feel cold or numb.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite since the fall; generally low intake of calcium-rich foods and rarely uses vitamin D or calcium supplements; tends toward comfort foods when stressed.
current_general_status.sleep: Restless sleep due to pain and anxiety; awakened frequently overnight.
current_general_status.excretion: No change in urination or bowel habits; no incontinence.
current_general_status.weight_change: Long-term low body weight with gradual mild weight loss over the past year.

previous_diseases: Age-related bone density loss noted on prior screening with a history of height reduction
Gastroesophageal reflux symptoms intermittently
medical_history.long_term_medication: Occasional antacid as needed; no regular calcium or vitamin D supplementation; no chronic hormone therapy.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Remote left wrist fracture from a minor fall about 5 years ago, treated with casting; no major surgeries reported.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date with routine adult vaccines per patient report; last influenza vaccine this season.

personal_history.smoking: Never smoker.
personal_history.alcohol: Rare alcohol use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: G2P2, both term vaginal deliveries; no major obstetric complications reported.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Menopause in her early 50s with gradual postmenopausal symptoms; no postmenopausal bleeding.

family_history.similar_illness: Mother had a hip fracture after a fall in her late 70s.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: None.

persona: She is a 17-year-old high school student who tries to stay on top of school even as she gets winded easily and has days where her heart feels like it is racing. She’s dedicated and vigilant about tracking symptoms on her Fitbit and prefers privacy, keeping most details to herself except with her doctor. When she talks, she’s direct and factual, using simple words and answering questions clearly without wandering. Feeling a bit cut off from others lately, she’s been leaning on fiction books, mindfulness, and stress-management habits to cope.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive shortness of breath with exertion and intermittent palpitations worsening over the past 6 months, notably after several untreated sore-throat illnesses in the last 2 years.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months she has developed gradually worsening exertional dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, and episodes of rapid heartbeat noted on her Fitbit, now occurring with minimal activity such as climbing a flight of stairs. She reports chest tightness/pressure during exertion, fatigue that interferes with school attendance, and a persistent cough that is worse when lying flat; on a few occasions she has awakened at night short of breath. Over the last 3 weeks she has noticed intermittent ankle swelling at the end of the day and mild weight fluctuation related to fluid retention. She also describes periodic flares of migratory joint pains (knees and wrists) that come and go over days, often following sore throat episodes, and low-grade sweats. She denies fever currently, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, syncope, unilateral leg pain/redness, wheezing history, or recent travel; no productive purulent sputum and no known exposure to tuberculosis.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite mildly decreased; trying to reduce sugary drinks and portion size for weight management, but intake varies with fatigue and shortness of breath.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with occasional awakening short of breath and cough when supine; uses extra pillows.
current_general_status.excretion: No diarrhea; occasional decreased urine output on days with ankle swelling.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain over the last year with short-term up-and-down changes of a few pounds during swelling episodes.

previous_diseases: History of post-streptococcal inflammatory illness in childhood with joint pains and prior carditis suspicion (treated incompletely due to delayed care)
Recurrent episodes of migratory joint pain and feverish illness after sore throats during early adolescence
Recurrent pharyngitis with delayed or no antibiotic treatment due to access and insurance authorization barriers
medical_history.long_term_medication: Not consistently on preventive antibiotics; intermittently takes over-the-counter ibuprofen for joint pain and acetaminophen for throat discomfort. No regular cardiac medications reported.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No surgeries. History of physical assault with contusions in the past; no fractures or head injury reported.
infectious_diseases: Recurrent suspected streptococcal pharyngitis in the past several years
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per school requirements per patient report; unsure about annual influenza vaccine.

personal_history.smoking: Denies smoking or vaping; reports frequent secondhand smoke exposure in shared housing.
personal_history.alcohol: Denies alcohol use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: No pregnancies. No history of sexually transmitted infections reported.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Menarche at 12. Cycles generally regular every month; menses 4 to 6 days, moderate flow. Occasional fatigue worsens around menses.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: None known.

persona: He is an 8-year-old student who usually tries hard to do well and stay on top of things, but he second-guesses himself and gets overwhelmed when something feels "off." In the visit he talks fast and at length, jumping from the soccer-ball hit to how he now sits closer to the board, worries about "going blind," and asks repeatedly if it could be something terrible, needing frequent reassurance. His caregiver rents their home and emphasizes healthy living with a strong focus on hydration and nutrition, plus stricter limits on screens because headaches and light sensitivity seem worse after device time. The family hopes for clear coordination between school supports and any eye specialists involved.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually worsening blurred vision in the right eye with glare for about 3 months, noticed after a playground soccer ball hit to the eye about 6 months ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Since the eye injury, the child has had intermittent episodes of right-eye blurred vision that have become more frequent and more noticeable over the last 3 months, especially in bright light and when reading at school. Parent reports occasional complaints of seeing a "double" or "ghost" image with the right eye alone and needing to sit closer to the board. Associated symptoms include light sensitivity, tearing, and mild aching discomfort after prolonged screen use; occasional headaches after school. No fever, no upper respiratory symptoms, no eye discharge, no generalized redness, no recent new trauma, no neurologic deficits, and no pain with eye movements.

current_general_status.diet: Age-appropriate mixed diet; good appetite.
current_general_status.sleep: Generally sleeps 9-10 hours; sometimes delayed sleep due to evening headaches or light sensitivity.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements for age.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change noted.

previous_diseases: Right eye blunt injury from sports impact about 6 months ago (evaluated by urgent care; no fracture reported)

persona: He is a middle-class middle school student who likes to feel prepared and safe, and he gets especially focused when something seems risky or unfair. He speaks in a direct, factual way, answering questions clearly in simple terms, and he prefers getting straight to what he can do to prevent problems. He’s proud of being seen as responsible and sometimes wants recognition for doing the right thing, but he also values having time for school, friends, and downtime. He reads long articles when a topic matters, enjoys gardening to unwind, and is careful about keeping family financial details private.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Persistent nosebleed and gum bleeding that started suddenly after a minor bump during school sports about 10 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: After a mild facial bump at school, he developed a nosebleed that recurred repeatedly and was difficult to stop with usual pressure; later the same day he noticed gum bleeding while brushing teeth and new easy bruising on the arms/legs. Bleeding has been intermittent but prolonged compared with prior minor injuries. Accompanied by fatigue and mild lightheadedness; no syncope. No fever, no sore throat, no cough, no abdominal pain, no black/tarry stools, no red urine, no severe headache, no joint swelling, and no recent medication use such as aspirin or ibuprofen.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite slightly decreased since the bleeding episodes; tolerating regular meals and fluids.
current_general_status.sleep: Slept poorly due to recurrent nosebleeds overnight.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements; no visible blood reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: No recent weight change.

Easy bruising noted over the past year after minor bumps

Pattern discussed in the family as inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.

persona: She is a 32-year-old ecology professor who usually makes decisions by trying to stay relaxed and grounded, but lately she has been frightened by how quickly her right arm pain has escalated. She talks fast and at length, jumping between details about her teaching schedule, a busy household of six, and friends from an online farming community, then abruptly asking if this could be something severe and needing repeated reassurance. Although she is financially comfortable and values dignity, she feels unusually vulnerable after losing insurance and being unfamiliar with how coverage works, so she overprepares by buying supplements and peppering the visit with questions.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of right-sided outer elbow pain after an intensive racket-sport practice session 5 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Pain is localized to the outer (lateral) elbow with an aching, sometimes sharp quality, triggered by gripping and wrist extension. It began as soreness during practice but worsened over several days; she now notices pain when lifting a kettle, turning a doorknob, opening jars, and twisting a towel, with a subjective decrease in hand strength. Occasional mild warmth over the area and a faint clicking sensation with certain elbow movements are reported. She has tried resting briefly and avoiding heavy lifting with partial relief. No numbness or tingling in the hand, no neck or shoulder pain radiating down the arm, no swelling of the whole arm, no fever, no recent cough or systemic illness, and no recent direct fall onto the elbow.

current_general_status.diet: Eating regularly but choosing inexpensive options due to recent loss of insurance; appetite mildly reduced by pain-related stress.
current_general_status.sleep: Light sleep with occasional awakening when rolling onto the affected arm; no persistent insomnia.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements.
current_general_status.weight_change: No recent significant weight change.

previous_diseases: None significant; reports occasional tension-type headaches during high stress periods

persona: She is a 30-year-old self-employed design professional with significant financial comfort, proud of her independence and used to making cautious, strategic decisions after thinking things through. She tends to form deep emotional bonds and, while warm, can be hard to interview because her story comes out in broad strokes - things feel "off" or "weird," and the timeline blurs unless someone gently pins down details. As an international student, she is sensitive about language access and may ask about interpreter availability, especially when discussing fertility and intimacy; chronic short sleep and stress snacking shape her day-to-day routines, and she is bluntly anti-vegan when diet comes up.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Irregular menses with intermittent milky nipple discharge and difficulty conceiving, worsening over the past 9 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 9 months, the patient reports progressively more spaced-out menstrual periods that sometimes stop for 2-3 months at a time, along with decreased libido and vaginal dryness causing discomfort with intercourse. She has noticed intermittent milky-white discharge from both nipples when pressure is applied, despite not currently breastfeeding. She and her partner have been attempting pregnancy for about 1 year without success. She also describes recurrent dull headaches over the last 4 months and occasional brief episodes of blurred vision when reading, without eye pain. Accompanying symptoms include fatigue, stress-related increased appetite with preference for comfort foods, and mild weight gain. She denies fever, chronic cough, hot flashes, palpitations, tremor, severe sudden 'worst headache,' focal weakness, chest pain, nausea/vomiting, or new focal neurologic deficits. No breast redness, warmth, or bloody nipple discharge.

current_general_status.diet: Increased snacking and comfort-food intake during stress; otherwise mixed diet with inconsistent meal timing.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep with difficulty falling asleep during high-stress periods.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel habits; no chronic diarrhea or constipation reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual gain of about 4-5 kg over the past year.

Gastroesophageal reflux disease
Major depressive disorder with past psychotic features (treated)

Previously treated with an antipsychotic medication for several months; stopped about 10 months ago due to side effects and inconsistent follow-up. Not on hormonal contraception currently.

personal_history.obstetric_history: G2P1. One prior term vaginal delivery 4 years ago. Breastfed for about 10 months postpartum; menses resumed several months after weaning. One early miscarriage at approximately 7 weeks about 18 months ago. Currently attempting pregnancy for about 1 year.
personal_history.menstrual_history: Menarche at age 12. Previously regular cycles until the past year; now irregular with occasional 2-3 month gaps. Last menstrual period approximately 10 weeks ago.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: Mother with hypothyroidism; father with hypertension. No known familial genetic syndromes reported.

persona: The patient is a newborn infant whose caregivers live on a tight budget and pay close attention to practical safety advice they hear from official health updates. When they talk with clinicians, they are direct and factual, describing exactly when the noisy breathing happens, what makes it worse, and how feeding has slowed, without guessing at causes. They are wary of anything that sounds exaggerated or misleading, and their frustration shows after weeks of disrupted sleep, but they stay cooperative and focused on clear next steps. They are also curious about whether gentle, non-drug options like acupuncture are ever appropriate, while still wanting solid evidence first.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent high-pitched noisy breathing (worse with crying and feeding) noticed over the past 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Caregivers report intermittent inspiratory noisy breathing that began gradually in the first weeks of life and has become more noticeable as the infant is more active. Episodes worsen when supine, during crying, and after feeds; improves when calm and held upright. During more pronounced episodes, there is increased work of breathing with mild chest wall retractions. Feeding has been slower with occasional coughing/choking and pauses to breathe; caregivers note frequent spit-ups and mild fatigue during feeds. No persistent fever, no productive cough, no wheezing, no cyanosis, no apneic spells, and no hoarse cry; voice quality is otherwise normal. Wet diapers and stooling continue, though intake seems reduced during symptomatic periods.

current_general_status.diet: Primarily breast milk with occasional formula supplementation; feeds take longer with frequent pauses.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleep is fragmented; noisy breathing is more noticeable when lying on her back.
current_general_status.excretion: Urine output present with multiple wet diapers daily; stools regular for age.
current_general_status.weight_change: Caregivers perceive slower-than-expected weight gain over the past 2 weeks.

previous_diseases: None
medical_history.long_term_medication: None
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None
medical_history.vaccinations: Received routine newborn prophylaxis per caregiver report; additional immunizations not yet due for age.

personal_history.alcohol: None
personal_history.obstetric_history: Born at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery per caregiver report; birth weight appropriate for gestational age; no NICU stay; passed initial newborn screening per caregiver recall.

persona: She is a 64-year-old woman who works part-time reviewing films and hosting a small podcast, with a sharp eye for aesthetics and a habit of bookmarking clinical evidence links to back up her opinions. In conversation she is talkative but private, arriving with pages of printouts and confidently insisting she already knows the exact cause, pushing for specific medications while downplaying details that do not match her theory and becoming defensive when challenged. Separated and currently trying to secure affordable housing, she can seem haunted and focused on making amends in her life, which makes her cautious and hesitant when discussing personal history.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent burning tongue pain and soreness with a smooth, red tongue surface worsening over the past 6 weeks, on a background of intermittent episodes for about 8 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports a persistent burning and stinging sensation mainly on the tip and lateral borders of the tongue, with increased sensitivity to spicy/acidic foods and discomfort while speaking for long periods. Over recent weeks she has noticed the tongue looks redder and smoother than usual with reduced surface texture, and occasional numbness and altered taste. Symptoms fluctuate, partially improving with avoidance of irritant foods but recurring. Associated symptoms include dry mouth, mild angular cracking at the corners of the mouth, and intermittent bad taste; she also notes episodes of white, wipeable coating on the tongue after wearing dentures for long stretches. No fever, no sore throat or tonsillar pain, no oral ulcers elsewhere, no facial swelling, no rash, no recent antibiotic-associated diarrhea, and no weight loss from systemic illness.

current_general_status.diet: Selective eating with carbohydrate counting for diabetes-conscious habits; tends to restrict intake and often skips protein-rich foods; avoids acidic/spicy foods due to oral burning.
current_general_status.sleep: Light sleep with frequent awakenings due to oral discomfort and dry mouth.
current_general_status.excretion: Regular bowel movements; occasional constipation when dietary restriction increases; no melena or hematochezia reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional mild weight loss over several months attributed to reduced oral intake during flare-ups.

previous_diseases: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled per patient)
Chronic atrophic gastritis with prior evaluation for anemia
Iron deficiency anemia (recurrent)
Vitamin B12 deficiency requiring intermittent supplementation
Recurrent oral candidiasis

medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Partial gastrectomy years ago for peptic ulcer disease; no major oral trauma.
medical_history.vaccinations: Routine adult vaccinations per patient; not fully documented.

persona: He is a 38-year-old bioinformatics expert in an industrial setting in Goldendale, living on a tight budget and quietly protective of his family. He tends to be sensitive and comfort-seeking, but makes decisions in a stealthy, low-profile way, especially when paperwork or finances are involved. In conversation he is a poor historian: he describes symptoms as "weird" or "off," gestures broadly to his upper belly, and gives a muddled timeline unless guided with specific questions. He reads best in bullet points and unwinds with world literature, while worrying about medical identity theft and insurance fraud.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of right upper abdominal discomfort with yellowing of the eyes starting 3 days ago after a weekend of heavy binge drinking.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three days ago, after several days of markedly increased alcohol intake, he developed progressive fatigue, poor appetite, nausea, and a constant dull ache/fullness in the right upper quadrant. Over the next 48 hours he noticed dark urine, pale stools, generalized itching, and yellowing of the sclera, with new bilateral ankle swelling by day 3. He reports intermittent low-grade chills without a documented high fever. He denies vomiting blood, black tarry stools, severe sharp chest pain at rest, shortness of breath, syncope, focal weakness, confusion severe enough to impair orientation, or recent acetaminophen overuse. No recent travel-related diarrheal illness, no cough or pleuritic pain, and no known exposure to toxic mushrooms or industrial solvents.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite for several months; often skips meals and relies on inexpensive, low-protein processed foods; limited intake of fruits/vegetables.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with early awakening; worse since symptoms began.
current_general_status.excretion: Loose stools intermittently; reports pale stools over the last 2 days; urine dark tea-colored; no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain over years with central obesity; no intentional recent weight loss.

Obesity
Intermittent gastroesophageal reflux symptoms

medical_history.blood_transfusion: Received an emergency blood transfusion in his early 20s during conflict-related injury (records unavailable).

Reports facial flushing and rapid warmth/palpitations after small amounts of alcohol consistent with alcohol flushing, yet continues drinking; intake has escalated recently. Last binge occurred 3–4 days ago.

persona: He is a 52-year-old district counselor who lives with his spouse and three children, and he tries to stay grounded in charity and respect for his ancestors even when money and access to services are tight. He comes across calm but a little unsettlingly matter-of-fact, answering questions directly in plain words and sticking to what he can clearly describe, and he often checks his Garmin watch to track his energy on days he feels worn down. Past military combat experiences made him wary of invasive care, yet he still finds small comfort and joy in routine and serving his community.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Over the past 8 months, noticed gradually darkening, symmetric patches on the cheeks and forehead along with worsening fatigue and intermittent abdominal bloating.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient reports a chronic, slowly progressive change in facial skin tone with dullness and symmetric dark brown to gray-brown patches most prominent on the malar areas and forehead, which tend to deepen after prolonged outdoor exposure. Over the same period he has developed increasing fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, poor appetite, early satiety, and intermittent abdominal distension, with occasional nausea. In the last 2 months he has noticed easy bruising and scattered small purplish spots on the forearms after minor bumps, and episodic gum bleeding when brushing. He also notes occasional itching and intermittent yellowing of the eyes noticed by coworkers, plus a few new small red 'spider-like' marks on the upper chest and redness of the palms. He denies fever, acute right upper quadrant colicky pain, vomiting blood, black tarry stools, confusion, fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, severe generalized rash, focal neurologic deficits, or sudden one-sided facial weakness.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite; tends to eat smaller portions due to early fullness; avoids heavy/fatty meals because they worsen bloating.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep with occasional nighttime itching; no clear day-night reversal reported.
current_general_status.excretion: Intermittent constipation alternating with normal stools; urine sometimes darker than usual; no persistent diarrhea reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional mild weight loss over several months, though abdominal girth intermittently increases with bloating.

Iron metabolism disorder with intermittently elevated ferritin and prior evaluation for iron overload

persona: He is a 6-year-old boy in Clarksville, Tennessee, living with family while they are under financial strain and actively looking for more affordable housing. When he feels unwell he becomes distractible and irritable, and he explains things in a vague, jumbled way, saying his stomach feels "weird" or "off" while gesturing broadly instead of pinpointing where it hurts. His caregiver tries to be careful and anti-misinformation minded, asking for clear guidance and wanting to follow public-safety rules, but they sometimes mix up similar medicine names and dosing when he starts to seem better.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of abdominal pain with diarrhea and vomiting starting 4 days ago, followed by fever for 2 days after returning from travel to Delhi, India about 10 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Approximately 4 days ago, the child developed sudden crampy periumbilical abdominal pain with watery diarrhea and multiple episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting; these gastrointestinal symptoms partially eased after about 2 days, but he then developed intermittent high fevers with chills and marked fatigue. Caregiver reports reduced appetite, mild headache, and increased irritability with slower responses than usual. No cough, runny nose, sore throat, shortness of breath, dysuria, ear pain, or neck stiffness. No rash noticed yet. No visible (frank) blood in stool was observed, though stool testing showed minimal microscopic RBCs that could reflect mild irritation or sampling effects. No recent animal bites. Exposure history notable for close contact with a neighbor adult who had a recent prolonged febrile diarrheal illness and later was reported to be a stool carrier; family also ate street food during travel. Symptoms are new with no prior similar episodes, and there is no history of previous similar infection.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite; tolerating small sips of oral fluids and small meals; prefers meat-heavy foods but currently eating less than usual.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleep fragmented due to fever and abdominal discomfort; caregiver occasionally gives an over-the-counter sleep aid on particularly restless nights.
current_general_status.excretion: Loose stools several times daily earlier in the course, now less frequent; urine output slightly decreased but still present; no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Possible mild weight loss over the past week due to reduced intake.

previous_diseases: Recurrent viral upper respiratory infections in early childhood
Atopic dermatitis (mild, intermittent)

medical_history.vaccinations: Routine childhood immunizations partially complete; has not received typhoid-related vaccination and missed some scheduled doses due to family travel.

family_history.similar_illness: Recent close contact with a neighbor reported to be a stool carrier after a febrile diarrheal illness; no known similar serious illnesses in first-degree relatives.
