persona: He is a newborn boy whose single parent is trying hard to keep the household steady and calm, leaning on a close support network and valuing harmony at home. The caregiver speaks in a direct, factual way and sticks to simple descriptions, saying he "breathes fast," "sweats while eating," and "gets tired" during feeds, without using medical jargon. They’ve been choosing generic options at the pharmacy and following the antibiotic plan carefully, but still feel guilty that he seems to struggle and isn’t gaining weight. They’re also seeking a second opinion and working on better sleep routines despite feeling pulled by ongoing school responsibilities.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive feeding difficulty with fast breathing and sweating during feeds since the first week of life, worsening over the past 3 days.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Since birth the infant has had tiring with feeds, needing frequent pauses and taking smaller volumes, with tachypnea and diaphoresis during feeding. Over the past several days, caregivers note increased work of breathing, more frequent spit-ups, and decreased intake with fewer wet diapers, along with poor weight gain compared with birth expectations. He has had recurrent nasal congestion and mild cough episodes without high fever, and becomes more breathless when crying. No episodes of turning blue with feeds, no witnessed apnea, no seizures, no projectile vomiting, and no known sick contacts.

He also reports decreased libido for about 1 year and intermittent erectile difficulty, with reduced morning erections. He denies nipple discharge but notes occasional breast fullness/tenderness. He reports fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. Accompanying symptoms include intermittent constipation and generalized muscle weakness. persona: He is a 42-year-old central bank official who keeps a structured routine and tends to be direct and factual when describing what he feels, using plain, everyday language. He is single and fosters one child, which makes him attentive and inclusive, but also a bit anxious and expectant when health issues linger, especially with limited access to consistent dental care. He dislikes sports and usually runs on less than six hours of sleep, so work stress can pile up; he calms down by calling a trusted friend or family member. In busy, noisy places he sometimes struggles to hear clearly, and he is motivated to understand exactly what the problem is and what the plan will be.

Over months, he noticed episodic gum swelling near the tooth that sometimes drains a small amount of pus and then improves, but recurs every few weeks. In the past 3 weeks, occlusal discomfort has become more consistent, and he reports a dull pressure sensation with intermittent halitosis. He also notes the affected tooth looks slightly darker than adjacent teeth and feels 'different' when biting, though not loose. No fever, no facial swelling extending to the eye/neck, no trismus, no difficulty swallowing, no numbness, and no sharp electric-like facial pain.

persona: He is a 66-year-old man from Hau Giang, Vietnam, widowed and widely respected in his community, who used to work in a local industry and is trying to return part-time despite feeling increasingly run-down. He gets overwhelmed easily and struggles with phones or online portals due to limited access, so he tends to describe symptoms as "weird" or "off" and mixes up when things started, often gesturing to broad areas rather than pinpointing details. Reform-minded and careful with money, he still likes to research big purchases in his own way, and he has been motivated to manage his weight for health while staying cautious about cleanliness and infection exposure.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion with recurrent mouth sores and easy bruising over the past 4 months, noticed gradually while trying to increase activity.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 4 months, he reports worsening low energy and generalized weakness, now needing to rest after climbing one flight of stairs and feeling lightheaded when standing quickly. Over the same period he has had intermittent painful oral ulcers and two episodes of low-grade fever that resolved after outpatient evaluation, along with more frequent bruising on the arms and legs and occasional gum bleeding with tooth brushing. He also notes intermittent left upper abdominal fullness; on exam the spleen is palpated approximately 2 cm below the left costal margin. He denies chest pain, syncope, hemoptysis, melena, hematemesis, focal neurologic deficits, dysuria, or persistent cough; no recent travel-related diarrheal illness; no known new medication exposures. He is particularly worried about infection exposure and hygiene standards due to prior hospitalizations.

persona: She is a 72-year-old woman in London who lives on a tight budget and tries hard to keep her routines steady, often focusing on small, practical steps to stay well. She is divorced, values charity and giving, and used to work in local government in a policy-focused role, but she now avoids anything that feels overly technical and rarely uses online patient portals. When she talks with clinicians she is direct and factual, using simple language and answering questions clearly, though she can feel misunderstood and wants reassurance that she is being taken seriously. She prefers non-prescription or alternative options for aches when she can.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Developed sudden chest tightness and palpitations with shortness of breath 2 days ago, occurring about 1 week after a severe flu-like illness began and shortly after the fever started to improve.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: About 9 days ago she developed abrupt high fever with chills, severe fatigue, headache, diffuse muscle aches, sore throat, and a dry cough after likely community exposure in London. She describes the initial illness as unusually severe for her, with poor oral intake and spending most of the day in bed. After 5 to 6 days, the fever and throat symptoms partially improved, but she remained markedly weak. Two days ago she began noticing intermittent chest tightness and a pounding, irregular heartbeat, initially with exertion but now sometimes at rest, along with shortness of breath when walking indoors and needing extra pillows to sleep comfortably. She also reports new lightheadedness, reduced exercise tolerance, and profound fatigue out of proportion to the residual cough. She denies coughing up blood, unilateral leg swelling or calf pain, a ripping back pain, new focal weakness or facial droop, black or bloody stools, or a new widespread rash. No known recent recreational drug use. She reports no prior similar episodes.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite since the febrile illness; currently eating small, irregular meals with limited protein and fluids due to nausea and low energy; acknowledges poor diet planning and recent malnutrition.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep due to anxiety and rumination about health; wakes with awareness of heartbeat and occasional breathlessness.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination slightly decreased with darker urine during the febrile period; bowel movements less frequent than usual over the past week, likely from reduced intake.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss over the past few weeks, worsened during the recent illness.

persona: She is a 57-year-old married, self-employed user experience designer who still tries to stay active around the barn with a prosthetic limb, though lately she paces herself and worries she is “pushing her heart too hard.” She talks fast and at length, interrupting with fears about dying suddenly and adding side details about her weekend routines, what she read online, and how her swelling seems to change with meals and sleep. Even while anxious, she is principled and values justice and forgiveness, and she asks for clear, guideline-based explanations while also wanting a holistic, neurobiology-informed view. With limited tech access she still uses telehealth when she can, admits fast-food habits, and needs frequent reassurance to stay consistent with medications.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressively worsening shortness of breath with exertion and increasing ankle swelling over the past 8 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 8 months she has noticed reduced exercise tolerance with dyspnea when climbing stairs and while doing barn work, initially intermittent but now occurring with mild exertion. She reports increasing fatigue, abdominal fullness/bloating after meals, and gradual bilateral lower-extremity swelling that is worse by evening and improves somewhat overnight; shoes feel tighter and she has had several episodes of sudden 2–3 day weight increases consistent with fluid retention. She also describes intermittent palpitations and a sense of chest heaviness during exertion without sharp pleuritic pain. Over the last 6 weeks she has had a dry cough that is worse when lying flat, which may relate to overall volume overload and possible pleural effusions in advanced right-sided heart failure; she denies fever, chills, sputum production, wheezing, or hemoptysis. She denies unilateral leg pain/redness, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, black stools, or urinary burning; no acute tearing back pain.

current_general_status.diet: Trying to maintain a balanced, health-focused diet; recently reduced appetite and early satiety due to abdominal bloating; attempts to limit salty foods.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep; needs 1–2 pillows and occasionally wakes short of breath when lying flat.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements less frequent when bloated; urination normal but notes decreased daytime urine output when swelling is worse.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain over months with intermittent rapid increases over a few days consistent with fluid retention.

persona: She is a 55-year-old renter who works as a paper mill technician and tends to handle decisions best in person, valuing community safety and strict privacy. Quiet but generally cooperative, she also has an "Internet doctor" streak: she confidently insists this is a femoral nerve problem from the pelvic surgery and presses for a specific scan and a clear treatment plan, brushing aside details that do not match her theory. Despite her stubborn certainty, she is approval-seeking and polite, asking if she is "doing recovery right" and focusing on fall prevention after her knee buckling scares her. Work monotony has been wearing on her, so she channels her side interest in content strategy and SEO into researching health tips.

present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Immediately upon first attempting to stand after the operation, she noticed the left knee would buckle and she could not actively extend it, making walking unstable and stairs very difficult. She reports aching pain and tenderness in the left groin that radiates down the medial thigh toward the medial calf, along with numbness and tingling over the anterior thigh and medial leg. Symptoms have remained persistent with minimal improvement; she has been using support to ambulate and is afraid of falling. She notes reduced confidence in the left leg when rising from a chair. No low back pain, no saddle anesthesia, no bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, no fever or wound drainage, no calf swelling or redness, and no new facial droop, speech difficulty, or arm weakness.

persona: She is a 21-year-old woman juggling graduate training in autism research while helping care for an elderly parent, stretching a low-to-middle income budget with self-pay coverage and constant worries about job security. Guided by faith-based routines, she keeps her circle small and makes decisions by quietly checking in with the people she trusts most. In clinic she comes off tough and understated, insisting she is "fine" and brushing off symptoms so she does not burden anyone, even when she is clearly stressed, anxious, and overwhelmed. She speaks briefly, avoids drama, and only shows up because family pushed her to.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive, symmetric proximal leg weakness consistent with later-onset spinal muscular atrophy (Type III/IV), with worsening difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair (more noticeable over the past 3 years).
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She reports slowly progressive proximal weakness consistent with later-onset SMA (Type III/IV). In retrospect, she first noted mild difficulty with running/climbing and getting up from deep squats in early adolescence, but symptoms became functionally limiting and more clearly progressive over the past 3 years, beginning in the thighs/hips and later involving the shoulders/upper arms. She has frequent fatigue on exertion and occasional hand tremor. Over time she has needed more rest breaks, has more difficulty carrying groceries, squatting, and walking long distances, and has had several low-impact falls without head injury. She notes intermittent muscle twitching in the thighs and occasional tongue quivering when tired. Respiratory baseline: no chronic daytime dyspnea at rest; intermittent orthopnea when lying flat for prolonged periods and mild shortness of breath mainly during upper respiratory infections. She is able to speak in full sentences; no baseline cyanosis reported; cough subjectively somewhat weak when ill but generally effective at baseline. Accompanying symptoms include intermittent back discomfort and stiffness, especially after prolonged sitting. She denies sensory loss, numbness, tingling, bowel or bladder incontinence, fever, acute severe back pain, vision changes, diplopia, dysphagia for liquids, new rash, joint swelling, or fluctuating weakness that improves with rest.

current_general_status.diet: Regular home-cooked diet; interested in learning diabetes-oriented meal planning due to family concerns, but often skips breakfast when busy.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep with difficulty finding a comfortable position; averages 6 to 7 hours nightly.

persona: She is a 53-year-old woman working irregular hours and scraping by, with a strong eye for beauty and a long-term goal of writing a book. She talks in a direct, matter-of-fact way, and at first can sound resentful about how long the congestion and poor sleep have dragged on, but she becomes collaborative once a plan is clear and practical. She likes decisions that feel evidence-based and trackable, keeps up strict hand hygiene, and drives herself to appointments. She admits she is forgetful and sometimes misses daily meds.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually worsening persistent nasal blockage with mouth breathing for about 2 years, worse over the past 3 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports chronic, mostly constant nasal obstruction that is often bilateral and does not reliably alternate sides; initially intermittent during allergy seasons but became persistent over the past 2 years. Over the last 3 months she notes increased nighttime congestion with snoring and waking with dry mouth, leading to non-restorative sleep and daytime fatigue. Associated symptoms include thick mucoid nasal discharge with intermittent episodes of mucopurulent drainage during infectious flares, post-nasal drip with throat irritation and an intermittent dry cough, reduced sense of smell, hyponasal speech, and intermittent frontal facial pressure/headache. She also describes intermittent ear fullness and ringing, especially when congestion is severe, and occasional watery eyes. Denies fever, acute severe facial pain, dental pain, significant epistaxis, wheezing, chest pain, shortness of breath, unintended weight loss, or recent foreign body exposure. No recent sick contacts; symptoms are recurrent and chronic rather than sudden in onset.

current_general_status.diet: Ingredient-conscious and reads food labels; appetite generally preserved but prefers softer foods when throat irritation is worse.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented due to nasal blockage and mouth breathing; wakes unrefreshed.
current_general_status.excretion: No notable change reported; regular bowel and urinary habits.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant recent weight change.

Allergic rhinitis (worse with dust and seasonal pollen exposure)
Nasal septal deviation diagnosed previously after evaluation for chronic obstruction
Chronic suppurative sinusitis with recurrent purulent nasal drainage episodes
Type 2 diabetes mellitus with intermittent poor control
medical_history.long_term_medication: Uses intranasal corticosteroid spray and oral antihistamines intermittently rather than daily as prescribed; saline rinses used inconsistently. Takes metformin for diabetes but reports missed doses at times.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No prior nasal surgery. Remote minor facial trauma without known fracture.
infectious_diseases: Recurrent upper respiratory infections in winter months
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on routine adult immunizations per patient; receives influenza vaccine intermittently.

persona: The patient is an 11-year-old student who tends to avoid competition and gets easily stressed when feeling unwell, becoming a bit withdrawn and vulnerable around unfamiliar adults. When asked about symptoms, he speaks in vague, messy timelines, saying his neck feels "weird" or "off" and gesturing broadly rather than pinpointing exactly where it hurts, so his caregiver often steps in to clarify. His family lives on a tight budget with limited insurance, and they lean on community support and giving back through their church as he prepares for baptism. A language barrier sometimes makes visits feel rushed, but he stays interested in how spaces look and often talks about fixing up rooms and making things nicer.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent right-sided neck swelling over ~3 months with an acute painful flare over the past 4 days following URI symptoms (sore throat/runny nose) and recent cold exposure.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last ~3 months, the patient has had several episodes of a tender lump along the right upper neck that enlarges during colds/URIs and partially improves but does not completely resolve, consistent with recurrent/reactive cervical lymphadenitis with an acute suppurative flare during the current illness. This current flare began 4 days ago after spending a long time outdoors in cold weather and developing runny nose, sore throat, and painful swallowing; neck pain and swelling increased with fatigue and decreased appetite. Caregiver reports intermittent low-grade fevers and chills during flares, and occasional headache. The neck area is tender and sometimes warm with mild redness during worse days; he avoids turning his head fully due to pain. Associated symptoms include recurrent tonsillar/throat discomfort and hoarse voice at times, suggesting a recurrent upper-airway/tonsillar source, and there was a recent episode of red streaking and tenderness extending from a small scratched area on the right jawline toward the neck, consistent with superficial skin break/lymphangitic inflammation contributing to nodal suppuration. There have also been intermittent crampy abdominal pain and loose stools over the past month, worse during viral-like illnesses. No shortness of breath, no drooling, no neck stiffness, no rash, no ear discharge, no dental pain currently, no weight loss, and no night sweats. No recent travel, no known tuberculosis exposure, and no cat scratches reported.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite reduced during flares; generally prefers convenience foods, struggles with diet planning, and sometimes skips balanced meals. Interested in high-protein foods for weight management but does not follow through consistently.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleep disrupted by throat/neck discomfort; takes warm showers/baths to relax and fall asleep.
current_general_status.excretion: Intermittent loose stools and abdominal cramps over the past month; urination normal.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear weight change reported.

Recurrent tonsillitis and pharyngitis (several episodes treated in urgent care)

Prior superficial skin infection on the chin/jawline with culture reported as Staphylococcus aureus (per caregiver)

infectious_diseases: Impetigo/superficial skin infection in the past year
Streptococcal pharyngitis suspected on prior visits (rapid tests variably positive per caregiver)

persona: He is a 52-year-old freelance computational chemist and a first-generation college graduate who prides himself on financial independence, even while living with his parents to keep expenses stable. He communicates in a rapid, anxious, overly detailed way, jumping between his symptoms, night-shift fatigue, recent wrist injury, and things he has read online, frequently interrupting to ask if something could be cancer or “something systemic,” and needing repeated reassurance. Old-school and email-focused, he prefers careful written instructions he can review on his Android phone, and he is actively trying to build sleep-hygiene routines because he believes better rest will help him heal.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent patchy skin discoloration with fine scaling over the upper chest and back, worsening over the past 6 months, with similar episodes in prior summers.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Began as a few small, round, well-demarcated spots on the upper chest and shoulders that gradually enlarged to fingernail-sized patches and in places merged into larger irregular areas across the upper back. Surface has a dry, fine "dandruff-like" scale, more noticeable after sweating or rubbing with a towel. Mild intermittent itch and irritation with friction from synthetic work shirts, but no pain, oozing, bleeding, or crusting. Reports flares during hot, humid weather and with heavy sweating; partial improvement during cooler months. No fever, no malaise, no new medication other than chronic steroids, no recent travel-related rash, no oral sores, no genital lesions, and no similar rash on hands/feet or nail changes.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals due to night shifts; often relies on quick packaged foods; appetite fair.
current_general_status.sleep: Night shift schedule with fragmented daytime sleep; averages 4 to 6 hours and feels unrefreshed.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements regular; no diarrhea or constipation; urination normal.
current_general_status.weight_change: Underweight baseline; mild unintentional weight loss of about 3 to 4 kg over the last year attributed to stress and missed meals.

persona: The patient is a 1-year-old child from a low-income family, with caregivers who rely on a community shuttle and keep careful track of health because of a heart condition and slow weight gain. The caregiver speaks in a direct, factual way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, but there is a tired, dissatisfied edge and a quiet yearning for the child to feel normal again. The family is principled and well-regarded in a small support circle, valuing friendship and leaning on others after fleeing conflict. Past medical trauma makes clinic visits stressful, so they prefer visual explanations to understand each step and stay cooperative.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Runny nose and nasal congestion starting 3 days ago, with worsening cough and fast breathing since yesterday after close contact with a sick household member.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three days ago developed abrupt rhinorrhea and nasal congestion during a period of cold, humid weather in a crowded, poorly ventilated living space. Within 24 hours progressed to frequent cough and low-grade fever, then developed wheezing and increased work of breathing since yesterday, with faster breathing and occasional pauses in feeding to catch breath. Caregivers note decreased appetite, fewer wet diapers than usual, irritability, and mild lethargy. Two episodes of post-tussive vomiting; no diarrhea. No choking episode, no foreign body concern, no rash, no neck stiffness, and no known toxic ingestion. No barky cough/stridor, and no severe high fever with shaking chills reported. CBC shows mild leukopenia, which can be compatible with viral infection, but given reported chronic immunosuppressant use it warrants correlation with immune status and consideration of repeat CBC/clinical monitoring if clinically indicated.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased oral intake; breastfeeding/formula and solids reduced due to congestion and coughing; tires easily during feeds.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep with frequent waking from cough and noisy breathing.
current_general_status.excretion: Urine output decreased compared with baseline; stools normal in frequency and appearance.
current_general_status.weight_change: No documented acute weight change; baseline underweight for age per caregiver.

previous_diseases: Congenital heart disease (hemodynamically significant defect diagnosed in infancy; followed by pediatric cardiology)
Poor weight gain/failure to thrive (malnutrition noted over several months)

medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No prior surgeries. History of medical trauma reported by caregiver related to a previous painful procedure performed without adequate anesthesia, leading to high distress during medical visits.
infectious_diseases: Recurrent upper respiratory infections since starting frequent contact with other children

persona: She is a 1-year-old girl in a Nordic country, living in a home where her parents share day-to-day responsibilities and keep routines steady. Since about 6 months, her parents have noticed she is slower to sit up and pull to stand, makes fewer babble-like sounds, and gets fussy when plans change; meals can take longer because she tires out. They speak in a direct, factual way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, though they can lose focus if visits drag on. They are hopeful and supportive of therapy, prefer visual examples, and value rehabilitation steps they can practice at home.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Parents report developmental delays and poor language progression noted since around 6 months of age, ongoing over the past 6 months; evaluation is being conducted in a pediatric developmental/genetics context (47,XXX) and any endocrine testing is for age-appropriate screening/interpretation rather than pubertal concern.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Infant has slower-than-expected attainment of gross motor milestones (delayed sitting and pulling to stand) and limited babbling/word-like sounds compared with peers; concerns have become more apparent as age-related expectations increased. Parents also describe mild hypotonia and occasional irritability with changes in routine. Feeding is generally adequate but she fatigues with prolonged meals. No episodes of loss of consciousness, no seizures, no persistent vomiting, no chronic diarrhea, no recurrent high fevers, and no cough or wheezing. No acute injury or toxin ingestion. No evidence of regression; skills are slowly progressing but behind expected level.

current_general_status.diet: Mixed diet of formula and age-appropriate purees/soft solids; appetite fair; occasional prolonged feeding time.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleeps 10-11 hours overnight with 1-2 daytime naps; intermittent night waking when unsettled.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination normal; stools soft, 1-2 per day; no blood or persistent diarrhea.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain; parents feel growth is slightly slower than peers but no acute weight loss.

Pregnancy notable for reported maternal radiation exposure early in gestation (occupational/medical exposure per parent report; details/dose unclear) and this is not established as causal for 47,XXX. Parents report a prior parental sex chromosome test abnormality identified during fertility/genetic evaluation; the specific finding (e.g., mosaicism/structural rearrangement) is unknown from available history and is not presumed to directly explain the child’s 47,XXX. Delivered at term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery; birthweight appropriate for gestational age. No NICU stay.

persona: He is a 32-year-old rigging specialist getting by on a tight budget, currently separated and juggling infant care on little sleep. He speaks in a direct, factual way, answering questions clearly in everyday words, but he can come off formal and a bit confrontational when he feels his concerns are dismissed, especially about keeping his personal information private. Strong-willed and practical, he tries to stay active with judo when his skin isn’t acting up, and he unwinds with long warm showers. He admits he often forgets meds unless he sets phone reminders.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent sudden-onset target-like red rash on hands/forearms with intermittent painful mouth sores, most recently flaring 3 days ago after starting a sulfonamide antibiotic for a skin infection; patient reports he did not mention/recall his prior sulfonamide allergy when the antibiotic was prescribed.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 2 years, he reports episodic flares every few months with rapid development over 24 hours of symmetric red patches and raised papules on the backs of the hands and forearms, sometimes extending to the elbows and knees. Lesions enlarge and develop darker dusky centers with a paler ring and outer red rim, associated with mild burning and occasional itching; some evolve into small clear blisters. During the current flare, he notes more extensive lesions on the feet and lower legs and painful oral erosions making eating and speaking uncomfortable. He also reports mild fatigue and a scratchy sore throat at the start of this episode but denies high fever, persistent cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe headache, or joint swelling. He denies eye pain, significant photophobia, thick purulent eye discharge, genital ulcers, dysuria, widespread skin peeling, or rapidly progressive skin tenderness.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced oral intake over the last 2 days due to mouth pain; otherwise typically balanced and avoids known triggers/foods that have caused past reactions.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep due to infant care and discomfort from the rash; reports feeling burnt out from caregiving demands.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel movements; normal urination without pain.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant unintentional weight change.

persona: She is a bright, energetic preschooler whose parent speaks with anxious certainty, arriving with a self-made “diagnosis” from online searches and pushing hard for specific tests or prescriptions while brushing off details that do not match their theory. At home, the caregiver is very hand-hygiene focused and tends to skim headlines rather than read full guidance, which can fuel worry and second-guessing. The family tries to lead conversations in the exam room and can get defensive when reassured, but they are ultimately motivated by doing the right thing and keeping her healthy.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradual yellow-orange discoloration of the palms and soles noticed by parent about 6 weeks ago, with intermittent recurrence over the past month.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Parent reports the child developed a yellow to orange-yellow tint first on the palms and soles, then more noticeable around the nasolabial folds and forehead. Color change has been slowly progressive and more apparent under daylight. Mild dry skin with occasional itching, worse after baths, but no pain and no limitation in activity. Appetite has been picky; she frequently asks for carrots, pumpkin puree, orange-colored snacks, and fruit smoothies containing papaya and carrots, which increased over the past 2 months. No yellowing of the whites of the eyes, no dark urine, no pale stools, no abdominal pain, no vomiting, no fever, no cough, and no recent medication changes. No easy bruising, no swelling, and no change in urine output.

current_general_status.diet: Picky eater with frequent intake of carotene-rich foods (carrots, pumpkin, papaya) and orange-colored smoothies/snacks; limited variety and small portion sizes.
current_general_status.sleep: Parent is sleep-conscious; bedtime routine is consistent but child has occasional difficulty settling; overall sleeps about 10 hours nightly with rare awakenings.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements most days; occasional hard stools; urination normal per parent.

persona: She is a 32-year-old single convenience store owner who keeps a tight budget, tends to stay home and keep to herself after long days, and often relies on quick fast food between childcare and work. A former national swim team member, she’s used to performing under pressure and carries herself with an almost expert, selective confidence while still valuing inclusivity in how she treats people. In conversation she presents like an “internet doctor,” arriving with a firm self-diagnosis and pushing for specific treatments, citing label-reading habits and ingredient concerns while dismissing details that don’t fit her theory.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent painful, easily bleeding growth around the right thumbnail with progressive nail lifting for about 3 months, worsening again over the last 2 weeks after a minor manicure-related injury and frequent wet work.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Started as mild tenderness and redness at the lateral nail fold, followed within days by a small, bright red raised bump that rapidly enlarged and now bleeds with light contact (toweling, diaper changes). Over weeks, she noticed whitening and cloudiness at the distal-lateral nail edge with gradual separation and a widening gap under the nail, associated with throbbing pain and intermittent serous drainage; on two occasions there was scant purulent-appearing discharge after soaking. Symptoms fluctuate but recur, especially after repeated exposure to dishwashing/cleaning agents and minor periungual trauma. Accompanying symptoms include localized swelling, tenderness to pressure, and occasional crusting. She denies fever, chills, spreading redness up the hand, numbness/tingling, color changes in the fingers with cold, joint pain, oral ulcers, or new rashes elsewhere.

current_general_status.diet: Generally balanced; trying to follow a low-sodium pattern and has been using carbohydrate counting from prior counseling, though adherence is inconsistent since becoming a new parent.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleeps less than 6 hours most nights due to childcare demands.
current_general_status.excretion: No change in bowel or urinary habits.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change reported.

Seasonal allergic rhinitis

medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No major surgeries. Recurrent minor periungual trauma from nail trimming/manicures; recent small cut near the right thumbnail 2 weeks ago.

persona: An 88-year-old middle-class man who lives alone and tries to stay disciplined about healthy habits, though nocturia and fatigue have made his days feel smaller. In conversation he is a poor historian, describing things as "off" or "uncomfortable" and giving a jumbled timeline, sometimes gesturing broadly to his belly and sides rather than pinpointing pain. He can start out a bit resentful about repeated visits and long waits, especially with a mild language barrier, but becomes more collaborative when he feels heard and included in decisions. He unwinds with comedy shows and keeps expectations practical and direct.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually worsening bilateral flank and abdominal fullness with intermittent dull pain and higher home blood pressure readings over the past 10 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 10 months, he has developed a slowly progressive sense of abdominal distension and bilateral flank heaviness with intermittent dull aching pain, worse after prolonged standing and with decreased fluid intake. Over the last 3 months he reports reduced exercise tolerance and more frequent nocturia, along with occasional episodes of dark-tinged urine and foamy urine. Home blood pressure readings have trended upward despite adherence to prior medications, with intermittent morning headaches. He has had two episodes of fever with dysuria in the past year treated as urinary infection with oral antibiotics, with temporary improvement. He notes generalized fatigue and mild itching but denies severe shortness of breath at rest. No acute colicky radiating pain, no passage of stones, no persistent vomiting, no frank anuria, no chest pain, no focal neurologic deficits, and no recent major unintentional weight loss. No chronic cough or fever suggesting pulmonary infection.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite mildly reduced; prefers lactose-free foods; drinks modest fluids to avoid nocturia; tries to limit salty foods.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep due to nocturia and intermittent flank discomfort; uses journaling to manage stress.
current_general_status.excretion: Nocturia 3-5 times nightly; daytime frequency increased; occasional dysuria during past infection episodes; intermittent dark urine; bowel movements regular with occasional constipation when taking pain medication.
current_general_status.weight_change: Mild unintentional weight loss over several months, about 2-3 kg.

Chronic kidney disease with gradually rising creatinine over several years (previously described as 'reduced kidney function')
Recurrent urinary tract infections
Benign prostatic hyperplasia with lower urinary tract symptoms
Hyperlipidemia
Anemia of chronic disease suspected in prior labs
medical_history.long_term_medication: Amlodipine and an ACE inhibitor used for blood pressure control (dose adjustments in the last year); tamsulosin for urinary symptoms; atorvastatin; intermittent acetaminophen for pain; avoids regular NSAID use due to kidney concerns.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Prolonged ICU stay with intubation after severe pneumonia about 6 years ago; no major surgeries reported.
infectious_diseases: Severe community-acquired pneumonia requiring ICU admission (remote)
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date on influenza most years and pneumococcal vaccination per age; COVID-19 vaccination completed with boosters per local recommendations.

persona: She is a 90-year-old retired science journalist who still keeps up with medical news, but lately feels that her own body is doing something “weird” and hard to pin down. When she talks, she’s raw and emotional, often jumping around in the timeline and using broad descriptions like “off,” needing gentle, repeated questions and extra time to make decisions. She lives simply on a tight budget, leans on faith for steadiness, and tries to stay active with short daily walks and household routines. With a high-deductible plan, she worries about costs and pushes for diagnostic accuracy and a second opinion.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of a loud high-pitched ringing/buzzing in the right ear that began after a recent flight with noticeable ear pressure changes about 2 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She describes a constant, high-pitched electrical/cicada-like sound, most prominent in the right ear and occasionally perceived as “inside the head,” starting shortly after descent on a flight; it has persisted without full relief and becomes more noticeable in quiet settings and at bedtime. She reports increased difficulty hearing conversation (especially in background noise) that has been gradually worsening over years, but the intrusive ringing is new. Associated symptoms include intermittent ear fullness on the right, mild jaw discomfort with chewing and morning jaw stiffness, and heightened anxiety with irritability and trouble falling asleep since onset. She feels physically fatigued from poor sleep and stress. She denies true spinning vertigo, severe nausea/vomiting, ear drainage, fever, recent upper respiratory infection, sudden severe unilateral hearing blackout, facial weakness, new severe headache, focal numbness/weakness, confusion, or new visual changes. persona: She is a 55-year-old widowed software developer who prefers to keep her personal matters private and tends to brush off health concerns with a quiet, "it's nothing, I'm fine," often only showing up because a family member insisted. Nostalgic by nature, she talks about earlier years fondly, including her time as a hockey player, and she leans on practical judgment more than charts or analytics, especially when she is anxious. With limited access to technology and a tight budget, she is price-sensitive, asks for a clear plan, and hopes for rapid test results so she can get back to caring for her adolescent sibling with a gentle, steady approach.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent postcoital spotting and recurrent light vaginal bleeding for about 8 months, with occasional watery discharge for 2 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 8 months (postmenopausal) she has noticed small amounts of bright red or brown spotting after intercourse and occasionally after heavy lifting, initially rare but now occurring most weeks. She also reports increased mucoid to watery vaginal discharge over the last 2 months with mild pelvic heaviness. She denies severe pelvic pain, fever, chills, foul-smelling discharge, dysuria, flank pain, passage of tissue, or recent new sexual partners. No syncope or large-volume bleeding. She reports a long history since her reproductive years of recurrent painless second-trimester pregnancy losses (suggestive of cervical insufficiency). She also recalls a later vaginal delivery complicated by heavy bleeding requiring urgent intervention, during which she was told she had a cervical tear/laceration that was not definitively repaired at the time; she attributes her known cervical scarring to this event (with additional risk from prior uterine evacuation procedures). She denies years of ongoing postmenopausal bleeding; the current spotting/discharge began in the last 8 months after menopause.

persona: She is a 42-year-old data scientist and online educator with a successful, reform-minded streak, usually principled and disciplined about routines and evidence. Lately she arrives by taxi because fatigue and poor sleep make driving feel like too much, and in the exam room she speaks quickly, anxiously, and at length, jumping between symptom timelines, work stress, and small details while repeatedly asking clarifying questions. She tends to assume the worst and needs steady reassurance, yet she also prefers minimal social contact outside close family and keeps health reading to a minimum, relying on clinicians to walk her through the facts.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent generalized itching with worsening fatigue over the past 8 months, prompting an emergency department visit tonight due to inability to sleep.

Fatigue has gradually increased, with reduced stamina and difficulty completing daily tasks, which she attributes partly to caregiving burnout. Over the last 6 weeks she has noticed occasional nausea and a vague right upper abdominal discomfort without severe pain. In the past 2 weeks, she has intermittently noted darker urine and lighter stools but is unsure about visible yellowing of the eyes. She denies fever, chills, vomiting blood, black stools, persistent watery diarrhea, cough, chest pain, shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, dysuria, flank pain, acute severe abdominal pain, confusion, or syncope. No recent travel, no known hepatitis exposure, and no recent new high-risk sexual contacts reported.

persona: She is a 37-year-old woman in Bungay who lives on a fixed income and takes pride in staying organized, even when she feels run down. Retired from breakdancing, she’s practical and a bit cynical about “experts,” but she still wants fair, straightforward care and tries to treat people with respect and forgiveness. She speaks in clear, everyday terms and answers questions directly, focusing on what she can feel: the belly pain, fever, bloating, and how it’s changing day to day. She’s emotionally torn and conflicted about her health and the uncertainty of what comes next.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of diffuse abdominal pain with fever and rapid abdominal bloating starting 3 days ago, temporally associated with a fall at home.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three days ago, after a fall at home (no clear head injury; unsure if any rib or hip injury), she developed abrupt, worsening, diffuse abdominal pain (constant dull ache with intermittent cramping, worse with movement) with fever and progressive abdominal distension. A brief trauma-focused evaluation did not suggest acute intra-abdominal bleeding (no hypotension or syncope reported, no noted progressive pallor, and abdominal ultrasound showed complex ascites with septations/peritoneal thickening rather than hemoperitoneum). Over the next 48 hours she noted early satiety, nausea, reduced appetite, and alternating loose, mushy stools and constipation, with increasing fatigue and night sweats. She reports a sensation of abdominal fullness and tightness, and mild shortness of breath when lying flat as the abdomen has become more distended. She denies hematemesis, black or bloody stools, urinary burning or flank pain, new vaginal discharge, or missed periods. No persistent cough, pleuritic chest pain, or known sick contacts; no recent travel. No sudden severe localized right-lower-quadrant pain, no jaundice, and no continuous vomiting to suggest an acute mechanical obstruction.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite for 3 days; tolerating small amounts of liquids and bland foods; early satiety.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented due to abdominal discomfort and fever; reports night sweats.

current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months with more noticeable recent loss; clothes fitting looser. Context suggests chronic inflammation and reduced intake/malnutrition, with anemia likely consistent with anemia of chronic disease; plan is to monitor CBC trends and support nutrition during treatment.

persona: An 85-year-old widowed retired physics academic on a tight budget, she values respect for elders and can be socially anxious in clinics, often switching providers when she feels unheard. She arrives with printouts and confidently frames her symptoms as a specific diagnosis she found online, pushing for particular scans and medications while brushing aside details that do not match her theory, and she can get defensive when challenged. Despite that, she responds well to calm, step-by-step explanations and appreciates being walked through each decision, keeping a daily stretching routine to manage stiffness and stress.

Over the next several days she noted worsening diffuse muscle aches and joint pains (knees and wrists) and new episodic crampy abdominal pain with nausea; she reports one episode of dark, tarry stool yesterday. She also noticed new numbness and burning pain in the right foot with mild weakness when lifting the toes, without back pain. Home blood pressure readings have been higher than usual. She denies cough, sore throat, dysuria, flank pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, new confusion, severe headache, neck stiffness, or recent trauma; no known sick contacts. No recent long-distance travel; no known new food exposures.

persona: He is a 38-year-old man who has been couch-surfing and sometimes sleeping in shelters after a recent financial collapse, and he tries to stay grounded by helping others when he can. He’s direct and factual when he talks, using simple terms and answering questions clearly, and he tends to trust clinicians while still preferring traditional, familiar choices over things he sees online. He’s emotionally invested in what’s happening to his body, reads only in his native language, and learns best when someone explains things with real-life examples. Because his mobility is limited and he uses a wheelchair, he paces himself day to day and is open to gentle, holistic approaches alongside medical care.

He reports no coffee-ground emesis, no bright red blood per rectum, and no recent anticoagulant use. He describes episodic abdominal distention and ankle swelling in the past year that fluctuates with diet and activity.

Over the last 2 months, episodes have become more frequent with occasional night waking due to back discomfort and difficulty turning in bed; he also reports episodic heel pain with tenderness when first stepping out of bed and mild fatigue during sports. He has had one episode of a painful red eye with photophobia and tearing that resolved after an urgent visit and eyedrops. No high fever, no recent sore throat, no persistent cough, no chest pain, no acute traumatic injury, no focal weakness or numbness, no bowel or bladder incontinence, and no unexplained weight loss.

persona: He is a 2-year-old boy in Alachua County who is mostly quiet with clinicians, while his caregiver does most of the talking in a rapid, anxious, detail-heavy way and frequently asks for reassurance. Appetite has been variable, and he occasionally complains of leg discomfort by crying when picked up under the arms. No fever, no persistent cough, no vomiting or chronic diarrhea at present, no acute injury, no seizures, and no episodes of loss of consciousness. No known toxin ingestion. Symptoms are chronic/recurrent rather than sudden.

He also admits he rarely drinks water and wants symptoms handled early rather than “waiting it out.”

Over the last 3 months, he noticed gradually enlarging, painless lumps in the neck and groin with increasing fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. He reports intermittent low-grade fevers and drenching night sweats several nights per week, along with unintentional weight loss and decreased appetite. He also describes episodic crampy abdominal discomfort, bloating, and looser stools that fluctuate with gluten intake, and occasional early satiety. He denies acute shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, focal limb swelling, severe sudden abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, black or bloody stools, jaundice, severe headaches, focal neurologic deficits, or recent sick contacts.

persona: He is a newborn boy, and his parents are practical-minded and tend to stick to facts when talking with clinicians, describing what they see in simple, everyday language. They report his lips turning bluish, fast breathing, getting tired and sweaty during feeds, and a few brief spells where he suddenly looked pale and limp with a very fast heartbeat felt while holding him. They are calm but clearly seeking reassurance and doing things “by the book,” and they understand best when staff use pictures or diagrams to explain what is happening and what to watch for.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Brought by parents for sudden worsening bluish discoloration of lips and fast breathing starting several hours after birth (today).
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Parents report cyanosis noted shortly after delivery that became more pronounced over the first day of life, with episodic worsening during feeding/crying. Infant has tachypnea with mild subcostal retractions, poor feeding with tiring and sweating, and increased sleepiness between feeds. Several brief episodes of sudden pallor with limpness and rapid heartbeat sensation felt by caregiver when holding the infant, each lasting less than a minute and resolving spontaneously. No fever, no cough or nasal congestion, no vomiting or diarrhea, no choking episode, no rash, and no known sick contacts. No seizures observed, and no focal weakness. Urine output appears reduced compared with expected for age but not absent. Caregivers were advised that in cyanotic congenital heart disease, oxygen may provide limited improvement in saturations due to mixing/right-to-left shunt; oxygen should be used as supportive therapy while primary management focuses on ductal/systemic perfusion optimization and cardiology-directed care, with escalation to CPAP/intubation if apnea or respiratory failure occurs.

current_general_status.diet: Breastfeeding attempted but feeds are short with frequent pauses; supplementation discussed due to fatigue during feeds.
current_general_status.sleep: More sleepy than expected; wakes but tires easily.
current_general_status.excretion: Fewer wet diapers than expected for the first day of life; passed meconium.
current_general_status.weight_change: Birth weight not yet rechecked; no documented weight change since delivery.

previous_diseases: Prenatally suspected complex congenital heart defect with postnatal confirmation of two great arteries arising predominantly from the right ventricle with a ventricular septal communication
Ventricular volume/pressure overload with ventricular enlargement noted on early cardiac imaging
Suspected genetic/chromosomal abnormality (family counseled prenatally; testing planned, concern for 22q11 deletion)
medical_history.long_term_medication: None for the infant prior to admission; received routine newborn prophylaxis (vitamin K) per delivery record.

Prenatal ultrasound suggested complex cardiac malformation; family referred for fetal cardiology. No known perinatal infections reported. Apgar scores not provided by family.

family_history.hereditary_diseases: None.

persona: She is a 64-year-old woman who lives on a limited budget and keeps a disciplined routine, taking pride in fairness and sportsmanship. With a practical, slightly creative streak, she likes to read full articles before making decisions and prefers clear options rather than guesswork. Since being widowed, she has learned to adjust and stay steady under stress, though an ongoing legal battle has her watching wait times and appointment availability closely. She speaks in simple, direct terms about what she feels and when it happens, and she is upfront about pain, bleeding, and how it affects walking, urinating, and intimacy.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent burning pain at the urethral opening with a small bleeding, red bump noticed at the urethral meatus for about 6 months, worsening over the past 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: About 6 months ago she began to notice episodic stinging/burning at the urethral opening, most noticeable during urination and after prolonged walking or friction from underwear. Over time she noted a small red protrusion at the urethral opening that has slowly enlarged and has bled small amounts when wiping or after sexual activity. In the last 3 weeks she reports more urinary frequency and urgency, increased nocturia, and occasional spraying of the urinary stream with a sensation of incomplete emptying. She also describes needing to strain to start and maintain the stream, attributing this to a long-standing obstructive pattern that has become more bothersome recently. Accompanying symptoms include mild suprapubic discomfort at times and dyspareunia related to irritation at the meatus. She denies fever, flank pain, nausea/vomiting, foul-smelling vaginal discharge, new vaginal lesions, gross heavy bleeding, passage of clots, new back pain, or recent trauma.

current_general_status.diet: Generally balanced diet; tries to limit sugary foods and caffeine to reduce urinary irritation; adequate oral intake.
current_general_status.sleep: Light, fragmented sleep due to waking to urinate 2 to 3 times nightly; occasional difficulty falling asleep when anxious.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination with frequency/urgency, occasional weak stream and spraying, and intermittent straining; bowel movements regular without constipation.

persona: He is a 19-year-old college student living on campus, currently between jobs and looking for affordable housing while trying to stay independent. He cares about environmental issues and follows debates closely, tending to respect people who can work across party lines, but he can feel insecure and easily threatened when stress piles up. When he talks with clinicians, he is direct and factual, describing what he feels in simple terms and answering questions clearly without drifting off-topic. Lately he has been sleeping poorly, skipping meals when overwhelmed, and he’s focused on practical ways to keep symptoms from disrupting school.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent painful mouth sores on the inner lip and cheek, with the current flare starting about 5 days ago after several nights of poor sleep and a period of high stress.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports a burning/tingling sensation at onset followed within 24 hours by one to three round, shallow ulcers with a white-yellow base and a red rim on the inner lower lip and right buccal mucosa. Pain is sharp and worsens with acidic/spicy foods, toothbrushing, and speaking; he has been avoiding solid foods and chewing on the affected side. Notes mild fatigue and irritability during flares, but no high fever or chills. He associates recurrences with stressful weeks and shortened sleep, and with mechanical irritation (vigorous brushing and occasional trauma from sharp/crunchy foods). No vesicular rash on the lips, no diffuse gum bleeding, no thick removable white plaques, no persistent sore throat, no cough or nasal symptoms, and no genital ulcers or eye pain/redness. Biopsy was pursued due to recurrent episodes with a repeatedly fixed site on the right buccal mucosa and concern for atypical persistence compared with prior flares, to exclude other pathology.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced intake due to oral pain; relying on soft foods and liquids; low intake of meat/leafy greens recently and tends to skip meals when stressed.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep for several weeks, often 4–6 hours per night with difficulty falling asleep during stressful periods.
current_general_status.excretion: No change; normal bowel and urinary habits.
current_general_status.weight_change: Slight unintentional weight loss over the past month due to reduced appetite and skipping meals.

previous_diseases: Recurrent episodes of painful oral sores since mid-teens
Seasonal allergic rhinitis

medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per patient report, including routine childhood vaccinations.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Presenting for a rapid-access/urgent add-on Endocrinology visit for evaluation and management of elevated cholesterol and related metabolic risk.

Noticed recurrent yellowish, slightly raised patches on the upper eyelids and occasional right upper abdominal discomfort over the past 6 months; presenting today for evaluation during an urgent care visit.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 6 months he has had gradually enlarging, non-painful yellow plaques near both eyelids that become more noticeable during periods of poor sleep and stress. He also reports intermittent fatigue, a sense of bloating after fatty meals, and mild intermittent dull discomfort in the right upper abdomen without sharp pain. No pruritus, no generalized jaundice, and no dark urine. He occasionally feels short of breath when climbing stairs but denies exertional chest pain, palpitations, syncope, calf swelling, or radiation of pain to the arm or jaw. No fever, no cough, no persistent nausea/vomiting, and no acute severe abdominal pain. He has not noticed visual loss, but reports occasional blurry vision after long screen use. Symptoms have been chronic and recurrent rather than sudden, prompting this visit.

current_general_status.diet: High intake of fried/fast foods and comfort foods during stress; frequent late-night snacking; limited fruit/vegetable intake.
current_general_status.sleep: Irregular sleep schedule with reduced sleep during periods of stress and after relocation.

persona: He is a 5-year-old kindergartener with Down syndrome whose parents are trying to keep routines steady after a recent fall and some new eye drifting. At home, his caregivers speak in a direct, factual way, using simple words like “his right eye turns in” and “he says things look like two,” and they answer questions clearly without rambling. The family values being inclusive and stays engaged with school supports, though they feel stressed about being uninsured and having to manage care on their own. He can get jealous of attention and may hold onto hurt feelings, but he warms up with patience and clear choices.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Parents noticed sudden new onset of inward turning of the right eye with intermittent double vision starting 3 days ago after a minor head injury at home.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Three days ago, after a fall with brief crying but no loss of consciousness, parents noticed the right eye intermittently drifting inward, especially when focusing on near objects. The child has been squinting and occasionally closing one eye to look at toys or the TV, and has started turning his head slightly to one side to see more comfortably. He reports that objects sometimes look “two,” and he rubs his eyes after short periods of drawing or screen time. Mild photophobia is noted outdoors with increased squinting. No fever, no eye discharge, no significant eye redness, no severe headache, no vomiting, no neck stiffness, no recent upper respiratory symptoms, no new limb weakness, no seizures, and no gait instability reported by caregivers.

current_general_status.diet: Picky appetite but taking regular meals; prefers soft foods; parents trying to reduce salty snacks.
current_general_status.sleep: Slightly reduced due to fussiness at bedtime; no snoring reported.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements for age.

persona: She is a 20-year-old woman who is currently unemployed and relies on public transportation, so she often puts off clinic visits until something feels really "off" or hard to ignore. She speaks in a vague, uncertain way and mixes up timing, saying things like it felt “weird” and “uncomfortable” for maybe a day or two, and she needs step-by-step guidance to answer questions clearly. Cautious and a bit skeptical of online health advice, she prefers local TV news and tends to regret decisions afterward, especially when she worries she waited too long. Even so, she is careful about hand hygiene and wants straightforward, practical instructions she can follow.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of severe right eye pain with redness and light sensitivity starting 2 days ago after accidentally scratching the eye while removing contact lenses.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She reports a sudden foreign-body sensation followed within hours by worsening sharp ocular pain, marked photophobia, excessive tearing, and increasing redness. Over the last 24 hours she has noticed blurred vision in the affected eye and thicker yellow-white discharge with crusting on waking, making it difficult to keep the eye open. She has tried frequent artificial tears and rinsing the eye at home with minimal relief and admits feeling embarrassed about the appearance of the eye and delayed seeking care. Associated symptoms include mild headache and poor sleep due to pain. She denies fever, sore throat, cough, recent flu-like illness, vesicular facial rash, significant eye itching, chemical exposure, or pain with eye movements.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite due to discomfort; prefers lactose-free options; drinks coffee daily.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep over the last 2 nights due to eye pain and photophobia.

persona: The patient is a 14-year-old student who lives in seasonal worker housing and tries to present as tough and independent, often putting on an expressive, indirect front when worried. Congenitally deaf, the patient communicates mainly through sign language and quick written notes, and tends to arrive with screenshots and confident self-diagnoses from the internet, getting defensive if clinicians challenge the theory or slow down requested tests. Despite valuing healthy living and preaching hand hygiene, day-to-day habits skew toward cafeteria snacks and salty foods, and recent fatigue, bruising, and gum oozing have made the patient anxious about costs and losing control.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of easy bruising and scattered red-purple skin spots with gum bleeding, worsening over the past 2 weeks after a recent cold; presented today after a fall at home with increasing swelling and pain in the left wrist and prolonged oozing from minor scrapes.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 8-10 months, the patient has had intermittent, recurrent episodes of unexplained petechiae on the lower legs and forearms and easy bruising after minor bumps, with some episodes improving and then returning. Over the last 2 weeks, following an upper respiratory tract infection with sore throat and runny nose that resolved without antibiotics, he noticed a higher frequency of new non-blanching spots and larger bruises, plus gum oozing when brushing teeth and one episode of spontaneous nosebleed that took longer than usual to stop. Today he fell at home and since then has had disproportionate bruising around the wrist with persistent oozing from small abrasions and concern for a possible fracture. He reports mild fatigue and anxiety about bleeding and medical costs. No fever, no productive cough, no weight loss, no night sweats, no joint swelling or severe morning stiffness, no chest pain or shortness of breath, no abdominal pain, no black tarry stools, no bright red blood per rectum, no visible blood in urine, no severe headache, no vomiting, no confusion, and no recent use of aspirin or other blood-thinning medications.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite over the past week due to stress; otherwise eats mixed home-prepared and cafeteria foods in seasonal worker housing.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep for the last several nights related to acute anxiety and wrist pain.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel habits; urine normal in color; no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: No known unintentional weight change.

persona: She is a 32-year-old married woman who balances caregiving at home with a mid-income job in financial advising, so her days are structured and busy. She tends to be nervous but practical, trusting the healthcare system and preferring clear plans, and she checks the patient portal regularly to keep track of instructions and results. She speaks in simple, direct terms about what she feels and what makes it worse, and she wants straightforward answers without scare tactics. A past painful procedure without enough numbing makes her ask up front about pain control. She respects elders, leans pro-union, and unwinds with a short home workout when she can.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent small lump on the right side of the vulva that flares up intermittently for about 8 months, with worsening tenderness over the past 5 days.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient reports a pea-sized bump on the labial area that comes and goes, usually painless at baseline but gradually enlarges during flares. Over the last 5 days it became more tender with localized swelling and warmth, making sitting and walking uncomfortable and causing pain with intercourse; she notes occasional scant drainage after warm compresses. She denies vaginal bleeding, foul-smelling discharge, dysuria, urinary frequency, flank pain, generalized rash, fever/chills, nausea/vomiting, or new ulcer-like lesions. She reports no notable increase in vaginal discharge and no odor; however, any mild internal vaginal irritation is minimal compared with the focal vulvar pain. Given the proximity of the inflamed vulvar lesion, abnormal vaginal test indices (e.g., elevated pH/inflammatory cells) may reflect sampling contamination or adjacent inflammation from the vulvar infection, though a concurrent minimally symptomatic vaginal process cannot be fully excluded. No recent new sexual partner; no known STI exposure.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals due to work and childcare demands; tends to eat low-cost, carbohydrate-heavy foods; hydration variable.
current_general_status.sleep: Reduced sleep (frequent nighttime awakenings) related to childcare and discomfort during the recent flare.

persona: He is a 14-year-old boy from Tampico, Illinois, living with his family in a tight budget household. He is motivated to make changes, trying to improve brushing and cut sugary drinks, and he often reads hospital website articles to understand what the doctors tell him. He speaks in a direct, factual way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, but he can get anxious at night and worries about missing school and sports. He cares a lot about giving back and respecting his family’s roots, and he takes pride in his country and community.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset gum swelling and spontaneous bleeding for 10 days after a period of inconsistent toothbrushing and no recent dental care.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 10 days he developed rapidly worsening gum enlargement, most pronounced along the upper and lower front teeth, with dark red to pale, swollen gums that bleed spontaneously and with gentle brushing. Bleeding has become persistent at times and is associated with mouth soreness, occasional small necrotic-appearing ulcers on the oral mucosa, and worsening halitosis, leading him to avoid brushing. He also reports new fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, intermittent low-grade fever with chills, lightheadedness, and decreased appetite; his caregiver noticed pallor and small bruises on the legs with minimal trauma. Over the same period he has felt “lumps” in the neck that are mildly tender. He denies recent dental procedures, major nosebleeds, coughing blood, black or bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, severe headache, focal weakness, chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, night sweats, or known sick contacts.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite decreased; prefers soft foods due to gum pain; trying to limit sugary drinks after a recent diagnosis of type 2 diabetes (on metformin with nutrition counseling; working on carbohydrate counting/label reading) but struggles with label reading.

persona: She is a 33-year-old health policy officer who is financially comfortable, socially active, and values inclusivity, leaning on her spouse for support when she feels anxious. She tends to be attached and seeks reassurance, but in appointments she is a poor historian, describing symptoms as "weird" or "off" with a jumbled timeline and broad gestures rather than clear locations or intensities. She tries to eat mostly plant-forward foods but often falls back on convenience meals during stressful workweeks, and she feels conflicted about vaccines, questioning mandates while still wanting to meet vaccination requirements when they affect her life plans.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of dark red vaginal spotting with intermittent lower abdominal cramping starting about 10 hours ago after a stressful workday; positive home pregnancy test one week ago. Evaluation today for early pregnancy bleeding; baseline serum hCG obtained this visit with plan to repeat in 48 hours. Early dating based on transvaginal ultrasound measurements suggests very early gestation; cardiac activity may not yet be expected and follow-up imaging is planned.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She reports new scant-to-moderate dark red spotting that began abruptly and has persisted through the day, with intermittent crampy suprapubic pain and mild lumbosacral soreness. Spotting increases slightly with prolonged standing and improves somewhat with rest. She notes mild nausea and breast tenderness consistent with early pregnancy, and increased anxiety about the pregnancy. She denies passage of tissue, heavy bleeding soaking pads, syncope, fever or chills, foul-smelling discharge, dysuria, flank pain, severe unilateral pelvic pain, shoulder pain, or acute shortness of breath. No recent abdominal trauma. No known recent sexually transmitted infection exposure.

persona: She is a 3-year-old preschooler whose parents are intensely growth-focused and easily irritated after months of disrupted meals and sleep. Her caregiver speaks like an “Internet doctor,” arriving with YouTube health-channel notes, a firm theory about what’s happening, and pointed requests for specific imaging or antibiotics, brushing off details that don’t fit. They are financially dependent on support and sometimes mention sponsors or donors who help with expenses, while being unusually strict about digital privacy and limiting who can access photos or records. They favor low-carb, soft-texture foods and are diligent about masks in crowded places.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of an enlarged-appearing tongue with feeding and speech difficulties, noticed intermittently over the past 6 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Caregivers report the tongue has looked larger than usual on and off, with visible tooth impressions along the sides and occasional surface cracks. Over time, episodes have become more frequent and last longer, and the child increasingly struggles with chewing and swallowing solid foods, often taking a long time to finish meals and preferring softer textures. Speech has become less clear compared with peers, with difficulty forming some sounds. During worse periods there is intermittent oral discomfort and drooling, which caregivers note is related to poor lip seal and difficulty managing/clearing saliva with swallowing rather than an increase in saliva volume. Caregivers also report signs consistent with dry mouth at baseline including a drier oral mucosa with thirst and sticky/thick saliva at times, and they have noticed increased dental plaque/early caries concerns. At night, caregivers occasionally notice noisy breathing and brief pauses in breathing, especially when sleeping on her back, with occasional mouth-breathing/snoring. No fever, no cough, no wheezing, no choking episode/foreign-body ingestion, no vomiting, no acute facial or lip swelling, and no rash.

current_general_status.diet: Prefers soft foods; reduced intake during flare-ups due to discomfort and swallowing difficulty.
current_general_status.sleep: Restless sleep with occasional snoring/noisy breathing; rare witnessed brief pauses in breathing per caregiver.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urine output; stools regular, occasional constipation when oral intake is low.

He talks fast and at length, jumping between symptoms, school, and YouTube health videos, and he frequently interrupts to ask if this could be something dangerous, needing steady reassurance. Safety-conscious and empathetic, he feels guilty about missed practices and worries about his family’s budget, often asking for the least expensive options. A long stretch of illness in a close relative left him especially sensitive to lingering symptoms.

Over the last 3 weeks he reports more frequent flares with limping, warmth over the knee during episodes, and reduced range of motion when swelling is present; he sometimes avoids stairs and cannot fully squat. Accompanying symptoms include fatigue, reduced endurance at school and sports, and occasional low-grade malaise. No high fever, no sore throat or recent diarrheal illness, no new rash, no eye redness or vision changes during flares beyond baseline visual impairment, no mouth ulcers, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no persistent back pain, and no recent major joint trauma.

persona: He is a 17-year-old high school student from a low-income household who wants to "start fresh" and stay on track with school, but he is frustrated by feeling run-down and having frequent minor illnesses. He talks like an Internet expert, confidently citing influencers, threads, and screenshots as proof of what is "really" going on, and he pushes for specific tests or medications while dismissing details that do not match his theory. He is reform-minded and direct, asking for cost transparency and clear out-of-pocket estimates, and he tries to steer conversations toward quick, convenient refills and actionable next steps.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent fatigue with easy bruising and frequent minor infections over the past 10 months, worsening over the last 6 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 10 months he has had gradually increasing tiredness, reduced exercise tolerance with routine walking, and intermittent lightheadedness, initially during school activities and now with daily tasks. During the same period he developed easy bruising on the arms and legs after minor bumps, occasional gum bleeding when brushing, and more frequent upper-respiratory infections that seem to linger longer than usual. Over the last 6 weeks, family has noticed increased pallor and he reports decreased concentration, mild shortness of breath on exertion, and occasional nosebleeds. He also reports long-standing short stature compared with peers, patchy brown skin hyperpigmentation since childhood, and a congenital thumb abnormality (small/hypoplastic thumb) that has not changed. No high fever, no productive cough, no chest pain, no jaundice, no severe abdominal pain, no persistent focal bone pain, and no recent travel-related diarrheal illness.

persona: He is a 67-year-old man who has spent years working in apparel exporting and still takes quiet pride in good design and how things look and feel. Shy and soft-spoken, he is grateful for help but is a poor historian, describing symptoms as “weird” or “off” and giving a jumbled timeline, often gesturing broadly to his back and hips rather than pinpointing a spot. He tries to stay calm and focuses on improving sleep, uses a medication reminder app, and prefers rideshares to get around. With a science-minded streak from past molecular genetics training, he now strongly advocates for safer medication labeling and use.
