In the last 2 weeks after a febrile respiratory illness, symptoms flared with more diffuse bone discomfort, muscle cramps, and generalized itching, with reduced activity tolerance. Denies sudden focal trauma, chest pain, acute shortness of breath, hematuria, dysuria, red/hot swollen joints, focal neurologic deficits, saddle anesthesia, bowel or bladder incontinence, or new radicular shooting pain down one leg.

persona: A 76-year-old woman who values dignity and prefers to keep problems private, she comes across stoic and quick to insist she is "fine" even when discomfort is clearly wearing her down. She works part-time doing supply deliveries for a pharmaceutical distributor when her joints and energy allow, and she tends to make reflective, slightly cynical observations about how hard it is to keep up with appointments and routines while living on the edge financially. Although frustration shows in clipped answers and long pauses, she rarely asks for help directly and only came in after a family member pushed her to. She worries about her appearance and sleep but minimizes it to avoid seeming weak.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent, severe itching with new rough, firm bumps on the arms and shins worsening over the past 8 months, with a flare in the last 3 weeks after several insect bites while sleeping outdoors.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Started as small, intensely itchy red bumps on the forearms and lower legs that she scratched frequently; over months the lesions enlarged into multiple firm, dome-shaped nodules with a thick, rough surface, most prominent on the anterior shins and outer arms, with scattered lesions on the lower back/waistline. Itching is paroxysmal and often unbearable, worse at night and during stress, and triggered by clothing friction; scratching leads to excoriations, bleeding, and dark crusts, with residual hyperpigmented patches and thickened, lichenified skin around older lesions. New bumps continue to appear while older ones sometimes flatten but leave dark marks and small scars. She reports poor sleep from nocturnal itching, daytime fatigue, and increased anxiety about appearance. She denies fever, chills, weight loss from poor appetite, new mouth sores, generalized jaundice, new joint swelling, shortness of breath, chest pain, or a new widespread blistering rash; no known exposure to scabies in close contacts.

current_general_status.diet: Irregular meals due to unstable housing; relies on shelter meals when available; takes a daily multivitamin.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with frequent nighttime awakenings due to itching; naps during the day when possible.

persona: She is a 67-year-old woman who works multiple jobs, including coordinating programs for a philanthropic foundation, and she values dignity and privacy when discussing personal health. Calm and serene in demeanor, she tends to make decisions only after she has read a lot and weighed options carefully, though she is a novice with biology and sometimes gets overwhelmed by medical terms. In conversation she is a poor historian, describing outbreaks as "weird" or "off" and mixing up timelines, so she often gestures broadly and needs gentle, specific follow-up questions. Outside of work, she likes war fiction, follows a high-protein weight-management routine, and keeps a discipline-focused approach with her family.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent clusters of painful, itchy blisters on the lip margin with occasional genital soreness, most recently flared 5 days ago after a period of intense work stress.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: For several years she has experienced intermittent outbreaks starting with tingling/burning and itching, followed within 12-24 hours by grouped small clear vesicles. Lesions typically occur at the lip border and around the nostrils; during some flares she also notes external genital itching and stinging discomfort with urination when the skin is irritated. Vesicles rupture easily, leaving shallow erosions that crust over and heal in about 7-10 days; recurrences have generally been milder than the earliest episodes but have become more frequent during periods of sleep deprivation and high work pressure, estimated at approximately 5-6 outbreaks in the past 12 months. Associated symptoms during the current flare include localized tenderness and mild fatigue. She denies persistent fever, generalized rash, new oral ulcers throughout the mouth, eye pain/redness or vision changes, severe headache, neck stiffness, flank pain, or malodorous vaginal discharge.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite slightly decreased during painful episodes; otherwise regular diet and enjoys cooking as a stress reliever.

She values charity and keeping family traditions alive, and she stays connected with a circle of veterans through community events. She is careful about medicines, avoids unnecessary antibiotics, and often helps with vaccine education, though her busy schedule means she sometimes relies on quick meals and her sleep suffers during flare-ups.

She denies high fever, shaking chills, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, calf pain/swelling, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, or recent new pet exposures. In the past month he notes numbness and burning pain in the feet with occasional tingling in the hands, and foamy urine with mild ankle swelling. Accompanying symptoms include reduced appetite, early satiety, easy bruising, and occasional lightheadedness. He denies acute shaking chills with rigors, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, persistent focal abdominal pain, severe headache or neck stiffness, persistent vomiting or diarrhea, dysuria, gross hematuria, new oral ulcers, or recent travel outside the endemic work region; no known recent sick contacts.

present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Two days ago, while walking in hot weather in her neighborhood (urban heat island effect), she developed abrupt lightheadedness and a brief period of "graying out" vision lasting under a minute, requiring her to sit down. Since then she has had intermittent episodes of dizziness and marked fatigue with reduced exercise tolerance, along with occasional awareness of an irregular heartbeat and mild chest pressure on exertion that resolves with rest. She reports feeling "slowed down" and occasionally mildly short of breath when climbing stairs. Symptoms are worse with exertion and heat; she has tried increasing fluids with partial relief. No fever, no cough, no pleuritic pain, no leg swelling, no hemoptysis, no focal weakness, no facial droop, no persistent vertigo with room-spinning sensation, and no new severe headache. Denies black stools or overt bleeding. No recent new medications, but she was told at a recent lab check that her potassium was high and has been more cautious with diet since.

She reports tender, red nodules on both shins over the last week and intermittent swelling and aching of the right knee that flares in the evening. She notes low-grade fatigue and subjective low fever without chills. No sore throat, no cough, no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no focal weakness, no confusion, no severe headache, no neck stiffness, no persistent diarrhea, no black or bloody stools, and no dysuria or abnormal vaginal discharge.

persona: She is a 48-year-old single clinical pharmacist who recently relocated for work and usually values work-life balance, though she often pushes herself to return quickly after major life changes. Financially comfortable and generally open and transparent when asked directly, she becomes noticeably anxious when she has to explain things on the spot and tends to describe symptoms as “weird” or “off,” mixing up timelines and gesturing broadly rather than naming specifics. She keeps a mostly sedentary routine outside of occasional tennis or golf, is focused on postpartum recovery exercises, and appreciates a trauma-informed, nonjudgmental tone that helps her stay engaged. present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 8 months she has noted progressively higher home blood pressure readings despite adherence to prescribed therapy, with intermittent throbbing occipital headaches, lightheadedness, and occasional blurred vision. Over the past 3 weeks she reports more frequent chest tightness with palpitations during exertion and two episodes of nausea without vomiting. She also describes intermittent dull left flank discomfort that comes and goes, not clearly related to position. She notes increased nighttime urination and mild ankle swelling by evening. She denies fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, syncope, focal weakness/numbness, severe tearing back pain, dysuria, gross hematuria, or recent NSAID overuse. She reports occasional muscle cramps and generalized weakness but no episodic paralysis. Recent urinalysis showed mild proteinuria (1+), microscopic hematuria (RBC ~5/HPF), and low specific gravity (1.006); in the setting of a small echogenic left kidney and severe left renal artery stenosis, these findings are felt most consistent with chronic ischemic nephropathy/concurrent chronic kidney disease, with alternative intrinsic parenchymal etiologies (e.g., primary glomerulonephritis/interstitial nephritis) considered less likely given the current imaging and clinical context and absence of systemic/inflammatory features.

persona: She is a 74-year-old retired-leaning risk management advisor who still takes occasional consulting calls and prefers handling scheduling and follow-ups by text because phone conversations make her feel flustered. In visits she speaks quickly and at length, jumping between her sleep troubles, aches, headlines she has read, and worries that something serious is being missed, often asking for reassurance and clarity. With limited access to consistent healthcare and a bit of a language barrier, she asks for a conceptual explanation of what might be happening and why, while also emphasizing that she wants to avoid unnecessary tests. She values respect and patience, especially for older adults.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradual onset of widespread body aches with worsening fatigue and non-restorative sleep over the past 10 months, prompting evaluation during a group therapy session.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Reports diffuse musculoskeletal aching and soreness involving the neck, shoulders, upper back, lower back, hips, and thighs, described as a constant heaviness with intermittent flares that can be triggered by stress or poor sleep. Over the past several months, fatigue has become more prominent, with low daytime energy and feeling unrefreshed on waking despite adequate time in bed. Notes episodes of "brain fog" with slowed thinking and difficulty concentrating, especially on days after poor sleep. Also endorses intermittent tension-type headaches and alternating constipation and loose stools without blood. Describes anticipatory worry about what the evaluation might show and feels more irritable and down during pain flares. Denies fevers, chills, unintentional significant weight loss, focal joint swelling, new rashes, persistent morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, new weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder incontinence, chest pain, shortness of breath, or recent acute infection symptoms. Acknowledges that recent abnormal laboratory findings (anemia and elevated inflammatory markers/serologies) are not explained by fibromyalgia alone; fibromyalgia is a clinical diagnosis that may coexist with other conditions, and these abnormalities will be evaluated in parallel for possible inflammatory/autoimmune or other underlying causes.

persona: She is a 20-year-old student who prioritizes education and approaches decisions with an entrepreneurial, problem-solving mindset, but she can get easily disappointed when symptoms derail her plans. She communicates in a direct, factual way, describing what hurts, when it starts, and what helps in simple terms, and she often asks for trustworthy links or evidence to guide next steps. present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent cramping lower abdominal pain starting with menses for the past 5 years, worse over the last 6 months and most intense on day 1 of bleeding.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Pain begins within a few hours of menstrual onset and is spasmodic, centered in the suprapubic area, sometimes radiating to the low back and inner thighs. Over the past 6 months the cramps have become more severe and are accompanied by nausea, loose stools, dizziness, fatigue, and occasional cold sweating; she often needs to lie down and misses classes/work on day 1. Menstrual flow is described as heavy for the first 2 days with passage of clots and frequent pad changes; symptoms typically improve by day 2 and resolve by day 3. She reports prolonged sitting for school/work and long drives, which seem to worsen pelvic pressure and cramping. She has tried heating pads and over-the-counter ibuprofen with partial relief when taken after pain begins, but less benefit if she delays dosing. No fever, no abnormal vaginal discharge, no foul odor, no intermenstrual bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no persistent pelvic pain outside of menses, no urinary burning, and no pain with bowel movements outside the menstrual period.

Parents report the events are more likely after sleep deprivation or irregular sleep. She has a history of remote neurologic injury (perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury and a remote childhood stroke), but there has been no new or recent head trauma. No persistent fever, no cough or breathing difficulty, no chest pain, no cyanosis between events, no prolonged focal weakness after episodes, and no known toxin or medication ingestion.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Cyanosis and respiratory distress shortly after birth; episodes of tachycardia confirmed on ECG/monitoring.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Term newborn developed central cyanosis and increased work of breathing within hours after birth. Caregivers noted intermittent episodes of rapid heart rate; subsequent monitoring/ECG confirmed intermittent supraventricular tachycardia (up to ~220 bpm). Symptoms fluctuate with feeding/handling, with persistent murmur and tachypnea.

medical_history.previous_diseases: No prenatally diagnosed structural heart disease; congenital tricuspid/right-heart abnormality suspected/identified on postnatal evaluation after onset of cyanosis and murmur.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Newborn developed bluish discoloration of lips and increased work of breathing shortly after birth (within the first 2 hours of life).
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Male infant born at term who became progressively cyanotic with intermittent rapid breathing and mild chest retractions in the first hours after delivery. Feeding attempts are poor with easy fatigue and sweating; mother notes the infant tires quickly at the breast/bottle and appears more dusky when crying. Periods of irritability alternate with lethargy. Episodes of fast heartbeat are suspected by caregivers based on visible precordial activity and brief pallor, though exact rhythm is not documented. No fever, no cough, no nasal congestion, no vomiting or diarrhea, and no rash. No history of choking or aspiration event. Urine output is present but slightly decreased since birth; no blood in stool.

current_general_status.diet: Breastfeeding/bottle attempts with reduced intake due to fatigue; requires frequent pauses.
current_general_status.sleep: More sleepy than expected for age; difficult to keep awake for feeds.
current_general_status.excretion: Passed meconium; urine output present but fewer wet diapers than expected in first day of life.
current_general_status.weight_change: Too early to assess meaningful weight change since birth.

previous_diseases: Congenital malformation of the tricuspid valve/right heart present from birth

persona: He is a 17-year-old student and the first in his family aiming for college, usually quiet with strangers but more open around a small, steady group of friends. He speaks in a direct, factual way, answering questions plainly and expecting clear plans; he can be a bit hard to satisfy because he double-checks details and worries about falling behind. He values healthy habits and tries to stay active within his limits, using simple sound-based relaxation techniques when his back acts up. He’s motivated by long-term goals like investigation work and is interested in community preparedness for health risks tied to climate and disasters.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Lifelong short stature with disproportionate limb shortening and intermittent back discomfort, with increasing self-noted posture changes over the past 2 years.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: The patient reports chronic, lifelong slow linear growth compared with peers and long-standing disproportionate shortening of the upper arms and thighs, with difficulty keeping up in sports due to short stride and a waddling gait. Over the last 2 years he has noticed more frequent mid-to-lower back aching after prolonged walking and carrying a backpack, and family has commented on a more pronounced curve at the upper low back and a more prominent abdomen and buttocks. He also describes occasional tingling in the legs when sitting for long periods, which resolves after changing position. He notes mild speech/articulation strain when speaking quickly and intermittent snoring. He denies acute limb pain, joint redness or warmth, fever, chronic cough, chest pain, syncope, progressive weakness, bowel or bladder incontinence, severe headaches, or vision loss.

current_general_status.diet: High-salt, mostly home-prepared foods when available; appetite generally good.
current_general_status.sleep: Often sleeps 6 to 7 hours; intermittent snoring; no witnessed apneas reported by patient.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel movements and urination; no constipation or urinary retention.
current_general_status.weight_change: Stable weight over the past 6 months.

previous_diseases: Recurrent otitis media in early childhood (resolved)
Intermittent low back pain associated with posture and activity
medical_history.long_term_medication: None currently. Previously trialed growth-promoting therapy during childhood per family report, discontinued after limited perceived benefit.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: None. No prior limb-lengthening or spinal surgery. No major trauma.
infectious_diseases: Childhood viral upper respiratory infections (no complications)
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per caregiver report, including routine childhood immunizations.

personal_history.smoking: Denies tobacco or vaping.
personal_history.alcohol: Denies alcohol use.
personal_history.obstetric_history: Birth history: delivered at term per caregiver report. Pregnancy notable for maternal exposure to ionizing radiation and use of certain prescribed medications during pregnancy; these exposures are reported as historical context only and are not considered etiologic. No known prenatal infections. Neonatal course uncomplicated, but disproportionate limb shortening and larger head size were noted in infancy. Achondroplasia is caused by a pathogenic variant in FGFR3 (often occurring de novo/spontaneously), and no causal link between the reported prenatal exposures and achondroplasia has been established.

family_history.similar_illness: None known in first- or second-degree relatives; no relatives with disproportionate short stature reported.
family_history.hereditary_diseases: None known.

persona: She is a 33-year-old woman with trisomy 21 who lives with intermittent support from household members for logistics and medical follow-up but can communicate her symptoms clearly and works with structured routines. She has been under intense stress recently, with low self-esteem and unstable housing after a financial collapse, and she worries that every new symptom means something catastrophic. She talks fast and at length, interrupting with fears about cancer or suddenly stopping breathing, then circles back to small details like the exact supplement label, her sleep medication timing, and what she read online. Despite her circumstances, she values charity and tries to give what she can, monitors sugar closely, and aims for high-protein meals that fit her vegan routine. She dreams of becoming a musician and wants steady reassurance while she explains everything.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of noticeable tongue swelling with slurred speech and difficulty swallowing starting about 18 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She reports a baseline tendency toward a slightly large tongue for years (consistent with trisomy 21/hypothyroidism) without prior functional limitation, but states the current episode represents a clear, abrupt increase in tongue size. After returning from work and taking a new over-the-counter supplement and an as-needed medication for sleep prescribed recently, her tongue began to feel abruptly larger; within several hours she noticed new scalloped tooth impressions along the tongue edges, a tight foreign-body sensation in the mouth, and increasing difficulty articulating words. Swallowing solids became uncomfortable and she started switching to liquids and soft foods; she also notes mild burning discomfort and increased drooling. Overnight she experienced intermittent shortness of breath when lying flat and loud snoring per a household member, which improved when sitting up, and she feels the tongue fullness has continued to progress since onset. No fever, no sore throat, no generalized hives, no wheezing, no lip or eyelid swelling, no chest pain, no recent dental procedure, and no recent upper respiratory infection symptoms. She denies tongue trauma or biting, and denies ingestion of caustics. Symptoms are more prominent than her usual baseline tongue size.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced intake since onset; tolerating cool liquids and pureed foods; normally vegan and avoids alcohol.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep since onset due to discomfort and positional breathing difficulty; also reports insomnia related to recent bereavement.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination; no diarrhea or constipation reported.
current_general_status.weight_change: No clear recent weight change; decreased intake over the last day.

previous_diseases: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) diagnosed in childhood
History of mild obstructive sleep symptoms in the past year (not formally studied)
Benign tongue mass noted on dental exam 2 months ago with pending specialist evaluation

Recently started intermittent sedative-hypnotic for insomnia (taken 2 nights in the last week) and began a new herbal supplement 2 days ago; reports possible workplace exposure to lab solvents/cleaning agents with inconsistent PPE use during a recent project.

persona: She is a 23-year-old immigrant support worker who takes pride in healthy living, relying on simple home-cooked meals and practical routines while also enjoying looking put-together. She helps cover expenses for her household and is raising one child in her care (not biologically hers), but admits she struggles with technology and often needs things explained step-by-step. She speaks in a direct, factual way, using everyday words to describe what she feels and answering questions clearly without guessing. The sudden symptoms leave her aghast at first, then quietly reflective, especially about hygiene and staying well for her family; she also has a knack for languages and African scripts.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of urinary urgency and burning with urination starting 2 days ago after sexual intercourse.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 48 hours she developed a sudden, compelling need to urinate with frequent small voids and a mild burning/irritated sensation in the urethra during urination. Symptoms have persisted and are worst in the evenings, with waking to urinate 2 to 3 times nightly. She reports suprapubic pressure and a sense of pelvic heaviness, and had 1 episode of small urine leakage when she could not reach the bathroom in time. She feels very tense about the symptoms and is worried about hygiene and hospital-acquired infections, which has increased her anxiety and made it harder to sleep. No fever or chills, no flank pain, no nausea/vomiting, no visible blood in urine, no abnormal vaginal discharge or foul odor, and no new genital sores. No recent new medications or chemical irritants noted. She describes intermittent difficulty starting the urine stream and a sensation of urethral tightness/spasm during voiding.

current_general_status.diet: Plant-based friendly; appetite slightly decreased due to discomfort.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor; chronic insomnia related to stress, worse since symptom onset.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination frequent with urgency and occasional hesitancy; bowel movements regular.
current_general_status.weight_change: No noticeable recent weight change.

previous_diseases: Intermittent functional urinary symptoms during periods of high stress (self-reported, previously self-limited)

persona: He is a 32-year-old master electrician and IIT Delhi alumnus who manages a few inherited land parcels, paying out of pocket and preferring to keep dealings straightforward. In conversation he stays stoic and downplays discomfort, insisting he is "fine" and only showing up because a family member pushed him, though the strain of being separated leaves him with a quiet sense of loss. Sociable when relaxed but distrustful of institutions, he values honesty and transparency and responds best to clear explanations, especially since he can mix up similar medication names. He is also outspoken about ship safety standards and practical injury prevention.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Severe pain over the left shoulder blade with inability to lift the left arm after a traffic accident and fall from height about 3 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: The patient reports abrupt onset of intense posterior shoulder and upper back pain immediately after being thrown from a height during a traffic accident, landing on the left upper back/shoulder region. Pain is sharp and deep, worsens with arm movement and deep breaths, and he has been unable to abduct or elevate the left arm due to pain and weakness. He noticed rapid swelling and bruising over the shoulder blade area and feels grinding/clicking with small shoulder movements. Accompanying symptoms include mild shortness of breath from pain, nausea, and lightheadedness when standing; given the mechanism and pleuritic component, associated thoracic injury (e.g., rib fracture, pneumothorax/hemothorax) is a concern to be assessed. He reports tingling over the outer left shoulder and upper arm but can still move his fingers and wrist. He denies loss of consciousness, persistent headache, vomiting, chest pressure, coughing up blood, abdominal pain, or new neck pain; no fever or recent illness.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite since the injury due to pain and nausea; tolerating small sips of water.
current_general_status.sleep: Unable to sleep since the injury because of severe pain when lying on the back or left side.
current_general_status.excretion: No urination difficulty reported since injury; no bowel movement since the event (short duration).
current_general_status.weight_change: No recent weight change reported.

persona: She is a 17-year-old student from an emigrant family who tries to balance school, training, and helping out with expenses in small, informal ways when she can. She speaks politely and wants to keep her dignity, but she tends to describe things as "weird" or "off" and mixes up the timeline, gesturing to a broad area rather than pinpointing the exact spot. Reflective but often pessimistic when stressed, she worries about falling behind and sleeps lightly, decompressing by putting on headphones and listening to music. She is plant-based friendly and tries to make thoughtful food choices.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of pain and swelling just above the right heel starting immediately after a sprint training session 5 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She developed sharp pain at the back of the ankle a few centimeters above the heel during repeated sprint starts and jumping drills after an inadequate warm-up. Pain is worse with running, jumping, and push-off and has progressed to pain with fast walking and stairs. She notes morning stiffness that eases slightly after a few minutes of walking but returns with activity; the area feels tight and tender to touch, with mild localized swelling and occasional warmth/redness after practice. She denies a popping sensation, sudden calf weakness, inability to bear weight, ankle instability, numbness/tingling, fever, chills, skin wounds, or pain centered on the ankle joint itself.

current_general_status.diet: Regular diet, but tends to choose mostly plant-forward/plant-based friendly meals; tries to eat balanced meals though appetite is mildly decreased due to stress and pain.
current_general_status.sleep: Light sleep with difficulty falling asleep due to worrying about missing school sports; wakes once at night from discomfort when turning in bed.

persona: She is an 88-year-old widowed woman, a retired adjunct faculty member with a mid-income, preparedness-minded approach to daily life and a long-held vegetarian diet. Opinionated and detail-oriented, she arrives with printed notes and confidently insists she has already identified the problem online, pushing for a specific scan or “the same reduction as last time,” and brushing off details that do not match her theory. Since her stroke left her with residual weakness and her spouse’s death has deepened her anxiety, she relies on prayer and quiet spiritual reflection to steady herself while preparing to relocate to a new city, expecting respectful, elder-aware communication from her care team.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of jaw locking open with inability to close the mouth after yawning, most recently occurring about 2 hours ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past year, she has had intermittent sudden episodes triggered by excessive mouth opening (yawning, laughing, singing at family gatherings, or taking large bites), during which the jaw feels stuck open with immediate pain in front of both ears, drooling, muffled/unclear speech, and difficulty chewing and swallowing. Episodes were initially every few months but have increased to several times per week, and she now avoids talking and supports the chin with her hand due to fear of recurrence. The current episode began after a wide yawn; she has persistent inability to close the mouth, aching pain and tightness in the jaw muscles, and mild swelling/tenderness around the joints. She reports that a similar episode was reduced in an urgent care setting months ago but she did not keep the bandage/immobilization on for long because it was uncomfortable, and recurrences became more frequent afterward. She also notes one prior event after an external force to the mandible while her mouth was open (accidental bump from a caregiver while assisting with oral hygiene). She has had prolonged wide mouth opening during dental procedures in the past, which she believes has triggered symptoms. She denies fever, sore throat, tooth abscess symptoms, facial numbness, recent head trauma, chest pain, shortness of breath, new focal weakness, or loss of consciousness.

current_general_status.diet: Soft foods and liquids recently due to fear of triggering jaw locking; decreased appetite since bereavement.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with difficulty falling asleep related to anxiety and grief; daytime fatigue.
current_general_status.excretion: Chronic constipation; no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional mild weight loss over the last few months.

persona: He is a newborn boy whose caregivers live with tight finances and try hard to do the right thing, guided by a strong sense of justice, forgiveness, and quiet spiritual reflection. They are easily moved and protective, especially because he is followed by a specialist for immune problems, and they keep a close eye on wet diapers, feeds, and how alert he seems. When speaking with clinicians they are direct and factual, using simple words like “threw up,” “runny poop,” and “fewer wet diapers,” and they answer questions clearly without rambling. The family has also faced being victims of a crime, which makes them cautious about safety and community issues.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Acute onset of repeated vomiting followed by watery diarrhea and low-grade fever starting about 24 hours ago during the autumn/winter season.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Per caregiver report, the infant was well until yesterday when he suddenly began vomiting multiple times with decreased interest in feeds, then developed frequent watery, yellow-green loose stools over the following hours. He has had a low-grade fever and increasing fussiness/lethargy. Caregivers note fewer wet diapers and darker urine, with dry lips and mildly sunken eyes suggesting dehydration. CBC leukocytosis may be stress/dehydration-related, but given underlying immunodeficiency, monitor closely for possible bacterial coinfection and reassess promptly if higher/persistent fever, blood in stool, worsening lethargy, or clinical deterioration. No blood or mucus in stool, no bilious vomiting, no projectile vomiting, no persistent abdominal distension, no rash, no cough or breathing difficulty, and no known toxin/medication ingestion.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced oral intake; taking smaller, less frequent formula/breast milk feeds and occasionally vomits after feeds.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep since onset; more irritable and wakes frequently.
current_general_status.excretion: Frequent watery stools; decreased urine output with fewer wet diapers than usual.
current_general_status.weight_change: No documented weight change yet; caregivers report he appears slightly less plump since symptoms began.

previous_diseases: Immunodeficiency diagnosed in early infancy (under specialist follow-up)

persona: He is a 2-year-old boy from a high-income household where routines, healthy living, and a low-carb-leaning approach to family meals are emphasized. His parents stay calm and effectiveness-oriented when making decisions, but they are vague and scattered when describing what feels “off,” often mixing up timelines and using general words that make details hard to pin down. They’ve been practicing mindfulness and stress-management strategies to cope with appointments and his communication frustrations. There’s no history of major surgeries for him, and he is not on daily medicines, though the family relies on reminders for follow-through.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Parents report the child has seemed not to respond to sounds on the right side and has delayed speech development; symptoms have been present since infancy but have become more clearly noticed/recognized over the past ~10 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Parents describe reduced response to calling his name and environmental sounds, especially when approached from the right, with better response when sounds are on the left. They report the hearing concern likely began in early infancy (present since infancy) but was recognized more clearly and became more concerning over the past ~10 months; it has not shown a clear progressive worsening. The child sometimes startles to loud noises but often does not localize quieter sounds. Associated concerns include delayed expressive speech and frustration during group play or when there is background noise. No ear pain, no fever, no ear discharge, no recurrent upper respiratory infections, no balance problems, and no recent head trauma. No persistent tinnitus behaviors noted (no frequent ear pulling or distress in quiet settings). No history suggesting acute otitis media episodes.

current_general_status.sleep: Generally sleeps through the night; occasional difficulty settling when routines change.

persona: He is an 8-year-old student who recently moved to a new school in Berlin and does best when routines feel steady and family is close by. He and his caregivers speak in a direct, factual way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, and they strongly prefer privacy and confidentiality when discussing anything sensitive. At home he can seem tired from rough sleep and may get irritable, especially on stressful school days, but he is cooperative and wants practical steps that help. He relaxes with light humor and does better when adults communicate calmly and consistently.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent involuntary facial movements and throat-clearing sounds for about 6 months, worse over the last 4 weeks, evaluated via video consultation.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months, caregivers report repeated brief episodes of frequent eye blinking and facial grimacing with intermittent shoulder shrugging, along with recurring throat clearing and sniffing sounds that the child says he "can't stop." Symptoms wax and wane but have increased in frequency during the past month after starting a new school environment in Berlin, with noticeable worsening during homework, test situations, or when he feels anxious or excited, and partial improvement when relaxed or distracted (especially while watching a comedy show). Child describes an uncomfortable "urge" or tight feeling around the eyes/throat before episodes with short relief after the movement/sound. Teachers note reduced attention span and distractibility during class when symptoms are prominent. Associated symptoms include tension, irritability, and occasional trouble falling asleep; no loss of consciousness, no rhythmic jerking, no generalized weakness, no persistent headache, no fever, no recent head trauma, no vision loss, no sore throat, and no cough productive of sputum. Family reports symptoms are more noticeable after intake of central nervous system stimulants (caffeinated soda/energy drinks) and on days with higher psychological stress.

current_general_status.diet: Picky eater with low appetite; tends to skip breakfast on school days; drinks caffeinated soda on some afternoons.
current_general_status.sleep: Difficulty initiating sleep 2 to 3 nights per week; working on a consistent bedtime routine and limiting screens in the evening.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal bowel and bladder habits.

persona: At 37, she presents as competent and self-contained, thriving professionally and preferring to keep life steady for her kids and extended family. She speaks in clipped, minimizing phrases, insisting it is "nothing" and offering details only when pressed, admitting she came in mainly because a relative would not drop it. She works with data by day and keeps a quiet side interest in visual design, often unwinding with meditation and NPR on her commute, and she tends to describe events in a suspenseful, almost awestruck way while still trying to preserve harmony and avoid burdening anyone.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually worsening right-sided nasal blockage with intermittent blood-streaked nasal discharge for about 6 months, with recurrent flares over the past 6 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 months she has noticed progressive nasal obstruction (right greater than left) with a persistent foreign-body sensation and intermittent nasal pruritus. Over the past 6 weeks, symptoms have become more frequent with episodes of minor epistaxis, especially after nose blowing, and she reports a new sensation of a "soft growth" inside the right nostril that bleeds easily when touched. She endorses reduced sense of smell and occasional post-nasal drip, but denies fever, facial swelling, severe facial pain, dental pain, purulent foul-smelling discharge, trauma, easy bruising/bleeding elsewhere, weight loss, night sweats, chronic cough, wheeze, or dyspnea. No eye redness, conjunctival mass, excessive tearing, or purulent ocular discharge. No urinary bleeding, dysuria, or urethral mass. No skin nodules or verrucous lesions noticed. Associated symptoms include irritability, reduced appetite for solid foods, and occasional headache on the same side. No fever, no facial swelling, no pus drainage noticed by caregiver, no recent cough or sore throat, no ear discharge, and no vomiting or neck stiffness.

Rest and reduced activity improve symptoms; running uphill, longer distance, and speed work worsen them. present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent sudden episodes of rapid pounding heartbeat with shortness of breath and fatigue, worsening over the past 3 months; most recent episode started abruptly while resting 2 days ago and stopped suddenly after about 20 minutes.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last year he has had intermittent spells of abrupt-onset palpitations described as a fast regular pounding in the chest, often accompanied by chest tightness, shortness of breath on minimal exertion, lightheadedness, and marked fatigue. Episodes initially occurred every few months but have become more frequent over the past 3 months, now weekly, sometimes triggered by exertion, emotional stress, or nicotine use, and occasionally occurring at rest; the rapid heartbeat typically stops as suddenly as it starts. Between episodes he notes reduced exercise tolerance compared with his baseline and increasing pallor, with occasional dizziness and blurred vision when standing. During the worst spells he has felt near-fainting but denies complete loss of consciousness. He denies fever, productive cough, wheezing, pleuritic chest pain, leg swelling, calf pain, focal neurologic deficits, or sudden severe headache. He denies black tarry stools, vomiting blood, or visible blood in urine, and denies recent major trauma. No recent new stimulant medications or over-the-counter decongestants. He reports some symptoms compatible with thyrotoxicosis over recent months (intermittent heat intolerance, fine tremor, increased sweating, and increased anxiety/irritability), without prominent diarrhea or clear weight loss with increased appetite.

At baseline after left congenital cataract surgery, the child had some reduced left-eye visual function/amblyopia risk but was able to visually attend and track; the current change is a new decline in left-eye tracking/visual attention with new light sensitivity and increased rubbing. The child has been more clingy during the day, but there is no obvious severe eye pain. No purulent discharge, no significant tearing, and no eyelid swelling. No fever, cough, runny nose, vomiting, diarrhea, or rash. No known recent head/eye injury. No reported foreign body exposure. No generalized lethargy. Caregivers deny a visible white pupil reflection in photos and deny sudden complete vision loss.

persona: He is a 58-year-old man who restores motorcycles and is gradually transitioning toward retirement, staying quietly cooperative in most interactions while remaining intensely focused when a problem needs solving. He cares deeply about peace, justice, and equality, and he gets excited by new tools or techniques that might improve his work, but caregiving duties at home often squeeze his schedule and add stress. When describing how he feels, he tends to be a poor historian, using words like "weird" or "off" with a muddled timeline and broad gestures, and he sometimes mixes up similar medication names. Cooking helps him unwind.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of palpitations with intermittent chest tightness and shortness of breath over the past 8 months, worsening over the last 3 weeks after moving to a new home; also developed a persistent cough for 2 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 8 months, the patient reports intermittent sudden-onset "skipped beats" and brief fluttering sensations, sometimes followed by a momentary pause and a strong beat, occurring at rest and with exertion. Episodes have gradually become more frequent and longer, now several times daily over the last 3 weeks, occasionally associated with chest tightness or pressure and exertional shortness of breath, with reduced exercise tolerance and fatigue. He also notes occasional lightheadedness when episodes are frequent but denies true spinning vertigo. Accompanying symptoms include easy fatigability, mild ankle swelling by evening on some days, and anxiety during palpitations. He developed a persistent, mostly dry cough for 2 weeks without hemoptysis; given the CXR showing mild pulmonary vascular congestion, this is felt possibly related to volume overload/heart failure, though alternative causes (viral illness/bronchitis, smoking-related irritation, medication-related cough such as ACE-inhibitor use) should be reviewed. He denies fever, sore throat, pleuritic chest pain, wheezing, focal consolidation symptoms, unilateral leg swelling, recent prolonged immobilization, or known sick contacts. He denies sustained crushing chest pain lasting more than 20 minutes, vomiting, or new focal neurologic deficits. No complete loss of consciousness, though he describes one near-blackout episode while climbing stairs 1 month ago that resolved with rest. He reports that poor sleep and stress during the move appear to trigger symptoms; caffeine occasionally worsens palpitations. Cardiac enzymes are mildly elevated (troponin I 0.06, CK-MB 28); with no sustained ischemic chest pain and ECG described as showing pathologic Q waves consistent with prior infarction without documented new dynamic ischemic changes, this may represent chronic myocardial injury in ischemic cardiomyopathy versus a small acute NSTEMI, so serial troponins (trend) and clinical correlation are indicated to clarify the working assessment.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite fair; tries to self-manage with dietary changes but often relies on convenience foods during work and since the move; attempting to reduce salt and saturated fat.
current_general_status.sleep: Sleep fragmented with frequent awakenings; palpitations and cough sometimes disrupt sleep; averages about 5 to 6 hours nightly.

Takes low-dose antiplatelet therapy as previously prescribed after the heart event. Uses occasional over-the-counter cough suppressant for the last week.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No major surgery. Reports medical trauma related to a prior painful procedure performed without what he felt was adequate anesthesia, leading to anxiety around invasive testing.

persona: She is a bright, energetic preschooler in a high-income household where education is a big focus, and her mom runs the home on clear rules and routines. Her mother is friendly and extroverted but extremely anxious, speaking fast and at length, jumping from daycare updates to a news story about genetic conditions, and interrupting to ask if this could be cancer or something life-threatening. Even while admiring how calmly clinicians explain things, she seeks constant reassurance and wants practical next steps, like having documentation ready for preschool and making any refills as convenient as possible. She also chats about caring for national park ecosystems and is very mindful about preventing falls at home.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Mother reports multiple light-brown flat patches on the child's skin noticed since infancy, with gradual increase in size and a few new spots over the past 8 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Several well-demarcated, smooth, flat tan-to-light brown patches are present on the trunk and face; caregivers state the spots were first noticed shortly after birth and have slowly enlarged as the child has grown, with a small increase in number recently. No itching, pain, scaling, bleeding, or ulceration. No recent new soaps/medications associated with the changes. No fevers, weight loss, chronic cough, or night sweats. No blistering, vesicles, or rapidly spreading rash. Caregivers deny episodes of facial swelling, hives, or wheezing. Caregivers have also noticed a few freckles in the armpit/groin region and are concerned about whether this could signal an underlying inherited condition.

current_general_status.diet: Age-appropriate diet with mildly picky eating; drinks milk and water well.
current_general_status.sleep: Generally sleeps through the night; occasional difficulty settling at bedtime.

persona: He is a serious-minded 6-year-old student who tries hard to be brave, but lately feels a bit dissatisfied and keeps wishing his mouth would stop hurting. When asked questions, he answers in simple, direct terms like, “My gums bleed when I brush,” and “They look red and puffy,” and he doesn’t add extra details unless prompted. His caregiver is practical and careful about medicines, preferring to avoid antibiotics unless they are truly needed, and they are attentive to his diabetes routine. Money is tight at home, so they rely on taxis for appointments and try to make food choices that are gentle on his mouth.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent gum bleeding noted during toothbrushing and when biting harder foods for the past 3 months.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last few months, the caregiver reports intermittent bleeding on the toothbrush and occasional blood on foods like apples, with gums that look red and puffy around several teeth. Symptoms fluctuate but have become more frequent in the past 3–4 weeks, especially when brushing near the gumline. Child also has occasional bad breath and mild gum tenderness with chewing, but no severe toothache. No fever, no facial swelling, no difficulty swallowing, no mouth ulcers, no recent trauma to the mouth, and no purulent drainage noticed at home.

current_general_status.diet: Picky eater with low intake of meat and fresh fruits/vegetables; prefers soft, starchy foods; occasional sweet snacks.
current_general_status.sleep: Generally sleeps through the night; mild restlessness on days with mouth discomfort.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements.

persona: He is a 53-year-old middle-class film blogger with a science-leaning, applied-physics mindset who also advocates loudly for accessible technology. He speaks fast and at length, jumping from his symptoms to what he read online, how his sugar tracking has been off lately, and even a recent cricket match, repeatedly interrupting to ask if this could be a stroke, cancer, or something fatal. Though he can come off cynical when stressed, he values justice and forgiveness and tries to regain control with deep breathing, light aerobics when able, and practical planning around his limited ability to travel to appointments.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of involuntary neck twisting and head tilt with severe neck pain starting about 8 hours ago after a physically demanding day, prompting an emergency department visit. No known recent exposure to dopamine-blocking medications (e.g., metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, antipsychotics) in the 72 hours prior to onset.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Patient reports abrupt episodes of involuntary turning of the head to the right with intermittent leftward tilt and brief backward pulling, initially occurring every few minutes and becoming more frequent and sustained over the last several hours. Spasms worsen with standing, walking, and attempts to hold the head straight, and slightly improve when he supports his chin with his hand and when he focuses on slow deep-breathing exercises. He notes tightness and cramping pain in the right side of the neck and upper shoulder with soreness over the trapezius area and a mild tension-type headache. He endorses marked anxiety with panic symptoms (palpitations, trembling, sense of impending doom) since symptom onset and fear of needles/procedures in the ED. He specifically denies taking any new or recent PRN/ED-administered antiemetics or antipsychotics (including metoclopramide or prochlorperazine), no recent dose changes of SSRIs, and no stimulant or recreational drug use in the 72 hours prior to onset; no known toxin exposure. He denies recent fever, sore throat, cough, rash, limb weakness, facial droop, slurred speech, numbness, gait instability, diplopia, vision loss, hearing loss, ear pain, recent head/neck trauma, loss of consciousness, chest pain, shortness of breath, or bowel/bladder dysfunction.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite since onset due to pain and anxiety; able to take fluids.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor sleep last night due to discomfort and repeated spasms.
current_general_status.excretion: No change; normal urination and bowel movements.
current_general_status.weight_change: No recent unintentional weight change reported.

Generalized anxiety with episodic panic symptoms
Chronic tension-type headaches

medical_history.blood_transfusion: Received transfusion at the time of amputation surgery (remote).

persona: He is a 13-year-old student who tends to keep to himself, but stays polite and non-confrontational when talking with adults. When asked about how he feels, he answers in a direct, factual way using simple words, focusing on what changed, when it started, and what makes it worse, without trying to sound "medical." His family is financially comfortable from owning land, and he cares a lot about fairness and forgiveness, so he expects respectful, culturally sensitive care. He likes getting clear written handouts to follow at home, but he sometimes forgets paperwork and routine tracking.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of high fever with sore throat and cough over the past 6 weeks, with the latest flare starting 2 days ago after returning to a crowded school dormitory.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 6 weeks he has had 3 similar episodes of abrupt fever that rises within hours (often peaking around 39 C), marked fatigue, headache, and myalgias. Between episodes, fever typically resolved and he returned close to baseline for several days, though a mild residual cough/congestion sometimes lingered before the next flare. The current episode began 2 days ago with chills followed by rapid-onset fever, severe sore throat with painful swallowing, tender neck swelling consistent with enlarged cervical nodes, nasal congestion with clear rhinorrhea, and a dry cough that has become more frequent and intermittently productive compared with prior days; sleep is disrupted by coughing. The chest X-ray was obtained during this current 2-day flare in the setting of the worsening cough and fever and is felt to reflect the acute illness rather than a remote prior episode, but reassessment is planned if recurrent febrile episodes continue. He also reports mild nausea and intermittent crampy abdominal pain with loose stools twice yesterday. This morning he noticed red, watery eyes with gritty foreign-body sensation and increased discharge. No chest pain, no wheezing history, no syncope, no severe dehydration symptoms, no persistent vomiting, no blood in stool, no dysuria or hematuria, no neck stiffness, and no known exposure to tuberculosis.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite; tolerating small amounts of soft foods and fluids due to throat pain.
current_general_status.sleep: Poor for 2 nights due to fever and coughing.
current_general_status.excretion: Urine output slightly decreased but present; stools loose twice yesterday, no blood.
current_general_status.weight_change: Mild unintentional weight loss of about 1 kg over the past month during recurrent illnesses.

Occasional viral upper respiratory infections

persona: He is a 1-year-old boy in daycare whose caregivers keep a steady, organized home routine and lean on their faith when things feel uncertain. They speak in a direct, factual way, using simple words and answering questions clearly, but they also look for reassurance and confidence about what to do next. Despite the interrupted sleep and reduced feeding from his stuffy nose, they remain calm and practical, tracking wet diapers and fevers and noting when his face seems tender to touch. In quieter moments, a caregiver unwinds with small crafts while also caring for an older child in the household.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: After 6 days of cold symptoms, worsening thick yellow nasal discharge and nasal blockage with new facial discomfort for the past 2 days.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Started with acute rhinitis-like symptoms about 6 days ago (runny nose, sneezing, mild cough). Over the last 2 days, nasal congestion became more persistent with thicker yellow nasal discharge, worse at night, with noisy breathing and decreased interest in feeds. Caregiver notes foul-smelling nasal discharge at times and frequent postnasal drip with gagging. He has been more irritable and intermittently febrile (caregiver reports tactile fever), and seems uncomfortable when the cheeks/nasal bridge are touched. Symptoms have not improved with typical home cold measures. No wheezing, no stridor, no cyanosis, no persistent vomiting, no diarrhea, no rash, no neck stiffness, and no lethargy or seizure activity reported. No periorbital swelling or eye movement pain reported. Given age and lack of orbital/intracranial red flags, CT imaging is deferred at this time; imaging would be obtained only if complications are suspected (e.g., periorbital swelling, EOM pain, visual changes, severe headache, neurologic signs), if symptoms become severe/refractory, or if there is no clinical improvement with appropriate therapy.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced oral intake compared with baseline due to nasal blockage; drinking small, frequent amounts.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep with frequent waking and mouth breathing.
current_general_status.excretion: Urine output slightly decreased but still producing wet diapers; stools unchanged.
current_general_status.weight_change: No known weight loss; caregiver has not observed significant change.

Known immunodeficiency under evaluation with pediatric immunology (history of frequent infections)
Congenital ciliary dysfunction with reduced mucociliary clearance (reported by caregiver, prior specialist visit)

persona: The patient is a 64-year-old retired judge who used to handle public-works and land-use disputes tied to infrastructure and agriculture, and still thinks in terms of practical steps and what will work. The patient speaks in a direct, factual way, using plain words to describe what can and cannot be done, and wants test results back quickly to make decisions without delay. Financially, the patient is stretched since the recent ICU stay, and faith helps steady the patient even while feeling worried, frustrated, and tired. The patient prefers video visits, is unusually handy with gadgets, and wears a mask in crowded places.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent productive cough with intermittent shortness of breath worsening over the past 3 weeks after a prolonged ICU stay with mechanical ventilation.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last several months the patient reports recurring episodes of cough producing thick white to yellow sputum that partially improve then relapse. Over the past 3 weeks, symptoms have intensified with increased sputum volume, exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and occasional low-grade chills; the patient noted two small streaks of blood in sputum over the past week. The patient also reports sore mouth and a cottony taste with visible white patches in the mouth that recur, and generalized weakness with poor appetite. No pleuritic chest pain, no leg swelling or calf pain, no acute sharp onset of breathlessness, no known aspiration event, and no recent travel or sick contacts; denies severe wheezing or episodic nasal itching/sneezing as primary symptoms. Candida growth in sputum is recognized as potentially representing airway colonization (particularly in chronically ill/immunosuppressed patients) and is not treated as sole proof of pulmonary infection; the working diagnosis of invasive pulmonary candidiasis is supported primarily by candidemia plus histopathology demonstrating yeast/pseudohyphae with invasion in lung tissue. Major alternative causes of diffuse/miliary-like nodules and ground-glass opacities (including tuberculosis, endemic fungal infections, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia) were considered and are felt less likely in the current clinical context based on available testing and lack of supporting clinical features, though they remain in the differential if the course is atypical or response to therapy is inadequate.

current_general_status.diet: Poor appetite; currently eating small portions, limited protein intake due to fatigue and cost constraints.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep due to nighttime cough and anxiety about upcoming test results.
current_general_status.excretion: Urination more frequent at night; bowel movements every 2-3 days, sometimes constipated since hospitalization.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss over the last 2 months with noticeable muscle wasting.

medical_history.blood_transfusion: Received blood transfusion during recent ICU hospitalization for anemia; no known transfusion reaction.

persona: She is a 62-year-old homeowner who works as a data science expert and puts family first, currently helping care for an adolescent sibling. In conversation she comes in debate-ready, referencing things she has read online and confidently stating what she thinks is happening, pushing for specific tests or medications and getting defensive when questioned. Despite her assertive "Internet doctor" posture, she often struggles to communicate clearly and has low health literacy, so she can miss key distinctions while focusing on a single explanation. She reads food labels closely and is ingredient-conscious, but worries about mental health stigma and confidentiality at work and tends to switch providers if she feels dismissed.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive shortness of breath with recurrent productive cough and ankle swelling worsening over the past 8 months, with a flare of wheezing and fever after a cold 10 days ago.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the past 1-2 years she has had chronic cough with daily sputum and exertional dyspnea, initially only with stairs but now with routine walking and dressing; over the last 8 months she also noticed palpitations and fatigue with reduced exercise tolerance and intermittent bluish lips in cold weather. In the past 3-4 months she developed afternoon-dependent swelling of the feet/ankles that improves by morning and increasing abdominal bloating after meals. Ten days ago she developed upper-respiratory symptoms followed by increased sputum volume and purulence, wheezing, chest tightness, and low-grade fever, with marked worsening of breathlessness and poor sleep. She reports occasional lightheadedness with exertion but no true syncope. Denies pleuritic sharp chest pain, hemoptysis, unilateral calf pain, sudden onset symptoms, high fever with rigors, or focal neurologic deficits. No orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea reported, and no known history of left-sided heart failure or congenital heart disease.

current_general_status.diet: Reduced appetite during the past 10 days; otherwise mixed diet with frequent processed foods; trying to follow diabetic diet advice.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented due to nighttime cough and breathlessness; naps in daytime.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements every 1-2 days; increased nighttime urination; no dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Gradual weight gain over several years with recent short-term fluctuation due to fluid retention; clothes and shoes feel tighter by afternoon.

Pulmonary hypertension attributed to chronic respiratory disease/pulmonary vascular changes

demographics.name: Wayne Lopez

persona: He is a 41-year-old man in supportive housing, working a low-wage job while also doing occasional cultural commentary when he has the energy, and he is in a rebuilding phase after a divorce. He speaks in a direct, factual way and prefers formal, deeper conversations so he can understand what is happening and make clear decisions, but he is more emotionally shaken than he lets on. He cares a lot about inclusivity and often connects his interest in sport, gender, and violence to everyday life, and he avoids ongoing subscriptions to keep costs predictable. On better days he helps with women's football, focusing on developing midfield talent, though pain and fatigue have been limiting him lately.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Recurrent low back and rib pain with worsening fatigue over the past 4 months, more noticeable after activity.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last several months he has developed intermittent deep, aching pain in the lower back and bilateral ribs that initially followed heavy lifting and long workdays but has become more frequent and persistent, sometimes waking him at night. He reports increasing fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and occasional lightheadedness on standing. Over the past 3 weeks he has noticed increased thirst and urination and two brief episodes of mild confusion described as feeling "foggy" at work. He also notes intermittent tingling/numbness in the toes and fingertips and has had two upper respiratory infections this season that seemed to linger longer than usual. He reports occasional morning eyelid puffiness and mild ankle swelling late in the day. Denies fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, melena/hematochezia, focal weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel or bladder incontinence, recent major trauma, or known kidney stones.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite; prefers soft foods lately due to fatigue; drinks more water than usual because of thirst.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented sleep due to back pain; wakes 1-2 times nightly to urinate.
current_general_status.excretion: Polyuria; no dysuria; bowel movements regular without blood; occasional constipation when pain is worse.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss of about 4 kg over 4 months.

medical_history.vaccinations: Childhood immunizations up to date; receives influenza vaccine most years; unsure about pneumococcal vaccination.

demographics.name: Sara Turner

persona: He is a 38-year-old single man who has spent years building a public-facing life through fashion blogging and occasional freelance writing, shaped by time living in Manila and frequent travel within Asia. He comes across stoic and minimizing, insisting he is “fine” and that it is “nothing,” and he admits he only booked care after family pressure, though he quietly worries about how visible changes affect his confidence. He trusts scientific evidence, prefers clear written instructions he can revisit, tracks routines on a Garmin, and struggles with short-term planning, price sensitivity, and clinic scheduling amid home power outages.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Noticed recurrent round patches of hair loss and worsening fingernail roughness/pitting over the past 8 months, with a flare beginning about 3 weeks after a viral upper-respiratory illness.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 8 months he has had episodic, well-defined circular patches of scalp hair loss that enlarge over weeks and partially regrow, then recur at new sites. He reports short broken hairs at patch borders and increased hair shedding when showering. Over the same period he developed progressive fingernail changes including multiple small pits, longitudinal ridging, dullness and rough “sandpaper” texture, and increased brittleness with occasional lifting at the free edge; toenails are less affected. He endorses intermittent scalp tingling/itch without significant pain. No fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Denies new tight hairstyles/traction, harsh chemical hair treatments, or compulsive hair pulling. Denies scarring, pustules, thick scale, or drainage on the scalp. Denies diffuse hair thinning pattern, new facial rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, swollen joints, or new numbness/weakness. No recent exposure to new occupational chemicals; no nail trauma history to explain the pattern.

persona: He is a 25-year-old married man who supports himself with low-to-middle income work while pursuing sculpture on the side, and he leans on faith-based routines when life feels uncertain. Adopted and strongly attached to past experiences, he tends to compare every new symptom to earlier eye flares and insists they prove the same cause. He arrives with a phone full of search results, confidently naming his own diagnosis and pushing for specific drops or scans, getting prickly when challenged. He tries to stay active with walking and boot-camp workouts, usually commuting by motorcycle.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Intermittent blurry vision with occasional monocular double vision in the right eye for about 6 months, worsening over the past 2 weeks after a minor badminton-related eye impact.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last 6 months he has noticed fluctuating right-eye visual clarity, worse with reading and bright indoor lighting, along with intermittent monocular diplopia and halos/glare. Symptoms were initially brief and self-resolving, but over the past 2 weeks episodes have become more frequent and longer-lasting, with increased eye strain and occasional mild aching pain and redness. He reports periodic sensitivity to light and a sensation of pressure during flares, especially after exertion or when bending forward. He reports recurrent episodes of "intraocular inflammation" in the same eye over the past year treated intermittently with prescribed anti-inflammatory eye drops, but he is unsure whether a formal uveitis diagnosis was made and follow-up was incomplete. Currently, symptoms suggest intermittent irritation/redness without clear documentation of active anterior chamber or vitreous inflammation (cells/flare) at this visit; inflammatory activity will be specifically assessed on slit-lamp and dilated exam. Negative symptoms: no purulent discharge, no fever, no recent upper respiratory infection, no foreign-body sensation, no new generalized headache, no focal neurologic deficits, and no curtain-like vision loss; he denies sudden flashes and new shower of floaters.

current_general_status.diet: Generally balanced meals; prefers cooking at home and keeps indoor plants/terrariums, but meal timing is irregular due to caregiving responsibilities.
current_general_status.sleep: Light and fragmented sleep for several months, often interrupted by caregiving duties; feels unrefreshed.
current_general_status.excretion: No change reported; regular bowel and urinary habits.
current_general_status.weight_change: No significant weight change noted.

previous_diseases: Recurrent patient-reported episodes of right-eye intraocular inflammation/possible uveitis over the past year (intermittently treated; formal diagnosis uncertain)
Ocular trauma episodes related to sports (badminton shuttle/racket contact), most recently 2 weeks ago
No known asthma/COPD, no known bradycardia/heart block per patient report (timolol screening)
medical_history.long_term_medication: No daily systemic medications. Uses intermittent ophthalmic anti-inflammatory drops during flares (names not recalled); occasional over-the-counter ibuprofen for discomfort. No current beta-blocker use; baseline pulse to be checked prior to starting timolol and patient screened for asthma/COPD and conduction disease.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: No prior surgeries. History of minor blunt eye injuries during badminton coaching; no known penetrating eye injury.
medical_history.vaccinations: Childhood vaccinations up to date by history; influenza vaccine some years; unsure about latest booster timing.

persona: He is a 70-year-old man who has spent much of his life as a hands-on troubleshooter, taking pride in fixing problems quickly and getting frustrated when details are missing or unclear. Money has been tight, so he tries to stay wellness-focused in simple, practical ways, preferring “no-frills” choices and being careful about safety, while also valuing charity and showing respect for his ancestors. He speaks in a direct, factual way and answers questions plainly, but he can struggle to put feelings into words and may need a moment to organize what to say; he appreciates culturally sensitive care and clear step-by-step instructions, especially since he sometimes mixes up medication names and doses.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Progressive fatigue with reduced exercise tolerance and intermittent low-grade fevers for about 4 months, with worsening easy bruising over the past 3 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: Over the last several months he reports gradually increasing tiredness, decreased stamina on walks, and occasional lightheadedness on standing. He has had intermittent afternoon low-grade fevers and night sweats that improve after waking, along with reduced appetite and an unintentional weight loss. Over the past few weeks he noticed more frequent spontaneous bruising on the arms and legs and mild gum bleeding when brushing; he also reports a sense of fullness in the left upper abdomen and early satiety. He denies acute chest pain, focal neurologic deficits, severe headache, black stools, vomiting blood, painful urination, or large-volume visible blood in urine. No persistent productive cough, no pleuritic chest pain, and no recent travel or known sick contacts; he reports being diligent about hand hygiene. No new bone fractures; he does describe intermittent deep aches in the lower back and long bones that are not clearly related to activity.

current_general_status.diet: Decreased appetite with earlier satiety; eating smaller meals than usual.
current_general_status.sleep: Fragmented by night sweats and worry related to a hospitalized family member.
current_general_status.excretion: Bowel movements regular; no diarrhea or constipation. Urination normal without dysuria.
current_general_status.weight_change: Unintentional weight loss over several months.

previous_diseases: History of non-Hodgkin lymphoma treated years ago with radiotherapy and alkylating-agent chemotherapy (in remission by report)
Hypertension
Hyperlipidemia
medical_history.long_term_medication: Amlodipine and a statin; previously took low-dose aspirin intermittently but instructed to stop immediately and avoid aspirin/other antiplatelet or NSAID agents due to thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk. Patient reports occasionally confusing similar medication names and doses; medication reconciliation and clear labeling emphasized.
medical_history.surgery_or_trauma: Appendectomy in early adulthood; no major trauma reported.
medical_history.vaccinations: Up to date per age: annual influenza, COVID-19 boosters, and pneumococcal vaccination; unsure of exact dates.

family_history.similar_illness: Brother with a history of a blood disorder requiring hematology follow-up in later life (patient unsure of the exact diagnosis).
family_history.hereditary_diseases: Congenital genetic disorder in the family: patient reports he was diagnosed in childhood with a chromosomal condition associated with lifelong learning difficulties; he has required assisted living supports at times (details unclear in the chart).

persona: She is a 28-year-old single community planner on a tight budget who tries to stay active and used to enjoy para powerlifting, but lately she has had to scale back and plans her day around what her body will tolerate. She lives by her faith and leans on prayer and a warm bath or shower when she feels overwhelmed, though she can be visibly distressed and sometimes guarded until she feels listened to. When she talks with clinicians, she is direct and sticks to plain words, but she may unintentionally make the pain sound bigger when she is anxious; she likes tech tools and is learning to check her blood pressure at home, even if medical paperwork feels confusing.

present_illness_history.chief_complaint: Gradually worsening deep right groin and hip pain with intermittent flares for about 8 months, first noticed after increasing training and longer walks; pain has become more persistent over the past 6 weeks.
present_illness_history.symptoms_and_progression: She reports a dull, deep ache in the right groin/hip that initially occurred after activity and improved with rest, but has gradually progressed to daily pain with occasional night discomfort and brief episodes of catching and stiffness. Over the last 6 weeks, she has developed a noticeable limp after prolonged walking and difficulty with stairs, squatting, and getting up from a low chair; she has reduced para powerlifting sessions due to pain. She also describes intermittent color changes and numbness of fingers triggered by cold exposure and stress, along with episodic fatigue and joint aches in the hands and wrists during flares. She notes her feet sometimes feel unusually cold. She denies a recent fall or direct hip trauma. She denies fever, chills, acute hot swollen joint, purulent drainage, new skin infection, or recent dental procedures. She denies chest pain, new shortness of breath, calf swelling, focal weakness, bowel or bladder incontinence, saddle anesthesia, or severe low back pain radiating below the knee.

current_general_status.diet: Appetite slightly decreased during pain flares; otherwise regular meals.
current_general_status.sleep: Light and fragmented due to hip discomfort, worse at night over the past 6 weeks.
current_general_status.excretion: Normal urination and bowel movements; no dysuria or blood in stool.
current_general_status.weight_change: Mild weight gain over the past year attributed to steroid use; no recent rapid change.

Antiphospholipid antibody positivity with prior pregnancy morbidity consistent with antiphospholipid syndrome
Raynaud's phenomenon
medical_history.long_term_medication: Prednisone typically above 20 mg daily for the past 10 to 12 months with intermittent higher bursts during flares; hydroxychloroquine; mycophenolate mofetil; calcium and vitamin D supplementation; low-dose aspirin; warfarin anticoagulation managed by clinic (target range per prior records). Uses NSAIDs intermittently for pain when permitted.
